blocks:
Ayahs

طسٓمٓ (١)
Ta, Seen, Meem. (1)
تِلْكَ ءَايَـٰتُ ٱلْكِتَـٰبِ ٱلْمُبِينِ (٢)
These are the signs of the Manifest Book. (2)
نَتْلُوا۟ عَلَيْكَ مِن نَّبَإِ مُوسَىٰ وَفِرْعَوْنَ بِٱلْحَقِّ لِقَوْمٍ يُؤْمِنُونَ (٣)
We relate to you truly some of the account of Moses and Pharaoh for a people who have faith. (3)
إِنَّ فِرْعَوْنَ عَلَا فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ وَجَعَلَ أَهْلَهَا شِيَعًا يَسْتَضْعِفُ طَآئِفَةً مِّنْهُمْ يُذَبِّحُ أَبْنَآءَهُمْ وَيَسْتَحْىِۦ نِسَآءَهُمْ ۚ إِنَّهُۥ كَانَ مِنَ ٱلْمُفْسِدِينَ (٤)
Indeed Pharaoh tyrannized over the land, reducing its people to factions, abasing one group of them, slaughtering their sons, and sparing their women. Indeed, He was one of the agents of corruption. (4)
وَنُرِيدُ أَن نَّمُنَّ عَلَى ٱلَّذِينَ ٱسْتُضْعِفُوا۟ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ وَنَجْعَلَهُمْ أَئِمَّةً وَنَجْعَلَهُمُ ٱلْوَٰرِثِينَ (٥)
And We desired to show favour to those who were abased in the land, and to make them imams, and to make them the heirs, (5)
وَنُمَكِّنَ لَهُمْ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ وَنُرِىَ فِرْعَوْنَ وَهَـٰمَـٰنَ وَجُنُودَهُمَا مِنْهُم مَّا كَانُوا۟ يَحْذَرُونَ (٦)
and to establish them in the land, and to show Pharaoh and Haman and their hosts from them that of which they were apprehensive. (6)
وَأَوْحَيْنَآ إِلَىٰٓ أُمِّ مُوسَىٰٓ أَنْ أَرْضِعِيهِ ۖ فَإِذَا خِفْتِ عَلَيْهِ فَأَلْقِيهِ فِى ٱلْيَمِّ وَلَا تَخَافِى وَلَا تَحْزَنِىٓ ۖ إِنَّا رَآدُّوهُ إِلَيْكِ وَجَاعِلُوهُ مِنَ ٱلْمُرْسَلِينَ (٧)
We revealed to Moses’ mother, [saying], ‘Nurse him; then, when you fear for him, cast him into the river, and do not fear or grieve, for We will restore him to you and make him one of the apostles.’ (7)
فَٱلْتَقَطَهُۥٓ ءَالُ فِرْعَوْنَ لِيَكُونَ لَهُمْ عَدُوًّا وَحَزَنًا ۗ إِنَّ فِرْعَوْنَ وَهَـٰمَـٰنَ وَجُنُودَهُمَا كَانُوا۟ خَـٰطِـِٔينَ (٨)
Then Pharaoh’s kinsmen picked him up that he might be an enemy and a cause of grief to them. Indeed Pharaoh and Haman and their hosts were iniquitous. (8)
وَقَالَتِ ٱمْرَأَتُ فِرْعَوْنَ قُرَّتُ عَيْنٍ لِّى وَلَكَ ۖ لَا تَقْتُلُوهُ عَسَىٰٓ أَن يَنفَعَنَآ أَوْ نَتَّخِذَهُۥ وَلَدًا وَهُمْ لَا يَشْعُرُونَ (٩)
Pharaoh’s wife said [to him], ‘[This infant will be] a [source of] comfort to me and to you. Do not kill him. Maybe he will benefit us, or we will adopt him as a son.’ But they were not aware. (9)
وَأَصْبَحَ فُؤَادُ أُمِّ مُوسَىٰ فَـٰرِغًا ۖ إِن كَادَتْ لَتُبْدِى بِهِۦ لَوْلَآ أَن رَّبَطْنَا عَلَىٰ قَلْبِهَا لِتَكُونَ مِنَ ٱلْمُؤْمِنِينَ (١٠)
The heart of Moses’ mother became desolate, and indeed she was about to divulge it had We not fortified her heart so that she might have faith [in Allah’s promise]. (10)
وَقَالَتْ لِأُخْتِهِۦ قُصِّيهِ ۖ فَبَصُرَتْ بِهِۦ عَن جُنُبٍ وَهُمْ لَا يَشْعُرُونَ (١١)
She said to his sister, ‘Follow him.’ So she watched him from a distance, while they were not aware. (11)
۞ وَحَرَّمْنَا عَلَيْهِ ٱلْمَرَاضِعَ مِن قَبْلُ فَقَالَتْ هَلْ أَدُلُّكُمْ عَلَىٰٓ أَهْلِ بَيْتٍ يَكْفُلُونَهُۥ لَكُمْ وَهُمْ لَهُۥ نَـٰصِحُونَ (١٢)
Since before We had forbidden him to be suckled by any nurse. So she said, ‘Shall I show you a household that will take care of him for you and they will be his well-wishers?’ (12)
فَرَدَدْنَـٰهُ إِلَىٰٓ أُمِّهِۦ كَىْ تَقَرَّ عَيْنُهَا وَلَا تَحْزَنَ وَلِتَعْلَمَ أَنَّ وَعْدَ ٱللَّهِ حَقٌّ وَلَـٰكِنَّ أَكْثَرَهُمْ لَا يَعْلَمُونَ (١٣)
That is how We restored him to his mother so that she might be comforted and not grieve, and that she might know that Allah’s promise is true, but most of them do not know. (13)
وَلَمَّا بَلَغَ أَشُدَّهُۥ وَٱسْتَوَىٰٓ ءَاتَيْنَـٰهُ حُكْمًا وَعِلْمًا ۚ وَكَذَٰلِكَ نَجْزِى ٱلْمُحْسِنِينَ (١٤)
When he came of age and became fully matured, We gave him judgement and knowledge, and thus do We reward the virtuous. (14)

(Commentary)

The purpose of the Surah is to provide a beautiful promise to the believers (المؤمنين) — those who have faith in Allah and His message — who were in Mecca before the Migration (الهجرة), a small, oppressed group (شرذمة قليلون) deemed weak by the Pharaohs and tyrants of the Quraysh. It was a day of severity, hardship, and trial (فتنة). The promise is that Allah will favor them, make them leaders (أئمة), make them the inheritors (الوارثين), establish them firmly, and show the tyrants of their people exactly what they used to dread. The Exalted narrates to the believers the story of Moses and Pharaoh: how He created Moses at a time when Pharaoh was at the peak of his power, oppressing the Children of Israel (بني إسرائيل), slaughtering their sons and sparing their women. Yet, He raised him in the lap of his enemy until he reached maturity and full strength (بلغ أشده). Then, He saved him and brought him out from among them to Midian (مدين), later returning him to them as a messenger from Him with manifest authority (سلطان مبين), until He drowned Pharaoh and all his hosts, making the Children of Israel the inheritors and revealing the Torah (التوراة) to Moses as guidance (هدى) and insights (بصائر) for the believers.

The state of the believers follows this same course; it contains a promise for them of sovereignty, honor, and authority, and a promise to the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) of his return to a place of return (معاد).

The Surah then transitions from the story to explain that it is necessary, according to the wisdom (حكمة) of Allah, that He send down a Book from Him for the true call (الدعوة الحقة). It then mentions their criticism of the call of the Quran (القرآن) by their saying: "Why has he not been given the like of what was given to Moses?" and the response to it. It also mentions their excuse for not believing: "If we follow the guidance with you, we will be snatched away from our land," and the response to that, which includes the parable of the story of Korah (قارون) and his being swallowed by the earth (خسفه).

The Surah is Meccan (مكية), as evidenced by the context of its verses. The verses we have cited are a section of the story of Moses and Pharaoh, from the day Moses was born until he reached his full strength.

His saying, the Exalted: طسم تِلْكَ آياتُ الْكِتابِ الْمُبِينِ — "Ta Sin Mim. These are the signs of the Manifest Book." (28:1-2). The discussion regarding this has already preceded in similar instances.

His saying, the Exalted: نَتْلُوا عَلَيْكَ مِنْ نَبَإِ مُوسى وَفِرْعَوْنَ بِالْحَقِّ لِقَوْمٍ يُؤْمِنُونَ — "We relate to you some of the account of Moses and Pharaoh with the truth for a people who have faith." (28:3). The word "from" (مِنْ) denotes partitive (تبعيض), and "with the truth" (بِالْحَقِّ) is connected to His saying "We relate" (نَتْلُوا); meaning, We relate a recitation characterized by the truth, originating from Us and through Our revelation (وحي), without the devils (الشياطين) interfering in its delivery. It is also possible that it is connected to "account" (نبإ), meaning the account We relate to you is characterized by the truth, in which there is no doubt.

His saying: "for a people who have faith" (لِقَوْمٍ يُؤْمِنُونَ), the letter lam (ل) denotes causality (تعليل) and is connected to "We relate" (نَتْلُوا); meaning, We relate to you some of their account for the sake of a people who believe in Our signs.

The sum of the meaning is: We relate to you some of the account of Moses and Pharaoh, a recitation with the truth, so that those who believe in Our signs among those who follow you may reflect upon it. They are a humiliated and oppressed group in the hands of the Pharaohs of the Quraysh and the tyrants of their people. Thus, they may realize that the Allah in Whom they have believed, and in Whose Messenger they have trusted, and for Whose sake they have endured every harm, is the same Allah Who brought forth Moses (عليه السلام) to revive the truth and save the Children of Israel, granting them honor after their extreme humiliation—when their sons were being slaughtered and their women spared, while Pharaoh had exalted himself, sinking the claws of his coercion into them and surrounding them with his injustice.

He brought him forth in that dark atmosphere where there was no hope, raised him in the lap of his enemy, then took him out of Egypt, and then returned him to them with authority. Through him, He saved the Children of Israel and destroyed Pharaoh and his hosts by his hand, making them mere stories and dreams of the past.

Thus, He is Allah, majestic is His status (جل شأنه), narrating their story to His Prophet and hinting to him and to them by His saying: "for a people who have faith" (لِقَوْمٍ يُؤْمِنُونَ) that He will do for these people exactly what He did for those. He will favor these oppressed ones, make them leaders, and make them the inheritors, just as He did with the Children of Israel.

His saying, the Exalted: إِنَّ فِرْعَوْنَ عَلا فِي الْأَرْضِ وَجَعَلَ أَهْلَها شِيَعاً يَسْتَضْعِفُ طائِفَةً مِنْهُمْ — "Indeed Pharaoh exalted himself in the land and divided its people into factions, abasing a group of them..." (28:4). Exalting oneself in the land (العلو في الأرض) is a metaphor for tyranny and arrogance (الاستكبار). The word shiya‘an (شِيَعاً) is the plural of shi‘ah (شيعة), which means a faction (الفرقة). It is said in al-Majma‘: "Shiya‘ means factions; every faction is a shi‘ah, and they are so named because some of them follow (يتابع) others." End quote. The meaning of making the people of the land—likely the people of Egypt, with the definite article denoting specific reference—into factions was to sow discord among them so that they would not unite and revolt against him or overturn his affairs, as is the custom of kings in expanding their power and strengthening their authority. "Sparing the women" (استحياء النساء) means keeping them alive.

The sum of the meaning is: Pharaoh exalted himself in the land and dominated it by extending his authority over the people and enforcing his power among them. He divided its people into different factions and groups whose word would not unite on anything. By doing so, he weakened their collective strength to resist his power or prevent the execution of his will.

He was oppressing a group of them, namely the Children of Israel, the descendants of Jacob (عليه السلام). They had resided in Egypt since Joseph (عليه السلام) brought his father and brothers there; they settled and multiplied until they numbered in the thousands.

This Pharaoh, the king of Egypt contemporary to Moses (عليه السلام), treated them as enslaved captives. He increased their oppression to the point that he ordered the slaughter of their sons and the sparing of their women. In this was the annihilation of their men by killing the male children, which leads to the extinction of the people.

The reason for this was that he was of the corrupters (المفسدين) in the land. The general creation that brought humanity into existence did not differentiate in the extension of existence between one people and another. Then, He equipped everyone with that which guides them to a social life by enjoying the provisions of earthly life, and for everyone is that which corresponds to their value in society and their weight in cooperation.

This is the reform (الإصلاح) called for by creation and existence. Transgressing against that by liberating one group and enslaving another, or allowing one people to enjoy what they do not deserve while depriving others of what is suitable for them, is the corruption (الإفساد) that leads humanity to ruin and destruction.

The verse depicts the circumstances in which Moses (عليه السلام) was born, while the destructive causes surrounding the Children of Israel were aimed at his annihilation.

His saying, the Exalted: وَنُرِيدُ أَنْ نَمُنَّ عَلَى الَّذِينَ اسْتُضْعِفُوا فِي الْأَرْضِ — "And We desired to show favor to those who were abased in the land..." until His saying: ما كانُوا يَحْذَرُونَ — "...that which they used to dread." (28:5-6). The root meaning of al-mann (المن) — as inferred from the words of al-Raghib — is weight (الثقل). From this comes the naming of a weight used for measuring as a mann. A favor (المنة) is a "heavy" blessing (النعمة الثقيلة). To favor someone (من عليه) means to burden them with a blessing. He said: "This is expressed in two ways: one is by action, as in His saying: 'And We desired to show favor (نَمُنَّ) to those who were abased,' meaning We give them such blessings that they are 'burdened' by them; the second is by speech, as in His saying: 'They count it as a favor (يَمُنُّونَ) to you that they have embraced Islam,' and this is considered loathsome except when a blessing is met with ingratitude." End quote in summary.

"Establishing them in the land" (تمكينهم في الأرض) means giving them a place therein which they own and in which they are settled. It is narrated from al-Khalil that "place" (المكان) is a noun of place (muf‘al) derived from "being" (al-kawn), and due to its frequent use in speech, it was treated like the pattern fa‘al. Thus, it is said: tamakkana (he became established) and tamaskana (he became impoverished) similar to tamanzala (he took up residence). End quote.

His saying: "And We desired to show favor" (وَنُرِيدُ أَنْ نَمُنَ) etc., it is most appropriate for this to be a circumstantial clause (حال) referring to "a group" (طائِفَةً). The implied meaning is: he was abasing a group of them while We desired to show favor to those who were abased, etc. It has also been said that it is coordinated (معطوف) with His saying: "Indeed Pharaoh exalted himself in the land," but the first view is more apparent. In any case, "We desired" (نُرِيدُ) is used to recount a past state (حكاية الحال الماضية).

His saying: "and make them leaders" (وَنَجْعَلَهُمْ أَئِمَّةً) is an explanatory coordination (عطف تفسير) for His saying "to show favor" (نَمُنَ), as are the subsequent clauses.

The meaning is: the circumstances were those of Pharaoh’s exaltation, his dividing of the people, and his abasement of the Children of Israel—an abasement intended to destroy and annihilate them. Yet, the reality was that We desired to bestow upon these who were abased in every way a blessing that would "burden" them; namely, by making them leaders (أئمة) to be emulated, so they would be the followed after having been the followers. We would make them the inheritors (الوارثين) of the land after it was in the hands of others, and establish them in the land by giving them a place where they could settle and which they would own, after they had no place except where others chose to settle them. We would show Pharaoh, the king of Egypt, and Haman (هامان), his minister, and their hosts, from them—meaning from these abased ones—that which they used to dread: that they would prevail over them and take away their kingdom, their wealth, and their way of life, as they said regarding Moses and his brother when they were sent to them: "They desire to expel you from your land with their magic and do away with your exemplary tradition." (20:63).

The verse depicts what lay within these formidable circumstances that had decreed that not a single soul among the Children of Israel should live. Pharaoh’s tyrannical power surrounded them, and his terror filled every corner of their existence as he abased them to the point of annihilation. This was the outward appearance of the matter, but inwardly, the Divine Will (الإرادة الإلهية) was attached to saving them from them, transferring the weight of the blessing from the powerful and exalted folk of Pharaoh to the humiliated and abased Children of Israel, and changing the causes so that what was against the Children of Israel became for them, and what was for the folk of Pharaoh became against them. Allah judges, and there is none to repeal His judgment.

His saying, the Exalted: وَأَوْحَيْنا إِلى أُمِّ مُوسى أَنْ أَرْضِعِيهِ فَإِذا خِفْتِ عَلَيْهِ فَأَلْقِيهِ فِي الْيَمِ — "We revealed to the mother of Moses, [saying], ‘Suckle him, then, when you fear for him, cast him into the river...’" (28:7). Revelation (الإيحاء) is a hidden communication. In the Quran, it is used for Allah’s communication with some of His creation by way of inspiration (الإلهام) and casting into the heart, as in His saying: "because your Lord has inspired (أَوْحى) it" (99:5), and His saying: "And your Lord inspired (أَوْحى) the bee" (16:68), and His saying regarding the mother of Moses: "We revealed (أَوْحَيْنا) to the mother of Moses." It can also be in another manner, as with the Prophets and Messengers. It is used for other than Allah as well, as in His saying: "Indeed the devils inspire (لَيُوحُونَ) their allies" (6:121). Casting (الإلقاء) means throwing, and the river (اليم) refers to the sea or a large river.

In His saying: "We revealed to the mother of Moses," there is a concise omission (إيجاز بالحذف). The implied meaning is: the mother of Moses became pregnant with him—while the situation was one of such severity and intensity—and she gave birth to him, and We revealed to her, etc.

The meaning is: We said by a type of inspiration to the mother of Moses when she gave birth to him: Suckle him as long as you do not fear for him from Pharaoh’s side. But when you fear for him—that the folk of Pharaoh might discover him, take him, and kill him—then cast him into the river, which is the Nile according to the narrations. Do not fear his being killed, and do not grieve over his loss and separation from you. We shall surely return him to you after that and make him one of the messengers (المرسلين), so he will be a messenger to the folk of Pharaoh and the Children of Israel.

Thus, His saying: "We shall surely return him to you" (إِنَّا رَادُّوهُ إِلَيْكِ) is the justification for the prohibition in His saying: "and do not grieve" (وَلا تَحْزَنِي), as is also evidenced by His later saying: "So We restored him to his mother, that she might be comforted and not grieve" (28:13). The difference between fear (الخوف) and grief (الحزن) according to the context is that fear is regarding a disliked event that is possible to occur, while grief is regarding a disliked event that has definitely occurred.

His saying, the Exalted: فَالْتَقَطَهُ آلُ فِرْعَوْنَ لِيَكُونَ لَهُمْ عَدُوًّا وَحَزَناً إِنَّ فِرْعَوْنَ وَهامانَ وَجُنُودَهُما كانُوا خاطِئِينَ — "Then Pharaoh’s kinsfolk picked him up, that he should be an enemy and a cause of grief for them. Indeed Pharaoh and Haman and their hosts were in error." (28:8). Picking up (الالتقاط) is to find something and take it without seeking it; from this comes the term for a found object (luqatah). The letter lam (ل) in His saying "that he should be (لِيَكُونَ) for them an enemy and a cause of grief" is for the consequence (العاقبة) — as has been said. Grief (الحزن) and sorrow (الحَزَن) carry the same meaning, like illness (saqam and suqm). What is meant by "grief" here is the cause of grief; using the word "grief" itself is an exaggeration of his being the cause of their sorrow.

The word "those in error" (خاطِئِينَ) is the active participle of khati’a (he erred), just as mukhti’ is the active participle of akhta’a. The difference between the two, as mentioned by al-Raghib, is that al-khati’ (الخاطئ) is applied to one who intends an act that is not good and performs it. The Exalted said: "Indeed their killing was a great error (خِطْأً)" (17:31), and: "Indeed we were in error (خاطِئِينَ)" (12:91). Al-mukhti’ (المخطئ) is used for one who intends a good act but something else occurs from him; its verbal noun is al-khata’ (الخطأ). The Exalted said: "And whoever kills a believer by mistake (خَطَأً)" (4:92). The common meaning is deviating from the correct direction. End quote in summary.

Thus, His saying: "Indeed Pharaoh and Haman and their hosts were in error (خاطِئِينَ)" means they were in error in what they were doing to the sons of the Children of Israel and to Moses, out of caution against the destruction of their kingdom and the loss of their authority at their hands. They desired to change the decrees from their courses, so they killed a vast number of sons—which was of no use to them—and they left Moses when they picked him up and raised him in their own laps, even though he was the one by whose hand their state would collapse and their kingdom vanish.

The meaning is: the folk of Pharaoh found him and took him from the river, and the ultimate result of that was that he became an enemy to them and a cause of grief. Indeed, Pharaoh, Haman, and their hosts were in error in killing the sons and leaving Moses: they wanted to eliminate the one who would eliminate them, yet they ended up striving to protect him and working hard to raise him.

By this, it becomes clear that the interpretation of some—that their being "in error" means they were sinners (مذنبين) and thus Allah punished them by having their enemy raised by their own hands—is not sound.

His saying, the Exalted: وَقالَتِ امْرَأَتُ فِرْعَوْنَ قُرَّتُ عَيْنٍ لِي وَلَكَ لا تَقْتُلُوهُ عَسى أَنْ يَنْفَعَنا أَوْ نَتَّخِذَهُ وَلَداً وَهُمْ لا يَشْعُرُونَ — "Pharaoh’s wife said, ‘[He will be] a comfort to me and you. Do not kill him. Maybe he will benefit us, or we may adopt him as a son.’ And they were not aware." (28:9). This was an intercession from Pharaoh’s wife, who was with him when they brought Moses to him—as an infant picked up from the river. She addressed Pharaoh, saying: "[He is] a comfort to me and you" (قُرَّتُ عَيْنٍ لِي وَلَكَ), meaning he is a source of joy for us. "Do not kill him" (لا تَقْتُلُوهُ); she addressed them in the plural because the partners in the killing were many, including the cause, the direct actor, the commander, and the commanded.

She said what she said only because Allah, the Glorified, cast a love from Him into her heart, so she could not help but defend him from being killed and take him to herself. The Exalted said, in what He favored Moses (عليه السلام) with: "And I cast upon you love from Me, and that you might be reared under My eye." (20:39).

Her saying: "Maybe he will benefit us, or we may adopt him as a son" (عَسى أَنْ يَنْفَعَنا أَوْ نَتَّخِذَهُ وَلَداً), she said this because of the signs of majesty and the aura of Divine attraction (الجذبة الإلهية) she saw on his face. Her saying: "or we may adopt him as a son" indicates that they had no son.

His saying: "And they were not aware" (وَهُمْ لا يَشْعُرُونَ) is a circumstantial clause; meaning, she said what she said and interceded for him and averted the killing from him, while the people were not aware of what they were doing, what the reality of the situation was, or what its consequence would be.

His saying, the Exalted: وَأَصْبَحَ فُؤادُ أُمِّ مُوسى فارِغاً إِنْ كادَتْ لَتُبْدِي بِهِ لَوْ لا أَنْ رَبَطْنا عَلى قَلْبِها لِتَكُونَ مِنَ الْمُؤْمِنِينَ — "The heart of Moses’ mother became empty. She would have nearly disclosed it had We not braced her heart so that she might be one of the believers." (28:10). Disclosing (الإبداء) something is to manifest it. Bracing (الربط) something is to tie it firmly, which is a metaphor for making it steadfast (التثبيت).

What is meant by the heart of Moses’ mother becoming "empty" (فارِغاً) is its being empty of fear and grief. The necessary consequence of this was that no confused thoughts or conflicting illusions would occur to it, which would have agitated the heart and led to panic, causing her to disclose what she was supposed to hide regarding her son’s matter.

This is because the apparent context is that the reason for her not disclosing it was the emptiness of her heart, and the reason for the emptiness of her heart was the bracing of her heart, and the reason for the bracing was His saying to her in what He revealed to her: "Do not fear and do not grieve; We shall surely return him to you," etc.

His saying: "She would have nearly disclosed it had We not..." (إِنْ كادَتْ لَتُبْدِي بِهِ لَوْ لا) etc., the word in (إِنْ) is the lightened form of the heavy inna; meaning, she came close to manifesting the matter and revealing the secret had We not made her heart steadfast by bracing it. His saying: "so that she might be one of the believers" (لِتَكُونَ مِنَ الْمُؤْمِنِينَ) means those who trust in Allah’s protection, so she would be patient and not panic over him, and thus his matter would not be disclosed.

The whole section—meaning from His saying: "She would have nearly disclosed it" to the end of the verse—is in the position of explaining His saying: "The heart of Moses’ mother became empty." The sum of the verse’s meaning is: the heart of Moses’ mother became, due to Our revelation, free from the fear and grief that lead to disclosing the matter. Had We not made her heart steadfast through the revelation so that she would be trusting in Allah’s protection of him, she would have come close to disclosing his matter to them out of panic over him.

From what has preceded, the weakness of some interpretations of the verse’s phrases becomes clear, such as the saying of some regarding "The heart of Moses’ mother became empty" (فارِغاً) that it means "void of intellect" due to the fear and bewilderment that overcame her when she heard the infant had fallen into Pharaoh’s hands. Others said: "empty of the revelation" that had been revealed to her, due to forgetfulness. Others said: "empty of everything except the mention of Moses," meaning it became occupied solely with him. All of these are views that the context does not support.

Similar in weakness is their saying that the response to "had it not been" (lawla) is omitted, and the implied meaning is: "had We not braced her heart, she would have disclosed it and manifested it." The reason for their assuming this is the claim that lawla is similar to conditional instruments, so it must come at the beginning and its response cannot precede it. This has already been discussed in the commentary on His saying: "She certainly desired him, and he would have desired her had he not seen the proof of his Lord." (12:24).

His saying, the Exalted: وَقالَتْ لِأُخْتِهِ قُصِّيهِ فَبَصُرَتْ بِهِ عَنْ جُنُبٍ وَهُمْ لا يَشْعُرُونَ — "She said to his sister, ‘Follow him.’ So she watched him from a distance, while they were not aware." (28:11). It is said in al-Majma‘: "al-Qass (القص) is to follow the tracks; from this comes al-qasas (stories) in speech because the second part follows the first."

He also said: "The meaning of 'she watched him from a distance' (عَنْ جُنُبٍ) is that she saw him from a side, meaning from afar." End quote.

The meaning is: Moses’ mother said to his sister: "Follow the tracks of Moses until you see what his matter leads to." So she saw him from a distance after Pharaoh’s servants had taken him, while they were not aware that she was following him and watching him.

His saying, the Exalted: وَحَرَّمْنا عَلَيْهِ الْمَراضِعَ مِنْ قَبْلُ فَقالَتْ هَلْ أَدُلُّكُمْ عَلى أَهْلِ بَيْتٍ يَكْفُلُونَهُ لَكُمْ وَهُمْ لَهُ ناصِحُونَ — "We had forbidden him wet-nurses from before. So she said, ‘Shall I guide you to a household who will take care of him for you and they will be his well-wishers?’" (28:12). The "forbidding" (التحريم) in the verse is ontological (تكويني), not legislative (تشريعي); its meaning is that He made him such that he would not accept the breast of any wet-nurse and would refuse to suckle from them.

His saying: "from before" (مِنْ قَبْلُ) means before her presence there and her coming to them. "Wet-nurses" (المراضع) is the plural of murdiah (مرضعة), as has been said.

His saying: "So she said, ‘Shall I guide you to a household who will take care of him for you and they will be his well-wishers?’" is a consequence of what preceded, except that the context indicates an omission. It is as if it were said: We forbade him wet-nurses other than his mother before his sister came. Whenever they brought a wet-nurse to suckle him, he would not accept her breast. When his sister came and saw the situation, she said then to the folk of Pharaoh: "Shall I guide you to a household who will take care of him for your benefit and they will be well-wishers toward him?"

His saying, the Exalted: فَرَدَدْناهُ إِلى أُمِّهِ كَيْ تَقَرَّ عَيْنُها وَلا تَحْزَنَ وَلِتَعْلَمَ أَنَّ وَعْدَ اللهِ حَقٌّ وَلكِنَّ أَكْثَرَهُمْ لا يَعْلَمُونَ — "So We restored him to his mother, that she might be comforted and not grieve, and that she might know that Allah’s promise is true, but most of them do not know." (28:13). This is a consequence of what preceded, with the implication of what the context indicates. The sum is that she said: "Shall I guide you to such a household?" and they favored her with acceptance. She guided them to his mother, and they handed him over to her. Thus, We restored him to his mother through the arrangement of these causes.

His saying: "that she might be comforted and not grieve, and that she might know..." etc., is the reason for the restoration. What is meant by "knowing" (العلم) is certainty through witnessing (اليقين بالمشاهدة), for she already knew before that Allah’s promise is true and she was a believer. The restoration was intended so that she might be certain through witnessing that Allah’s promise is true.

What is meant by "Allah’s promise" is the Divine promise in general, as evidenced by His saying: "but most of them do not know" (وَلكِنَّ أَكْثَرَهُمْ لا يَعْلَمُونَ), meaning they do not have certainty in that, they doubt His promises, and their souls are not at peace with them. The sum is that she should realize, by witnessing the truth of this specific thing Allah promised her, that His promise in general is true.

It is sometimes said that what is meant by "Allah’s promise" is specifically the promise mentioned in the previous verse: "We shall surely return him to you and make him one of the messengers." However, His subsequent saying: "but most of them..." etc., does not suit this, as explained above.

His saying, the Exalted: وَلَمَّا بَلَغَ أَشُدَّهُ وَاسْتَوى آتَيْناهُ حُكْماً وَعِلْماً وَكَذلِكَ نَجْزِي الْمُحْسِنِينَ — "When he came of age and became mature, We gave him judgment and knowledge, and thus do We reward the virtuous." (28:14). Reaching full strength (بلوغ الأشد) is when a person reaches an age where their powers become strong, which is usually at eighteen. Maturity (الاستواء) is balance and stability; thus, maturity in life is a person’s stability in the affairs of their life. This varies among individuals but is mostly after reaching full strength. The discussion regarding the meaning of "judgment" (الحكم) and "knowledge" (العلم), their being given, and the meaning of "virtue" (الإحسان) has already preceded in various places in the Book.

(Narrative Discussion)

In al-Durr al-Manthur, Ibn Abi Shaybah, Ibn al-Mundhir, and Ibn Abi Hatim narrated from Ali ibn Abi Talib (may Allah be pleased with him) regarding His saying, the Exalted: "And We desired to show favor to those who were abased in the land," he said: "Joseph and his children."

I say: Perhaps what is meant is the Children of Israel; otherwise, the verse’s apparent meaning being contrary to this is not hidden.

In Ma‘ani al-Akhbar, with its chain of narration to Muhammad ibn Sinan from al-Mufaddal ibn ‘Umar, who said: I heard Abu ‘Abdillah (peace be upon him) say: "The Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) looked at Ali, al-Hasan, and al-Husayn (peace be upon them) and wept, saying: 'You are the oppressed ones after me.'" Al-Mufaddal said: I asked him: "What is the meaning of that?" He replied: "It means that you are the leaders (الأئمة) after me. Indeed, Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, says: 'And We desired to show favor to those who were abased in the land, and make them leaders and make them the inheritors.' This verse is applicable to us until the Day of Resurrection."

I say: The narrations from the Shi‘a paths regarding the verse being about the Imams of the Ahl al-Bayt (peace be upon them) are numerous. From this narration, it becomes clear that all of them are of the nature of "flow and application" (الجري والانطباق).

In Nahj al-Balaghah: "The world will surely incline toward us after its recalcitrance, just as a biting she-camel inclines toward her calf." Following that, he recited: "And We desired to show favor to those who were abased in the land, and make them leaders and make them the inheritors."

In the Tafsir of al-Qummi, regarding His saying, the Exalted: "We revealed to the mother of Moses..." until the end of the verse: My father narrated to me from al-Hasan ibn Mahbub from al-‘Ala’ ibn Razin from Muhammad ibn Muslim from Abu Ja‘far (peace be upon him), who said: "When his mother became pregnant with him, her pregnancy did not show until she gave birth to him. Pharaoh had assigned Coptic women to watch over the women of the Children of Israel. This was because it had reached him that the Children of Israel were saying: 'A man will be born among us called Moses ibn ‘Imran, by whose hand will be the destruction of Pharaoh and his companions.' Pharaoh said then: 'I will surely kill their male children so that what they desire does not happen.' He separated the men and women and imprisoned the men in jails.

When the mother of Moses gave birth to Moses, she looked at him, grieved for him, became distressed, and wept. She said: 'He will be slaughtered this moment.' Then Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, turned the heart of the woman assigned to her toward him. She said to the mother of Moses: 'What is the matter with you that your color has turned pale?' She replied: 'I fear my son will be slaughtered.' She said: 'Do not fear.' No one saw Moses except that they loved him—and that is the saying of Allah: 'And I cast upon you love from Me.'

The Coptic woman assigned to her loved him. Allah sent down the chest (التابوت) to the mother of Moses, and she was called: 'Place him in the chest and cast him into the river'—which is the sea—'and do not fear and do not grieve; We shall surely return him to you and make him one of the messengers.' So she placed him in the chest, closed it over him, and cast it into the Nile.

Pharaoh had a palace on the bank of the Nile for recreation. He looked from his palace—with Asiya (آسية), his wife—at a dark object in the Nile being raised by the waves and struck by the winds until it came to the gate of Pharaoh’s palace. Pharaoh ordered it to be taken, so the chest was taken and brought up to him. When he opened it, he found a boy in it and said: 'This is an Israelite.' But Allah cast a strong love into Pharaoh’s heart, and likewise into Asiya’s heart.

Pharaoh wanted to kill him, but Asiya said: 'Do not kill him. Maybe he will benefit us, or we may adopt him as a son.' And they were not aware that he was Moses."

In al-Majma‘, regarding His saying, the Exalted: "a comfort to me and you. Do not kill him," etc., from the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم): "By the One by Whom one swears, had Pharaoh acknowledged that he would be a comfort to him—as his wife acknowledged—Allah would have guided him through him just as He guided her. But he refused, due to the wretchedness that Allah had written for him."

In al-Ma‘ani, with its chain of narration to Muhammad ibn Nu‘man al-Ahwal from Abu ‘Abdillah (peace be upon him) regarding the saying of Allah, the Mighty and Majestic: "When he came of age and became mature," he said: "His coming of age (أشده) was eighteen years, and 'became mature' (استوى) means he grew a beard."

Ayahs

وَدَخَلَ ٱلْمَدِينَةَ عَلَىٰ حِينِ غَفْلَةٍ مِّنْ أَهْلِهَا فَوَجَدَ فِيهَا رَجُلَيْنِ يَقْتَتِلَانِ هَـٰذَا مِن شِيعَتِهِۦ وَهَـٰذَا مِنْ عَدُوِّهِۦ ۖ فَٱسْتَغَـٰثَهُ ٱلَّذِى مِن شِيعَتِهِۦ عَلَى ٱلَّذِى مِنْ عَدُوِّهِۦ فَوَكَزَهُۥ مُوسَىٰ فَقَضَىٰ عَلَيْهِ ۖ قَالَ هَـٰذَا مِنْ عَمَلِ ٱلشَّيْطَـٰنِ ۖ إِنَّهُۥ عَدُوٌّ مُّضِلٌّ مُّبِينٌ (١٥)
[One day] he entered the city at a time when its people were not likely to take notice. He found there two men fighting, this one from among his followers, and that one from his enemies. The one who was from his followers sought his help against him who was from his enemies. So Moses hit him with his fist, whereupon he expired. He said, ‘This is of Satan’s doing. Indeed he is clearly a misguiding enemy.’ (15)
قَالَ رَبِّ إِنِّى ظَلَمْتُ نَفْسِى فَٱغْفِرْ لِى فَغَفَرَ لَهُۥٓ ۚ إِنَّهُۥ هُوَ ٱلْغَفُورُ ٱلرَّحِيمُ (١٦)
He said, ‘My Lord! I have wronged myself. Forgive me!’ So He forgave him. Indeed, He is the All-forgiving, the All-merciful. (16)
قَالَ رَبِّ بِمَآ أَنْعَمْتَ عَلَىَّ فَلَنْ أَكُونَ ظَهِيرًا لِّلْمُجْرِمِينَ (١٧)
He said, ‘My Lord! As You have blessed me, I will never be a supporter of the guilty.’ (17)
فَأَصْبَحَ فِى ٱلْمَدِينَةِ خَآئِفًا يَتَرَقَّبُ فَإِذَا ٱلَّذِى ٱسْتَنصَرَهُۥ بِٱلْأَمْسِ يَسْتَصْرِخُهُۥ ۚ قَالَ لَهُۥ مُوسَىٰٓ إِنَّكَ لَغَوِىٌّ مُّبِينٌ (١٨)
He rose at dawn in the city, fearful and vigilant, when behold, the one who had sought his help the day before, shouted for his help [once again]. Moses said to him, ‘You are indeed clearly perverse!’ (18)
فَلَمَّآ أَنْ أَرَادَ أَن يَبْطِشَ بِٱلَّذِى هُوَ عَدُوٌّ لَّهُمَا قَالَ يَـٰمُوسَىٰٓ أَتُرِيدُ أَن تَقْتُلَنِى كَمَا قَتَلْتَ نَفْسًۢا بِٱلْأَمْسِ ۖ إِن تُرِيدُ إِلَّآ أَن تَكُونَ جَبَّارًا فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ وَمَا تُرِيدُ أَن تَكُونَ مِنَ ٱلْمُصْلِحِينَ (١٩)
But when he wanted to strike him who was an enemy of both of them, he said, ‘Moses, do you want to kill me, just like the one you killed yesterday? You only want to be a tyrant in the land, and you do not desire to be one who brings about reform.’ (19)
وَجَآءَ رَجُلٌ مِّنْ أَقْصَا ٱلْمَدِينَةِ يَسْعَىٰ قَالَ يَـٰمُوسَىٰٓ إِنَّ ٱلْمَلَأَ يَأْتَمِرُونَ بِكَ لِيَقْتُلُوكَ فَٱخْرُجْ إِنِّى لَكَ مِنَ ٱلنَّـٰصِحِينَ (٢٠)
There came a man from the city outskirts, hurrying. He said, ‘Moses! The elite are indeed conspiring to kill you. So leave. I am indeed your well-wisher.’ (20)
فَخَرَجَ مِنْهَا خَآئِفًا يَتَرَقَّبُ ۖ قَالَ رَبِّ نَجِّنِى مِنَ ٱلْقَوْمِ ٱلظَّـٰلِمِينَ (٢١)
So he left the city, fearful and vigilant. He said, ‘My Lord! Deliver me from the wrongdoing lot.’ (21)

(Commentary)

This is the second chapter of the story of Moses (peace be upon him), mentioning some of what occurred after he reached his full maturity (بلوغه أشده), which led to his departure from Egypt and his heading toward Midian.

His saying, the Exalted: وَدَخَلَ الْمَدِينَةَ عَلى حِينِ غَفْلَةٍ مِنْ أَهْلِها — "And he entered the city at a time when its people were unaware" (28:15), etc. There is no doubt that the city he entered at a time when its people were unaware was Egypt, and that he was living with Pharaoh. It is inferred from this that the royal palace where Pharaoh resided was outside the city, and that he went out from it and entered the city at a time when its people were unaware. What we have mentioned is supported by what follows in His saying: وَجاءَ رَجُلٌ مِنْ أَقْصَى الْمَدِينَةِ يَسْعى — "And there came a man from the city’s edge, running" (28:20), based on the apparent meaning (الاستظهار) that will be discussed.

The "time of unawareness" (حين الغفلة) for the people of the city is the time when people enter their homes, markets cease, and streets and alleys become empty of passersby, such as at midday or the middle of the night.

His saying: فَوَجَدَ فِيها رَجُلَيْنِ يَقْتَتِلانِ — "and he found there two men fighting" (28:15), meaning they were quarreling and striking one another. His saying: هذا مِنْ شِيعَتِهِ وَهذا مِنْ عَدُوِّهِ — "this one from his followers and that one from his enemies" (28:15), is a description of the state (حكاية حال) depicting the incident. Its meaning is that one of them was an Israelite from those who followed him in his religion—for the Children of Israel at that time traced their lineage in religion to their fathers Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob (peace be upon them), even if nothing remained of it for them but the name and they outwardly practiced the worship of Pharaoh—and the other was a Copt, an enemy to him, because the Copts were enemies of the Children of Israel. Another piece of evidence that this man was a Copt is His saying in another place, addressing his Lord: وَلَهُمْ عَلَيَّ ذَنْبٌ فَأَخافُ أَنْ يَقْتُلُونِ — "They have a charge against me, so I fear they will kill me" (26:14).

His saying: فَاسْتَغاثَهُ الَّذِي مِنْ شِيعَتِهِ عَلَى الَّذِي مِنْ عَدُوِّهِ — "The one who was from his followers sought his help against the one who was from his enemies" (28:15). Seeking help (الاستغاثة): is to seek aid (استنصار) from succor (الغوث), meaning assistance; that is, the Israelite asked Moses to help him against his Coptic enemy.

His saying: فَوَكَزَهُ مُوسى فَقَضى عَلَيْهِ — "So Moses struck him with his fist and finished him off" (28:15). The pronouns in "struck him" (فَوَكَزَهُ) and "off him" (عَلَيْهِ) refer to the one who was from his enemies. Striking with the fist (الوكز):—according to what al-Raghib and others mentioned—is to jab, push, or strike with the gathered palm.

Finishing off (القضاء): is the decree (الحكم), and "finishing him off" (القضاء عليه) is a metaphor (كناية) for completing his affair by his death. The meaning is: Moses pushed him or struck him with his fist, and he died. It was an accidental killing (قتل خطإ); were it not so, the appropriate expression in the discourse would have been "killing" (القتل).

His saying: قالَ هذا مِنْ عَمَلِ الشَّيْطانِ إِنَّهُ عَدُوٌّ مُضِلٌّ مُبِينٌ — "He said, ‘This is of Satan’s doing. Indeed he is a manifest, misleading enemy’" (28:15). The demonstrative "this" (هذا) refers to what occurred between them of fighting until it led to the death of the Copt. He attributed it in a certain sense to the work of Satan when he said: "This is of Satan’s doing" (هذا مِنْ عَمَلِ الشَّيْطانِ). The "of" (مِنْ) is for inception (ابتدائية), signifying the genus (الجنس) or origin (نشوئية). The meaning is: This enmity and fighting that occurred is of the genus of work attributed to Satan, or originates from the work of Satan, for it is he who cast enmity and hatred between them and incited the fighting until it led to the intervention of Moses and the killing of the Copt by his hand, thereby placing him in great danger. He knew that the incident would not remain hidden or concealed, and that the Copts would revolt against him, and their nobles and elite (الملأ), headed by Pharaoh, would take the severest revenge against him and anyone who caused it.

At that moment, he (peace be upon him) realized that he had erred in what he did of the strike that brought him to the brink of destruction. Falling into error is not attributed to Allah, the Glorified, because He only guides to truth and correctness (الصواب); thus, he judged that it was attributed to Satan.

His act, although it was not a sin (معصية) on his part because it occurred accidentally and because his defense of the Israelite was a defense against a disbelieving oppressor, yet Satan—just as he causes a human to fall into sin and disobedience through his whispering—likewise causes him to fall into any deviation from the correct course (مخالفة للصواب) by which he falls into hardship and difficulty. This is similar to how he caused Adam and his spouse to fall into what they fell into by eating from the forbidden tree, which led to their departure from Paradise.

Thus, his saying: "This is of Satan’s doing" (هذا مِنْ عَمَلِ الشَّيْطانِ) is a rejection (انزجار) on his part of the fighting that occurred, leading to the killing of the Copt and his falling into great danger, and a regret (ندم) for that. His saying: "Indeed he is a manifest, misleading enemy" (إِنَّهُ عَدُوٌّ مُضِلٌّ مُبِينٌ) is an indication from him that his act was of the misguidance (الضلال) attributed to Satan, even if it was not of the sin that entails guilt and punishment, but rather a pure error (خطأ محض) not attributed to Allah but to Satan, who is a manifest, misleading enemy. This was, on his part, a kind of poor management (سوء التدبير) and misdirected effort (ضلال السعي) leading him to a dire consequence. Therefore, when Pharaoh challenged him by saying: وَفَعَلْتَ فَعْلَتَكَ الَّتِي فَعَلْتَ وَأَنْتَ مِنَ الْكافِرِينَ — "And you committed that deed of yours which you committed, and you are one of the ungrateful" (26:19), he answered him by saying: فَعَلْتُها إِذاً وَأَنَا مِنَ الضَّالِّينَ — "I committed it then, while I was [yet] astray" (26:20).

His saying, the Exalted: قالَ رَبِّ إِنِّي ظَلَمْتُ نَفْسِي فَاغْفِرْ لِي فَغَفَرَ لَهُ إِنَّهُ هُوَ الْغَفُورُ الرَّحِيمُ — "He said, ‘My Lord! I have wronged myself. Forgive me!’ So He forgave him. Indeed He is the All-forgiving, the All-merciful" (28:16). This is an admission (اعتراف) before his Lord of wronging himself by bringing it to a place of danger and casting it into destruction. From this, it becomes clear that the "forgiveness" (المغفرة) requested in his saying: "Forgive me" (فَاغْفِرْ لِي) is the nullification of the consequence of his act, his rescue from distress, and his deliverance from the evil of Pharaoh and his elite, as is evident from His saying, the Exalted: وَقَتَلْتَ نَفْساً فَنَجَّيْناكَ مِنَ الْغَمِّ — "and you killed a man, and We delivered you from distress" (20:40).

This admission of wrongdoing and the request for forgiveness is similar to what occurred from Adam and his spouse, as recounted in His saying, the Exalted: قالا رَبَّنا ظَلَمْنا أَنْفُسَنا وَإِنْ لَمْ تَغْفِرْ لَنا وَتَرْحَمْنا لَنَكُونَنَّ مِنَ الْخاسِرِينَ — "They said, ‘Our Lord, we have wronged ourselves! If You do not forgive us and have mercy on us, we will surely be among the losers’" (7:23).

His saying, the Exalted: قالَ رَبِّ بِما أَنْعَمْتَ عَلَيَّ فَلَنْ أَكُونَ ظَهِيراً لِلْمُجْرِمِينَ — "He said, ‘My Lord! As You have blessed me, I will never be a supporter of the guilty’" (28:17). It is said that the "with" (بـ) in His saying: "As You have blessed me" (بِما أَنْعَمْتَ) is for causality (السببية), and the meaning is: "My Lord, because of what You have bestowed upon me, I owe it to You that I shall not be a helper to the guilty." Thus, it would be a covenant (عهد) from him to Allah, the Exalted. It is also said that the "with" (بـ) is for an oath (القسم) and the response is omitted, the meaning being: "I swear by what You have bestowed upon me, I shall surely repent or refrain, so I will never be a supporter of the guilty." It is also said that the oath is for seeking favor (استعطافي), which is the oath occurring in an initiation (الإنشاء), like saying "By Allah, visit me." The meaning would be: "I adjure You by Your favor upon me to be compassionate toward me and protect me, so I will never be a supporter of the guilty."

The first view is the most appropriate because the meaning of His saying: "As You have blessed me" (بِما أَنْعَمْتَ عَلَيَ)—according to what they mentioned—is either His favor, the Exalted, upon him when He protected him and saved him from Pharaoh's killing and returned him to his mother, or His favor upon him when He accepted his repentance for killing the Copt and forgave him, based on the premise that he knew of His forgiveness, the Exalted, through inspiration (إلهام), a vision (رؤيا), or the like. In any case, it would be an oath by other than Allah, the Exalted—meaning "I swear by Your protection of me" or "I swear by Your forgiveness of me"—and it is not customary in His speech, the Exalted, to recount an oath by someone else using something other than Him in this manner.

Regarding His saying: فَلَنْ أَكُونَ ظَهِيراً لِلْمُجْرِمِينَ — "I will never be a supporter of the guilty" (28:17), it is said that the "guilty" (المجرم) refers to one who causes another to fall into crime, or one whose assistance leads to a crime, like the Israelite who quarreled with the Copt, whose assistance caused Moses to fall into the crime of killing. Thus, there would be a metaphor in the attribution (مجاز في النسبة) in the word "guilty," in terms of naming the cause that leads to the crime as "guilty."

It is also said that the "guilty" (المجرمين) refers to Pharaoh and his people, and the meaning is: "I swear by Your favor upon me that I shall repent, so I will never be a helper to Pharaoh and his people by accompanying them, staying with them, and increasing their numbers as I used to do until this day."

This second view was rejected as being unsuitable for the context.

The truth is that His saying: "My Lord! As You have blessed me, I will never be a supporter of the guilty" (رَبِّ بِما أَنْعَمْتَ عَلَيَّ فَلَنْ أَكُونَ ظَهِيراً لِلْمُجْرِمِينَ) is a covenant from Moses (peace be upon him) not to help a criminal in his crime, as a way of thanking Allah for the blessing He bestowed upon him. The "blessing" (النعمة), having been mentioned unconditionally, refers to the divine guardianship (الولاية الإلهية), as evidenced by His saying, the Exalted: فَأُولئِكَ مَعَ الَّذِينَ أَنْعَمَ اللهُ عَلَيْهِمْ مِنَ النَّبِيِّينَ وَالصِّدِّيقِينَ وَالشُّهَداءِ وَالصَّالِحِينَ — "they will be with those whom Allah has blessed: the prophets, the truthful, the martyrs and the righteous" (4:69).

These are the people of the straight path (الصراط المستقيم), who are secure from misguidance and wrath, according to His saying, the Exalted: اهْدِنَا الصِّراطَ الْمُسْتَقِيمَ صِراطَ الَّذِينَ أَنْعَمْتَ عَلَيْهِمْ غَيْرِ الْمَغْضُوبِ عَلَيْهِمْ وَلَا الضَّالِّينَ — "Guide us on the straight path, the path of those whom You have blessed — such as have not incurred Your wrath, nor are astray" (1:6-7). The fact that refraining from helping the guilty follows from being blessed in this sense is clear and unconcealed.

From this, it becomes clear that the "guilty" (المجرمين) refers to the likes of Pharaoh and his people, not the likes of the Israelite whom he helped, for there was no crime in helping him, nor was the strike against the Copt a crime such that he (peace be upon him) would need to repent for it. How could it be so, when he (peace be upon him) is among the people of the straight path who do not go astray through disobedience? Allah, the Exalted, has explicitly stated that he was of the chosen ones (المخلصين) — those purified by Allah for His own service — whom Satan has no way to seduce, where He said: إِنَّهُ كانَ مُخْلَصاً وَكانَ رَسُولاً نَبِيًّا — "Indeed he was a chosen one, and he was an apostle and a prophet" (19:51).

Allah, the Exalted, also explicitly stated just before this that He gave him judgment (حكما) and knowledge (علما) and that he was among the virtuous (المحسنين) and the God-conscious (المتقين), whose state commands that he not be provoked by nationalistic zeal (عصبية قومية), or anger in an inappropriate situation, or by helping and supporting a criminal in his crime.

The word "He said" (قالَ) is repeated three times: "He said, ‘This is of Satan’s doing’" (قالَ هذا مِنْ عَمَلِ الشَّيْطانِ), "He said, ‘My Lord! I have wronged myself’" (قالَ رَبِّ إِنِّي ظَلَمْتُ نَفْسِي), and "He said, ‘My Lord! As You have blessed me’" (قالَ رَبِّ بِما أَنْعَمْتَ عَلَيَ). This is due to the difference in context (السياق) in the three sentences: the first sentence is a judgment (قضاء) and a ruling (حكم) from him, the second is a request for forgiveness (استغفار) and a supplication (دعاء), and the third is a covenant (عهد) and a commitment (التزام).

His saying, the Exalted: فَأَصْبَحَ فِي الْمَدِينَةِ خائِفاً يَتَرَقَّبُ فَإِذَا الَّذِي اسْتَنْصَرَهُ بِالْأَمْسِ يَسْتَصْرِخُهُ قالَ لَهُ مُوسى إِنَّكَ لَغَوِيٌّ مُبِينٌ — "So he became fearful and vigilant in the city, when lo, the one who had sought his help the day before, cried out to him [again] for help. Moses said to him, ‘You are indeed a manifest deviant’" (28:18). Restricting "he became" (فَأَصْبَحَ) with the phrase "in the city" (فِي الْمَدِينَةِ) is evidence that he remained in the city and did not return to Pharaoh's palace. Crying out for help (الاستصراخ): is seeking help (الاستغاثة) by raising the voice, from "screaming" (الصراخ) meaning shouting. Deviance (الغواية): is missing the correct course (إخطاء الصواب), the opposite of right-guidance (الرشد).

The meaning is: Moses became in the city—not having returned to Pharaoh's court—while he was fearful of Pharaoh, expecting trouble. Suddenly, the Israelite who had sought his help against the Copt the day before was crying out to him, raising his voice against another Copt. Moses said to the Israelite in rebuke and reprimand: "You are indeed a manifest deviant" (إِنَّكَ لَغَوِيٌّ مُبِينٌ), not following the path of right-guidance and correctness, because he was quarreling and fighting people where there was nothing in such quarreling and resistance but absolute evil.

His saying, the Exalted: فَلَمَّا أَنْ أَرادَ أَنْ يَبْطِشَ بِالَّذِي هو عَدُوٌّ لَهُما قالَ يا مُوسى أَتُرِيدُ أَنْ تَقْتُلَنِي كَما قَتَلْتَ نَفْساً بِالْأَمْسِ — "But when he sought to grasp the one who was an enemy to them both, he said, ‘O Moses! Do you intend to kill me as you killed a person yesterday?’" (28:19) until the end of the verse. Most commentators have mentioned that the pronoun in "he said" (قالَ) refers to the Israelite who was crying out to him for help. This is because he thought Moses intended to strike him when he heard him rebuking him earlier by saying: "You are indeed a manifest deviant" (إِنَّكَ لَغَوِيٌّ مُبِينٌ). He was terrified by what he saw of his intention to strike, so he said: "O Moses! Do you intend to kill me as you killed a person yesterday?" (يا مُوسى أَتُرِيدُ أَنْ تَقْتُلَنِي كَما قَتَلْتَ نَفْساً بِالْأَمْسِ), etc. At that point, the Copt realized that Moses was the one who had killed the Copt the day before, so he returned to Pharaoh and informed him of the news. Consequently, they conspired against Moses and resolved to kill him.

What they mentioned is appropriate, as the context (السياق) testifies to it; therefore, no weight is given to the claim that the speaker was the Copt rather than the Israelite. Furthermore, the meaning of the rest of the verse is clear. In His saying: "to grasp the one who was an enemy to them both" (أَنْ يَبْطِشَ بِالَّذِي هُوَ عَدُوٌّ لَهُما) there is a refutation of the current Torah, which mentions that these two fighters were both Israelites. It also supports that the one who said: "O Moses! Do you intend..." (يا مُوسى أَتُرِيدُ), etc., was the Israelite and not the Copt, because its tone is one of blame and complaint.

His saying, the Exalted: وَجاءَ رَجُلٌ مِنْ أَقْصَى الْمَدِينَةِ يَسْعى قالَ يا مُوسى إِنَّ الْمَلَأَ يَأْتَمِرُونَ بِكَ لِيَقْتُلُوكَ — "And there came a man from the city’s edge, running. He said, ‘O Moses! The elite are indeed conspiring against you to kill you’" (28:20), etc. Conspiring (الائتمار): is consultation (المشاورة). Sincere advice (النصيحة): is the opposite of betrayal (الخيانية).

It appears that the phrase "from the city’s edge" (مِنْ أَقْصَى الْمَدِينَةِ) is a qualifier for "came" (جاءَ). The context of the story suggests that the conspiracy was at Pharaoh's court and by his command, and that this man came from there. Pharaoh's palace was at the edge of the city and outside it. He informed Moses of their intention to kill him and advised him to leave the city.

This inference from the discourse supports what was previously mentioned—that Pharaoh's palace where he resided was outside the city. The meaning of the verse is clear.

His saying, the Exalted: فَخَرَجَ مِنْها خائِفاً يَتَرَقَّبُ قالَ رَبِّ نَجِّنِي مِنَ الْقَوْمِ الظَّالِمِينَ — "So he left it, fearful and vigilant. He said, ‘My Lord! Deliver me from the wrongdoing lot’" (28:21). In this, there is support for the fact that he did not view his killing of the Copt as a sinful crime (جرم) for himself.

(Narrative Discussion)

In the Tafsir of al-Qummi, it is said: "Moses remained with Pharaoh in the highest honor—until he reached manhood—and Pharaoh used to disapprove of what Moses (peace be upon him) spoke regarding monotheism (التوحيد)—until he intended [harm] against him. So Moses left him and entered the city—and lo, two men were fighting—one of them spoke according to the word of Moses and the other spoke according to the word of Pharaoh—so the one who was from his followers sought his help. Moses came—and struck Pharaoh's companion with his fist and finished him off, then hid in the city.

When it was the next day, another came—and grappled with that man who spoke according to the word of Moses—so he sought help from Moses. When his companion looked at Moses, he said to him: 'Do you intend to kill me as you killed a person yesterday?' So he let go of his companion and fled."

In al-Uyun, with its chain of narration to Ali ibn Muhammad ibn al-Jahm, he said: "I attended the assembly of al-Ma'mun while al-Rida (peace be upon him) was with him—al-Ma'mun said to him: 'O son of the Messenger of Allah—is it not your view that the prophets are infallible (معصومون)?' He said: 'Yes.' He said: 'Then inform me about the saying of Allah: فَوَكَزَهُ مُوسى فَقَضى عَلَيْهِ قالَ هذا مِنْ عَمَلِ الشَّيْطانِ (So Moses struck him with his fist and finished him off. He said, "This is of Satan’s doing").' Imam al-Rida (peace be upon him) said: 'Indeed Moses (peace be upon him) entered one of the cities of Pharaoh—at a time when its people were unaware, and that was between sunset and evening—and he found there two men fighting—this one from his followers and that one from his enemies—so he finished off the enemy according to the judgment of Allah, may His mention be exalted, and struck him with his fist and he died. He said: "This is of Satan’s doing," meaning the fighting that occurred between the two men—not what Moses (peace be upon him) did of killing him. "Indeed he"—meaning Satan—"is a manifest, misleading enemy."'

Al-Ma'mun said: 'Then what is the meaning of Moses' saying: رَبِّ إِنِّي ظَلَمْتُ نَفْسِي فَاغْفِرْ لِي (My Lord! I have wronged myself. Forgive me!)?' He said: 'He says: I placed myself in other than its proper place by entering this city—so forgive me, meaning hide me (استرني) from Your enemies so that they do not triumph over me and kill me—so He forgave him. Indeed He is the All-forgiving, the All-merciful. Moses said: "My Lord! By the strength You have bestowed upon me—such that I killed a man with a single strike—I will never be a supporter of the guilty—rather, I will strive against them with this strength until You are pleased."

So Moses (peace be upon him) became in the city fearful and vigilant—when lo, the one who had sought his help the day before cried out to him for help against another—Moses said to him: "You are indeed a manifest deviant—you fought a man yesterday and you fight today—I shall surely discipline you," and he intended to grasp him—but when he sought to grasp the one who was an enemy to them both, and he was from his followers—(قالَ : يا مُوسى أَتُرِيدُ أَنْ تَقْتُلَنِي كَما قَتَلْتَ نَفْساً بِالْأَمْسِ؟ إِنْ تُرِيدُ إِلَّا أَنْ تَكُونَ جَبَّاراً فِي الْأَرْضِ ـ وَما تُرِيدُ أَنْ تَكُونَ مِنَ الْمُصْلِحِينَ) (he said, "O Moses! Do you intend to kill me as you killed a person yesterday? You only desire to be a tyrant in the land, and you do not desire to be one of the reformers").' Al-Ma'mun said: 'May Allah reward you well on behalf of His prophets, O Abu al-Hasan.'"

Ayahs

وَلَمَّا تَوَجَّهَ تِلْقَآءَ مَدْيَنَ قَالَ عَسَىٰ رَبِّىٓ أَن يَهْدِيَنِى سَوَآءَ ٱلسَّبِيلِ (٢٢)
And when he turned his face toward Midian, he said, ‘Maybe my Lord will show me the right way.’ (22)
وَلَمَّا وَرَدَ مَآءَ مَدْيَنَ وَجَدَ عَلَيْهِ أُمَّةً مِّنَ ٱلنَّاسِ يَسْقُونَ وَوَجَدَ مِن دُونِهِمُ ٱمْرَأَتَيْنِ تَذُودَانِ ۖ قَالَ مَا خَطْبُكُمَا ۖ قَالَتَا لَا نَسْقِى حَتَّىٰ يُصْدِرَ ٱلرِّعَآءُ ۖ وَأَبُونَا شَيْخٌ كَبِيرٌ (٢٣)
When he arrived at the well of Midian, he found there a throng of people watering [their flocks], and he found, besides them, two women holding back [their flock]. He said, ‘What is your business?’ They said, ‘We do not water [our flock] until the shepherds have driven out [their flocks], and our father is an aged man.’ (23)
فَسَقَىٰ لَهُمَا ثُمَّ تَوَلَّىٰٓ إِلَى ٱلظِّلِّ فَقَالَ رَبِّ إِنِّى لِمَآ أَنزَلْتَ إِلَىَّ مِنْ خَيْرٍ فَقِيرٌ (٢٤)
So he watered [their flock] for them. Then he withdrew toward the shade and said, ‘My Lord! I am indeed in need of any good You may send down to me!’ (24)
فَجَآءَتْهُ إِحْدَىٰهُمَا تَمْشِى عَلَى ٱسْتِحْيَآءٍ قَالَتْ إِنَّ أَبِى يَدْعُوكَ لِيَجْزِيَكَ أَجْرَ مَا سَقَيْتَ لَنَا ۚ فَلَمَّا جَآءَهُۥ وَقَصَّ عَلَيْهِ ٱلْقَصَصَ قَالَ لَا تَخَفْ ۖ نَجَوْتَ مِنَ ٱلْقَوْمِ ٱلظَّـٰلِمِينَ (٢٥)
Then one of the two women approached him, walking bashfully. She said, ‘Indeed my father invites you to pay you the wages for watering [our flock] for us.’ So when he came to him and recounted the story to him, he said, ‘Do not be afraid. You have been delivered from the wrongdoing lot.’ (25)
قَالَتْ إِحْدَىٰهُمَا يَـٰٓأَبَتِ ٱسْتَـْٔجِرْهُ ۖ إِنَّ خَيْرَ مَنِ ٱسْتَـْٔجَرْتَ ٱلْقَوِىُّ ٱلْأَمِينُ (٢٦)
One of the two women said, ‘Father, hire him. Indeed the best you can hire is a powerful and trustworthy man.’ (26)
قَالَ إِنِّىٓ أُرِيدُ أَنْ أُنكِحَكَ إِحْدَى ٱبْنَتَىَّ هَـٰتَيْنِ عَلَىٰٓ أَن تَأْجُرَنِى ثَمَـٰنِىَ حِجَجٍ ۖ فَإِنْ أَتْمَمْتَ عَشْرًا فَمِنْ عِندِكَ ۖ وَمَآ أُرِيدُ أَنْ أَشُقَّ عَلَيْكَ ۚ سَتَجِدُنِىٓ إِن شَآءَ ٱللَّهُ مِنَ ٱلصَّـٰلِحِينَ (٢٧)
He said, ‘Indeed I desire to marry you to one of these two daughters of mine, on condition that you hire yourself to me for eight years. And if you complete ten, that will be up to you, and I do not want to be hard on you. God willing, you will find me to a righteous person.’ (27)
قَالَ ذَٰلِكَ بَيْنِى وَبَيْنَكَ ۖ أَيَّمَا ٱلْأَجَلَيْنِ قَضَيْتُ فَلَا عُدْوَٰنَ عَلَىَّ ۖ وَٱللَّهُ عَلَىٰ مَا نَقُولُ وَكِيلٌ (٢٨)
He said, ‘This will be [by consent] between you and me. Whichever of the two terms I complete, there shall be no imposition upon me, and Allah is witness over what we say.’ (28)

(Commentary)

This is the third chapter of his story (peace be upon him), mentioning his departure from Egypt to Madyan following his killing of the Copt out of fear of Pharaoh, and his marriage there to the daughter of an elderly man who is not named in the Quran. However, the narrations of the Imams of the People of the House (عليه السلام) and some narrations from the Sunnis mention that he was the Prophet Shu‘ayb, who was sent to Madyan.

His saying, the Exalted: وَلَمَّا تَوَجَّهَ تِلْقاءَ مَدْيَنَ قالَ عَسى رَبِّي أَنْ يَهْدِيَنِي سَواءَ السَّبِيلِ — "And when he turned his face toward Madyan, he said, ‘Maybe my Lord will guide me to the right way.’" (28:22). It is mentioned in al-Majma‘: تلقاء (toward) a thing means facing it; it is said: "He did that from his own accord (تلقاء نفسه)," meaning from the direction of his own inner prompting. He also said: سواء السبيل (the right way) means the middle of the road. End quote.

Madyan (مدين) — according to Marasid al-Ittila‘ — is the city of the people of Shu‘ayb. It lies opposite Tabuk on the Red Sea (بحر القلزم), with six stages of travel between them. It is larger than Tabuk and contains the well from which Musa drew water for the sheep of Shu‘ayb (peace be upon him). End quote. It is said that there was an eight-day journey between it and Egypt, and it was outside the dominion of Pharaoh, which is why he headed toward it.

The meaning is: When he turned his face, after leaving Egypt, toward Madyan, he said: "I hope my Lord will guide me to the middle of the road so that I do not go astray by deviating from it or leaving it for another."

The context — as you can see — indicates that he (peace be upon him) was intending to reach Madyan but did not know the road leading to it, so he hoped his Lord would guide him.

His saying, the Exalted: وَلَمَّا وَرَدَ ماءَ مَدْيَنَ وَجَدَ عَلَيْهِ أُمَّةً مِنَ النَّاسِ يَسْقُونَ — "And when he arrived at the watering-place of Madyan, he found there a group of people watering [their flocks]..." (28:23). الذود (keeping back) means restraining and preventing. What is meant by His saying: تَذُودانِ (keeping back) is that the two women were restraining their sheep from approaching the water or mixing with the sheep of the people. Similarly, what is meant by His saying: يَسْقُونَ (watering) is their watering of their sheep and livestock. الرعاء (shepherds) is the plural of الراعي (shepherd), who is the one who tends the sheep.

The meaning is: When Musa arrived at the water of Madyan, he found a group of people at the water watering their sheep, and he found near them, on their side, two women restraining their sheep and preventing them from approaching the watering place. Musa asked them, seeking clarification — since he found them keeping back the sheep without any man with their flock — "What is your business?" They said: "We do not water our sheep" — meaning that was their custom — "until the shepherds have finished and taken their sheep away, and our father is an elderly man" — unable to undertake the task of watering himself, which is why we have undertaken it.

His saying, the Exalted: فَسَقى لَهُما ثُمَّ تَوَلَّى إِلَى الظِّلِّ فَقالَ رَبِّ إِنِّي لِما أَنْزَلْتَ إِلَيَّ مِنْ خَيْرٍ فَقِيرٌ — "So he watered [their flock] for them. Then he withdrew into the shade and said, ‘My Lord! I am indeed in need of any good You may send down to me.’" (28:24). He (peace be upon him) understood from their words that their delay in watering was a form of modesty (تعفف) and veiling (تحجب) on their part, and an encroachment by the people upon them, so he hastened to the task and watered for them.

His saying: ثُمَّ تَوَلَّى إِلَى الظِّلِّ فَقالَ رَبِّ إِنِّي لِما أَنْزَلْتَ إِلَيَّ مِنْ خَيْرٍ فَقِيرٌ means he turned away to the shade to rest in it, as the heat was intense, and said what he said. Most commentators have interpreted his saying: رَبِّ إِنِّي لِما أَنْزَلْتَ etc., as a request for food to satisfy his hunger. Accordingly, it is better that the meaning of لِما أَنْزَلْتَ إِلَيَ (what You have sent down to me) refers to the physical strength with which he performed righteous deeds (الصالحات) that please Allah — such as defending the Israelite, fleeing from Pharaoh with the intention of reaching Madyan, and watering the sheep of Shu‘ayb. The preposition اللام in لِما أَنْزَلْتَ carries the meaning of "to" (إلى). Expressing his need for this strength, which Allah sent down to him from Himself by way of bestowal, is a metaphor for expressing his need for some food by which this bestowed, descended strength might be sustained.

It appears from this that he (peace be upon him) was intensely mindful (مراقبة) of his actions; he would not perform an act nor desire one, even if it were something necessitated by his human nature, except to seek the pleasure of his Lord and as a struggle for His sake. This is evident upon reflecting on the story: he is the one who said when he struck the Copt: رَبِّ بِما أَنْعَمْتَ عَلَيَّ فَلَنْ أَكُونَ ظَهِيراً لِلْمُجْرِمِينَ — "My Lord! As You have blessed me, I will never be a supporter of the guilty." (28:17). Then he said when he left Egypt fearful and vigilant: رَبِّ نَجِّنِي مِنَ الْقَوْمِ الظَّالِمِينَ — "My Lord! Deliver me from the wrongdoing lot." (28:21). Then he said when he began his journey: عَسى رَبِّي أَنْ يَهْدِيَنِي سَواءَ السَّبِيلِ — "Maybe my Lord will guide me to the right way." (28:22). Then he said when he had watered and turned to the shade: رَبِّ إِنِّي لِما أَنْزَلْتَ إِلَيَّ مِنْ خَيْرٍ فَقِيرٌ — "My Lord! I am indeed in need of any good You may send down to me." (28:24). Then he said when he hired himself to Shu‘ayb and contracted to marry his daughter: وَاللهُ عَلى ما نَقُولُ وَكِيلٌ — "and Allah is witness over what we say." (28:28).

What has been narrated from some that the اللام in لِما أَنْزَلْتَ is for causation (للتعليل), as well as the view of some that "good" (الخير) refers to the good of religion (خير الدين) — which is salvation from the wrongdoers — is far from what the context suggests.

His saying, the Exalted: فَجاءَتْهُ إِحْداهُما تَمْشِي عَلَى اسْتِحْياءٍ — "Then one of the two women approached him, walking bashfully..." (28:25) to the end of the verse. The pronoun in "one of them" refers to the two women. The indefiniteness of استحياء (bashfulness) is for magnification (للتفخيم), and the meaning of her walking "with bashfulness" is the manifestation of modesty (التعفف) in her gait. His saying: لِيَجْزِيَكَ أَجْرَ ما سَقَيْتَ لَنا — "that he may reward you for having watered [our flock] for us" — the ما is infinitive (مصدرية), meaning: to give you the reward for your watering for us. His saying: فَلَمَّا جاءَهُ وَقَصَّ عَلَيْهِ الْقَصَصَ قالَ لا تَخَفْ — "So when he came to him and recounted the story to him, he said, ‘Do not be afraid...’" (28:25) suggests that Shu‘ayb asked him about his condition, so he recounted his story to him, and Shu‘ayb comforted him by saying he had been saved from them, as they had no authority over Madyan.

At that point, Allah the Exalted completed the fulfillment of Musa's (peace be upon him) three supplications: he had asked Allah when leaving Egypt to save him from the wrongdoing people, and Shu‘ayb (peace be upon him) informed him of his salvation; he had hoped that He would guide him to the right way — which is in the sense of a prayer — and he arrived at Madyan; and he had asked Him for provision, so Shu‘ayb invited him to reward him for his watering, and Allah the Exalted added more, sufficing his provision for ten years and granting him a wife in whom he could find rest.

His saying, the Exalted: قالَتْ إِحْداهُما يا أَبَتِ اسْتَأْجِرْهُ إِنَّ خَيْرَ مَنِ اسْتَأْجَرْتَ الْقَوِيُّ الْأَمِينُ — "One of the two women said, ‘Father, hire him. Indeed the best you can hire is the powerful and the trustworthy.’" (28:26). The general use of "hiring" (الاستيجار) indicates that the intent was to employ him for all his needs that required someone to stand in his place, even if the specific requirement of the situation was tending the sheep.

His saying: إِنَّ خَيْرَ مَنِ اسْتَأْجَرْتَ etc., is in the position of providing the reason (التعليل) for her saying: "hire him." This is an instance of placing the cause in the position of the effect; the implied meaning is: "Hire him because he is powerful and trustworthy, and the best person you can hire is the one who is powerful and trustworthy."

In her judgment that he was "powerful and trustworthy," there is an indication that she observed from his manner of watering the sheep that which led her to conclude his strength, and likewise from the manifestation of his modesty in speaking to them and watering their sheep, and then in his accompanying her when she went to Shu‘ayb until he reached him, that which led her to conclude his trustworthiness.

From here it appears that the one who said: "Father, hire him" etc., is the same one who came to him and informed him of her father's invitation to him, as mentioned in the narrations of the Imams of the People of the House (عليه السلام) and held by a group of commentators.

His saying, the Exalted: قالَ إِنِّي أُرِيدُ أَنْ أُنْكِحَكَ إِحْدَى ابْنَتَيَّ هاتَيْنِ عَلى أَنْ تَأْجُرَنِي ثَمانِيَ حِجَجٍ — "He said, ‘I desire to marry you to one of these two daughters of mine, on condition that you hire yourself to me for eight years...’" (28:27). This was an offer from Shu‘ayb to Musa (peace be upon him) to hire himself for eight or ten years in exchange for marrying one of his two daughters. It was not a final contract (عقد قاطع), and the evidence for this is that the specific daughter to be married was not identified in his speech (peace be upon him).

So his saying: إِحْدَى ابْنَتَيَّ هاتَيْنِ (one of these two daughters of mine) is evidence of their presence at that time. His saying: عَلى أَنْ تَأْجُرَنِي ثَمانِيَ حِجَجٍ means: on the condition that you hire yourself to me — that is, you be a hired worker for me — for eight years. الحجج (pilgrimages) is the plural of حجة (pilgrimage), and what is meant by it is "years," with the consideration that every year contains a pilgrimage to the Sacred House. From this, it appears that the pilgrimage to the House — which is from the law of Ibrahim (peace be upon him) — was practiced among them.

His saying: فَإِنْ أَتْمَمْتَ عَشْراً فَمِنْ عِنْدِكَ — "but if you complete ten, that will be of your own accord" — means: if you complete it to ten years, that is from you and by your choice, without being obligated by me.

His saying: وَما أُرِيدُ أَنْ أَشُقَّ عَلَيْكَ — "I do not want to be hard on you" — is an announcement regarding the nature of the service he wants from him, that it is a task not described as difficult, and that he is a righteous master.

His saying: سَتَجِدُنِي إِنْ شاءَ اللهُ مِنَ الصَّالِحِينَ — "You will find me, Allah willing, [to be] one of the righteous" — means: I am among the righteous, and you will find me to be among them, Allah willing. Thus, the exception (الاستثناء) — "Allah willing" — is connected to Musa's finding him to be among them, not to his being among them in himself.

His saying, the Exalted: قالَ ذلِكا بَيْنِي وَبَيْنَكَ أَيَّمَا الْأَجَلَيْنِ قَضَيْتُ فَلا عُدْوانَ عَلَيَّ وَاللهُ عَلى ما نَقُولُ وَكِيلٌ — "He said, ‘This will be [the agreement] between me and you: whichever of the two terms I complete, there shall be no injustice to me, and Allah is witness over what we say.’" (28:28). The pronoun refers to Musa (peace be upon him).

His saying: ذلِكَ بَيْنِي وَبَيْنَكَ means: that which you have mentioned and established of the conditions and covenant and offered to me is fixed between us; neither I nor you have the right to oppose what we have conditioned. His saying: أَيَّمَا الْأَجَلَيْنِ قَضَيْتُ فَلا عُدْوانَ عَلَيَ is a clarification of the choice between the two terms set in the speech of Shu‘ayb (peace be upon him), which was: "eight years, but if you complete ten, that will be of your own accord." That is: I have the right to choose whichever of the two terms I wish; if I choose eight years, you have no right to wrong me or obligate me to more, and if I choose the increase and serve you for ten, you have no right to wrong me by preventing the increase.

His saying: وَاللهُ عَلى ما نَقُولُ وَكِيلٌ is an entrustment (توكيل) to Him, the Exalted, regarding what they conditioned, which includes making Him, the Exalted, a witness over what they say and referring the judgment and decree between them to Him if they should disagree. Therefore, he chose entrustment (التوكيل) over witnessing (الإشهاد) because both witnessing and judgment belong to Him, the Exalted. This is like the saying of Ya‘qub (peace be upon him) when he took the pledge from his sons to return his son to him, as Allah recounts: فَلَمَّا آتَوْهُ مَوْثِقَهُمْ قالَ اللهُ عَلى ما نَقُولُ وَكِيلٌ — "When they had given him their pledge, he said, ‘Allah is witness over what we say.’" (12:66).

(Narrative Discourse)

In the book Kamal al-Din, with its chain of transmission to Sudayr al-Sayrafi from Abu ‘Abdillah (peace be upon him) in a long tradition: وَجاءَ رَجُلٌ مِنْ أَقْصَى الْمَدِينَةِ يَسْعى ـ قالَ يا مُوسى إِنَّ الْمَلَأَ يَأْتَمِرُونَ بِكَ لِيَقْتُلُوكَ ـ فَاخْرُجْ إِنِّي لَكَ مِنَ النَّاصِحِينَ فَخَرَجَ مِنْها خائِفاً يَتَرَقَّبُ — "There came a man from the city’s outskirts, hurrying. He said, ‘O Moses! The elite are indeed conspiring to kill you. So leave. I am indeed a well-wisher to you.’ So he left it, fearful and vigilant" — from Egypt without a mount, a beast, or a servant, one land lowering him and another raising him until he reached the land of Madyan.

He reached the base of a tree and stopped, and there beneath it was a well — وَإِذا عِنْدَها أُمَّةٌ مِنَ النَّاسِ يَسْقُونَ — "and there he found a group of people watering" — and there were two weak young women with a small flock of theirs. He said: "What is your business?" They said: "Our father is an elderly man, and we are two weak young women unable to crowd the men; when the people have watered, we water." He took pity on them, took their bucket, and said to them: "Bring your sheep forward." He watered for them, and they returned early before the people.

Then Musa withdrew to the tree and sat beneath it — and said: رَبِّ إِنِّي لِما أَنْزَلْتَ إِلَيَّ مِنْ خَيْرٍ فَقِيرٌ — "My Lord! I am indeed in need of any good You may send down to me." It is narrated that he said that while he was in need of even half a date. When they returned to their father, he said: "What has made you so quick at this hour?" They said: "We found a righteous man who took pity on us and watered for us." He said to one of them: "Go and call him for me." So one of them came to him, walking bashfully. She said: إِنَّ أَبِي يَدْعُوكَ لِيَجْزِيَكَ أَجْرَ ما سَقَيْتَ لَنا — "My father invites you that he may reward you for having watered [our flock] for us."

It is narrated that Musa (peace be upon him) said to her: "Direct me to the road and walk behind me, for we, the children of Ya‘qub, do not look at the backs of women." When he came to him and recounted the story to him, he said: لا تَخَفْ نَجَوْتَ مِنَ الْقَوْمِ الظَّالِمِينَ — "Do not be afraid. You have been delivered from the wrongdoing lot."

He said: إِنَّ أِي أُرِيدُ أَنْ أُنْكِحَكَ إِحْدَى ابْنَتَيَّ هاتَيْنِ ـ عَلى أَنْ تَأْجُرَنِي ثَمانِيَ حِجَجٍ فَإِنْ أَتْمَمْتَ عَشْراً فَمِنْ عِنْدِكَ — "I desire to marry you to one of these two daughters of mine, on condition that you hire yourself to me for eight years; but if you complete ten, that will be of your own accord." It is narrated that he completed the fuller of them, for the prophets (peace be upon them) only take what is more virtuous and complete.

I say: Al-Qummi narrated something similar in his Tafsir.

In al-Kafi, from ‘Ali ibn Ibrahim, from his father, from Ibn Abi ‘Umayr, from someone who mentioned it from Abu ‘Abdillah (peace be upon him) regarding the saying of Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, recounting from Musa (peace be upon him): رَبِّ إِنِّي لِما أَنْزَلْتَ إِلَيَّ مِنْ خَيْرٍ فَقِيرٌ — "My Lord! I am indeed in need of any good You may send down to me." He said: "He was asking for food."

I say: Al-‘Ayyashi narrated from Hafs from him (peace be upon him) the same, and its wording is: "He only meant food." Also from Layth from Abu Ja‘far (peace be upon him) the same. In Nahj al-Balagha there is the same, and its wording is: "By Allah, he did not ask Him for anything but bread to eat."

In al-Durr al-Manthur, Ibn Mardawayh narrated from Anas ibn Malik that the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) said: "When Musa watered for the two young women and then withdrew to the shade, he said: رَبِّ إِنِّي لِما أَنْزَلْتَ إِلَيَّ مِنْ خَيْرٍ فَقِيرٌ — 'My Lord! I am indeed in need of any good You may send down to me.' He said: 'On that day, he was in need of a handful of dates.'"

In the Tafsir of al-Qummi: One of the daughters of Shu‘ayb said: يا أَبَتِ اسْتَأْجِرْهُ إِنَّ خَيْرَ مَنِ اسْتَأْجَرْتَ الْقَوِيُّ الْأَمِينُ — "Father, hire him. Indeed the best you can hire is the powerful and the trustworthy." Shu‘ayb (peace be upon him) said to her: "As for his strength, you have known it by his drawing the bucket alone; but how did you know his trustworthiness?" She said: "When he said to me: 'Stay behind me and direct me to the road, for we are from a people who do not look at the backs of women,' I knew he was not of those who look at the backs of women. This is his trustworthiness."

I say: Something similar is narrated in al-Majma‘ from ‘Ali (عليه السلام).

In al-Majma‘, al-Hasan ibn Sa‘id narrated from Safwan from Abu ‘Abdillah (peace be upon him) who said: "I asked: Which of the two was it that said: 'My father invites you'? He said: 'The one he married.' It was asked: 'Which of the two terms did he complete?' He said: 'The more complete and longer of them, ten years.' It was asked: 'Did he consummate the marriage with her before the condition was fulfilled or after its completion?' He said: 'Before it was completed.' It was said to him: 'Can a man marry a woman and condition for her father two months of labor; is that permissible?' He said: 'Musa knew that he would complete his condition for him.' It was asked: 'How?' He said: 'He knew that he would live until he fulfilled it.'"
