I say: The completion of ten years is narrated in al-Durr al-Manthur from the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) through several chains.

In the Tafsir of al-‘Ayyashi, al-Halabi said: Abu ‘Abdillah (peace be upon him) was asked about the House — was it visited for pilgrimage before the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) was sent? He said: "Yes — and the confirmation of that in the Quran is the saying of Shu‘ayb when he said to Musa (peace be upon him) when he married: عَلى أَنْ تَأْجُرَنِي ثَمانِيَ حِجَجٍ — 'on condition that you hire yourself to me for eight pilgrimages' — and he did not say eight years."

Ayahs

۞ فَلَمَّا قَضَىٰ مُوسَى ٱلْأَجَلَ وَسَارَ بِأَهْلِهِۦٓ ءَانَسَ مِن جَانِبِ ٱلطُّورِ نَارًا قَالَ لِأَهْلِهِ ٱمْكُثُوٓا۟ إِنِّىٓ ءَانَسْتُ نَارًا لَّعَلِّىٓ ءَاتِيكُم مِّنْهَا بِخَبَرٍ أَوْ جَذْوَةٍ مِّنَ ٱلنَّارِ لَعَلَّكُمْ تَصْطَلُونَ (٢٩)
So when Moses completed the term and set out with his family, he descried a fire on the side of the mountain. He said to his family, ‘Wait! Indeed, I descry a fire! Maybe I will bring you some news from it, or a brand of fire so that you may warm yourselves.’ (29)
فَلَمَّآ أَتَىٰهَا نُودِىَ مِن شَـٰطِئِ ٱلْوَادِ ٱلْأَيْمَنِ فِى ٱلْبُقْعَةِ ٱلْمُبَـٰرَكَةِ مِنَ ٱلشَّجَرَةِ أَن يَـٰمُوسَىٰٓ إِنِّىٓ أَنَا ٱللَّهُ رَبُّ ٱلْعَـٰلَمِينَ (٣٠)
When he approached it, he was called from the right bank of the valley in that blessed site from the tree: ‘Moses! Indeed I am Allah, the Lord of all the worlds!’ (30)
وَأَنْ أَلْقِ عَصَاكَ ۖ فَلَمَّا رَءَاهَا تَهْتَزُّ كَأَنَّهَا جَآنٌّ وَلَّىٰ مُدْبِرًا وَلَمْ يُعَقِّبْ ۚ يَـٰمُوسَىٰٓ أَقْبِلْ وَلَا تَخَفْ ۖ إِنَّكَ مِنَ ٱلْـَٔامِنِينَ (٣١)
And: ‘Throw down your staff!’ And when he saw it wriggling as if it were a snake, he turned his back [to flee], without looking back. ‘Moses! Come forward, and do not be afraid. Indeed you are safe.’ (31)
ٱسْلُكْ يَدَكَ فِى جَيْبِكَ تَخْرُجْ بَيْضَآءَ مِنْ غَيْرِ سُوٓءٍ وَٱضْمُمْ إِلَيْكَ جَنَاحَكَ مِنَ ٱلرَّهْبِ ۖ فَذَٰنِكَ بُرْهَـٰنَانِ مِن رَّبِّكَ إِلَىٰ فِرْعَوْنَ وَمَلَإِي۟هِۦٓ ۚ إِنَّهُمْ كَانُوا۟ قَوْمًا فَـٰسِقِينَ (٣٢)
‘Insert your hand into your shirt. It will emerge white, without any fault, and keep your arms drawn in awe to your sides. These shall be two proofs from your Lord to Pharaoh and his elite. They are indeed a transgressing lot.’ (32)
قَالَ رَبِّ إِنِّى قَتَلْتُ مِنْهُمْ نَفْسًا فَأَخَافُ أَن يَقْتُلُونِ (٣٣)
He said, ‘My Lord! Indeed, I have killed one of their men, so I fear they will kill me. (33)
وَأَخِى هَـٰرُونُ هُوَ أَفْصَحُ مِنِّى لِسَانًا فَأَرْسِلْهُ مَعِىَ رِدْءًا يُصَدِّقُنِىٓ ۖ إِنِّىٓ أَخَافُ أَن يُكَذِّبُونِ (٣٤)
Aaron, my brother—he is more eloquent than me in speech. So send him with me as a helper to confirm me, for I fear that they will impugn me.’ (34)
قَالَ سَنَشُدُّ عَضُدَكَ بِأَخِيكَ وَنَجْعَلُ لَكُمَا سُلْطَـٰنًا فَلَا يَصِلُونَ إِلَيْكُمَا ۚ بِـَٔايَـٰتِنَآ أَنتُمَا وَمَنِ ٱتَّبَعَكُمَا ٱلْغَـٰلِبُونَ (٣٥)
He said, ‘We will strengthen your arm by means of your brother, and invest both of you with such authority that they will not touch you. With the help of Our signs, you two, and those who follow the two of you, shall be the victors.’ (35)
فَلَمَّا جَآءَهُم مُّوسَىٰ بِـَٔايَـٰتِنَا بَيِّنَـٰتٍ قَالُوا۟ مَا هَـٰذَآ إِلَّا سِحْرٌ مُّفْتَرًى وَمَا سَمِعْنَا بِهَـٰذَا فِىٓ ءَابَآئِنَا ٱلْأَوَّلِينَ (٣٦)
When Moses brought them Our manifest signs, they said, ‘This is nothing but concocted magic. We never heard of such a thing among our forefathers.’ (36)
وَقَالَ مُوسَىٰ رَبِّىٓ أَعْلَمُ بِمَن جَآءَ بِٱلْهُدَىٰ مِنْ عِندِهِۦ وَمَن تَكُونُ لَهُۥ عَـٰقِبَةُ ٱلدَّارِ ۖ إِنَّهُۥ لَا يُفْلِحُ ٱلظَّـٰلِمُونَ (٣٧)
Moses said, ‘My Lord knows best who brings guidance from Him, and in whose favour the outcome of that abode will be. The wrongdoers will not be felicitous.’ (37)
وَقَالَ فِرْعَوْنُ يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلْمَلَأُ مَا عَلِمْتُ لَكُم مِّنْ إِلَـٰهٍ غَيْرِى فَأَوْقِدْ لِى يَـٰهَـٰمَـٰنُ عَلَى ٱلطِّينِ فَٱجْعَل لِّى صَرْحًا لَّعَلِّىٓ أَطَّلِعُ إِلَىٰٓ إِلَـٰهِ مُوسَىٰ وَإِنِّى لَأَظُنُّهُۥ مِنَ ٱلْكَـٰذِبِينَ (٣٨)
Pharaoh said, ‘O [members of the] elite! I do not know of any god that you may have besides me. Haman, light for me a fire over clay, and build me a tower so that I may take a look at Moses’ god, and indeed I consider him to be a liar!’ (38)
وَٱسْتَكْبَرَ هُوَ وَجُنُودُهُۥ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ بِغَيْرِ ٱلْحَقِّ وَظَنُّوٓا۟ أَنَّهُمْ إِلَيْنَا لَا يُرْجَعُونَ (٣٩)
He and his hosts unduly acted arrogantly in the land, and thought they would not be brought back to Us. (39)
فَأَخَذْنَـٰهُ وَجُنُودَهُۥ فَنَبَذْنَـٰهُمْ فِى ٱلْيَمِّ ۖ فَٱنظُرْ كَيْفَ كَانَ عَـٰقِبَةُ ٱلظَّـٰلِمِينَ (٤٠)
So We seized him and his hosts, and threw them into the sea. So observe how was the fate of the wrongdoers! (40)
وَجَعَلْنَـٰهُمْ أَئِمَّةً يَدْعُونَ إِلَى ٱلنَّارِ ۖ وَيَوْمَ ٱلْقِيَـٰمَةِ لَا يُنصَرُونَ (٤١)
We made them leaders who invite to the Fire, and on the Day of Resurrection they will not receive any help. (41)
وَأَتْبَعْنَـٰهُمْ فِى هَـٰذِهِ ٱلدُّنْيَا لَعْنَةً ۖ وَيَوْمَ ٱلْقِيَـٰمَةِ هُم مِّنَ ٱلْمَقْبُوحِينَ (٤٢)
We made a curse pursue them in this world, and on the Day of Resurrection they will be among the disfigured. (42)

(Commentary)

This is another chapter from the story of Musa (peace be upon him), containing a summary of his narrative from the time he set out with his family from Madyan heading for Egypt, his commission with the message to Pharaoh and his elite to deliver the Children of Israel, and their denial of him, until Allah drowned them in the sea. The story concludes with the bestowing of the Book upon him, as if this is the primary objective in the narration of the story.

His saying, the Exalted: فَلَمَّا قَضى مُوسَى الْأَجَلَ وَسارَ بِأَهْلِهِ آنَسَ مِنْ جانِبِ الطُّورِ ناراً — "So when Moses had completed the term and set out with his family, he descried a fire on the side of the Mount" (28:29) etc. What is meant by his "completing the term" (قضاء الأجل) is his finishing the period of service to Shu‘ayb (peace be upon him); it is narrated that he completed the longer of the two terms. Descrying (الإيناس) means perceiving and seeing. A brand (الجذوة) of fire is a piece of it, and warming oneself (الاصطلاء) means seeking warmth.

The context bears witness that the event took place at night, and it was a severely cold night. They had lost their way, and he saw a fire from the side of the Mount (الطور) as they were approaching it. He commanded his family to remain so that he could go toward what he had perceived, hoping he might find someone there to inform him of the way, or take a piece of the fire so they could warm themselves. In the story as found in Surah Ta-Ha, in place of His saying: لَعَلِّي آتِيكُمْ مِنْها بِخَبَرٍ — "maybe I will bring you some news from it" (28:29) etc., there is His saying: لَعَلِّي آتِيكُمْ مِنْها بِقَبَسٍ أَوْ أَجِدُ عَلَى النَّارِ هُدىً — "maybe I will bring you a brand from it, or find some guidance at the fire" (20:10), which more clearly indicates that they had lost their way.

Similarly, in His saying when addressing his family: امْكُثُوا — "Wait" (28:29) etc., there is evidence that there were others with her [his wife] for whom the plural address (خطاب الجمع) was appropriate.

His saying, the Exalted: فَلَمَّا أَتاها نُودِيَ مِنْ شاطِئِ الْوادِ الْأَيْمَنِ فِي الْبُقْعَةِ الْمُبارَكَةِ مِنَ الشَّجَرَةِ — "When he came to it, he was called from the right bank of the valley in the blessed site, from the tree" (28:30) etc. It is stated in al-Mufradat: The bank (شاطئ) of the valley is its side. He also said: The origin of valley (الوادي) is the place where water flows, and from this, the opening between two mountains is called a valley; its plural is awdiya (أودية). A site (البقعة) is a piece of land that differs in form from that which is adjacent to it.

What is meant by "the right" (الأيمن) is the right side as opposed to the left, and it is an adjective describing the "bank." No weight should be given to what some have said: that "the right" (الأيمن) is derived from "the right hand" (اليمين) as opposed to "the ill-omened" (الأشأم) derived from "ill-omen" (الشؤم).

The "blessed site" (البقعة المباركة) is a specific portion of the right bank in the valley where the tree was located from which he was called. Its blessedness is due to its being honored by the divine proximity and speech. He was commanded to remove his sandals therein because of its sanctity, as the Exalted said in the story in Surah Ta-Ha: فَاخْلَعْ نَعْلَيْكَ إِنَّكَ بِالْوادِ الْمُقَدَّسِ طُوىً — "so take off your sandals; you are indeed in the sacred valley of Tuwa" (20:12).

There is no doubt that the verse indicates that the tree was the source of the call and the speech in some manner. However, the speech—which is the speech of Allah, the Glorified—was not inherent in it as speech is inherent in a speaker among us. Rather, it was nothing but a veil (حجاب) through which He, the Glorified, veiled Himself, and He spoke to him from behind it in a manner befitting the presence of His sanctity regarding the meaning of veiling, while He encompasses all things. The Exalted said: وَما كانَ لِبَشَرٍ أَنْ يُكَلِّمَهُ اللهُ إِلَّا وَحْياً أَوْ مِنْ وَراءِ حِجابٍ أَوْ يُرْسِلَ رَسُولاً فَيُوحِيَ بِإِذْنِهِ ما يَشاءُ — "It is not for any human that Allah should speak to him except by revelation or from behind a veil, or by sending a messenger who reveals by His leave whatever He wishes" (42:51).

From here, the weakness of what has been said becomes apparent: that the tree was the locus (محل) of the speech because speech is an accident (عرض) that requires a locus to subsist in.

Similarly, the weakness of what has been said: that this speaking is the highest of the stations of the prophets (peace be upon them)—that they hear the speech of Allah, the Glorified, without an intermediary or a conveyor. This is because it was speech from behind a veil, and the veil is an intermediary. The apparent meaning of the verse from Surah al-Shura mentioned above is that the highest form of speaking is revelation (الوحي) — the direct divine communication to the heart — without the intermediary of a veil or a conveying messenger.

The saying: أَنْ يا مُوسى إِنِّي أَنَا اللهُ رَبُّ الْعالَمِينَ — "O Moses! Indeed I am Allah, the Lord of all the worlds" (28:30). The particle an (أن) here is explanatory (تفسيرية). It contains an announcement of the Transcendent Essence (الذات المتعالية) named by the Name of Majesty (اسم الجلالة) [Allah], described by the oneness of Lordship (وحدانية الربوبية) which negates absolute polytheism. For His being the Lord of all the worlds—and the Lord (الرب) is the Master and Disposer (المالك المدبر) of His kingdom who deserves worship from His subjects—leaves nothing in all the worlds to be a subject to another, such that there would be another lord besides Him or a deity worshipped other than Him.

Thus, the verse contains a summary of what was detailed in Surah Ta-Ha in this section of the call, regarding the reference to the three principles—namely, monotheism (التوحيد) — the absolute oneness of Allah in His essence, attributes, and acts, prophethood (النبوة), and the Return (المعاد) — the resurrection and final judgment — as He said: إِنَّنِي أَنَا اللهُ لا إِلهَ إِلَّا أَنَا فَاعْبُدْنِي وَأَقِمِ الصَّلاةَ لِذِكْرِي إِنَّ السَّاعَةَ آتِيَةٌ — "Indeed I am Allah—there is no god except Me. So worship Me, and maintain the prayer for My remembrance. Indeed the Hour is bound to come" (20:14-16).

His saying, the Exalted: وَأَنْ أَلْقِ عَصاكَ فَلَمَّا رَآها تَهْتَزُّ كَأَنَّها جَانٌّ وَلَّى مُدْبِراً وَلَمْ يُعَقِّبْ — "Throw down your staff!’ And when he saw it quirming as if it were a snake, he turned his back [to flee], and did not look back" (28:31). Its explanation has preceded in Surah al-Naml.

His saying, the Exalted: يا مُوسى أَقْبِلْ وَلا تَخَفْ إِنَّكَ مِنَ الْآمِنِينَ — "‘O Moses! Come forward and do not be afraid. Indeed you are safe" (28:31). With the estimation of "it was said," meaning: it was said to him: "Come forward and do not fear, for you are among the secure." In this address is a granting of security to him. Through this, the meaning of His saying in this part of the story in Surah al-Naml becomes clear: يا مُوسى لا تَخَفْ إِنِّي لا يَخافُ لَدَيَّ الْمُرْسَلُونَ — "O Moses! Do not be afraid. Indeed the apostles do not fear in My presence" (27:10). It is a granting of security, meaning: "You are a messenger, and the messengers are secure in My presence," and it is not a matter of rebuke or reproach at all.

His saying, the Exalted: اسْلُكْ يَدَكَ فِي جَيْبِكَ تَخْرُجْ بَيْضاءَ مِنْ غَيْرِ سُوءٍ — "Insert your hand into your opening. It will emerge white, without any ill" (28:32). What is meant by "inserting his hand into his opening" (سلوك يده في جيبه) is putting it inside. What is meant by "ill" (السوء)—according to what has been said—is leprosy.

It appears that in this qualification there is an allusion to what is in the current Torah in this part of the story: "Then the Lord said to him also, 'Put your hand into your bosom.' So he put his hand into his bosom, and when he took it out, behold, his hand was leprous, like snow."

His saying, the Exalted: وَاضْمُمْ إِلَيْكَ جَناحَكَ مِنَ الرَّهْبِ — "and draw your arm close to your side [to calm] from fear" (28:32) until the end of the verse. Fear (الرهب) is pronounced with a fatha then sukun, or two fathas, or a damma then sukun. As for the wing (الجناح), it is said: what is meant by it is the hand, and it is said: the upper arm (العضد).

It is said: the meaning of "drawing the wing to himself from fear" is that he should fold his hands over his chest when fear overcomes him upon witnessing the transformation of the staff into a serpent, so that the fear in his heart might depart.

It is also said: when he threw the staff and it became a serpent, he spread his hands as one who is protecting himself, and they are his "wings." So it was said to him: "Draw your wing to yourself," meaning: do not spread your hands out of fear of the serpent, for you are safe from its harm.

Both views—as you can see—are based on the sentence, namely His saying: وَاضْمُمْ (and draw) etc., being a continuation of His saying: أَقْبِلْ وَلا تَخَفْ إِنَّكَ مِنَ الْآمِنِينَ (Come forward and do not be afraid. Indeed you are safe). This does not harmonize with the intervention of His saying: اسْلُكْ يَدَكَ فِي جَيْبِكَ (Insert your hand into your opening) etc., between the two sentences by separation without a conjunction.

It is also said: the sentence is a metaphor (كناية) for the command to be resolute in what Allah, the Glorified, intended for him, and an urging to be serious in the matter of the message so that the fear that might overwhelm him in some circumstances does not hinder him.

It is not unlikely that the meaning of the sentence is a command to adopt the appearance of one who is humble and modest (الخاشع المتواضع). For it is the habit of the arrogant and self-conceited person to distance his upper arms from his sides, like one who stretches in his gait. Thus, it would be in the sense of what Allah commanded the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) regarding humility toward the believers by His saying: وَاخْفِضْ جَناحَكَ لِلْمُؤْمِنِينَ — "and lower your wing to the faithful" (15:88) according to some interpretations.

His saying, the Exalted: قالَ رَبِّ إِنِّي قَتَلْتُ مِنْهُمْ نَفْساً فَأَخافُ أَنْ يَقْتُلُونِ — "He said, ‘My Lord! Indeed I have killed one of their men, so I fear they will kill me" (28:33). This is a reference to his killing of the Copt with a blow, and he feared they would kill him in retaliation (قصاصا).

His saying, the Exalted: وَأَخِي هارُونُ هُوَ أَفْصَحُ مِنِّي لِساناً فَأَرْسِلْهُ مَعِي رِدْءاً يُصَدِّقُنِي إِنِّي أَخافُ أَنْ يُكَذِّبُونِ — "And my brother Aaron is more eloquent than me in speech. So send him with me as a helper to confirm me, for I fear that they will impugn me" (28:34). It is stated in al-Majma‘: It is said: "So-and-so is a helper (ردء) to so-and-so" when he assists him and strengthens his back.

His saying: إِنِّي أَخافُ أَنْ يُكَذِّبُونِ (for I fear that they will impugn me) is the justification for his request to send Harun with him. The context indicates that he feared they would deny him, causing him to become angry and unable to clarify his proof due to a speech impediment (لكنة) in his tongue; it was not that he asked for him to be sent so that they would not deny him, for whoever denies him would not care if he denies Harun along with him. Evidence for this is what occurs in Surah al-Shu‘ara’ in this part of the story: قالَ رَبِّ إِنِّي أَخافُ أَنْ يُكَذِّبُونِ وَيَضِيقُ صَدْرِي وَلا يَنْطَلِقُ لِسانِي فَأَرْسِلْ إِلى هارُونَ — "He said, ‘My Lord! I fear they will impugn me, and my breast will be straitened and my tongue will not be fluent. So send for Aaron" (26:12-13).

The summary of the meaning is: "My brother Harun is more eloquent than me in speech, so send him as a helper to me to clarify my truthfulness in my claim when they dispute with me, for I fear they will deny me and I will be unable to clarify the truthfulness of my claim."

His saying, the Exalted: قالَ سَنَشُدُّ عَضُدَكَ بِأَخِيكَ وَنَجْعَلُ لَكُما سُلْطاناً فَلا يَصِلُونَ إِلَيْكُما بِآياتِنا أَنْتُما وَمَنِ اتَّبَعَكُمَا الْغالِبُونَ — "He said, ‘We will strengthen your arm by your brother, and We will give you both an authority, so they will not reach you. With Our signs, you two and those who follow you shall be the victors" (28:35). "Strengthening his arm by his brother" is a metaphor for empowering him through him. "Not reaching them" is a metaphor for their lack of power over them to kill them or the like, as if the two groups are racing and one is always ahead while the other cannot catch up to reach them, let alone overtake them.

The meaning is: He said, "We will empower you and assist you with your brother Harun, and We will grant you both authority and victory over them, so they will not have power over you because of Our signs by which We shall make you manifest." Then He said: أَنْتُما وَمَنِ اتَّبَعَكُمَا الْغالِبُونَ (you two and those who follow you shall be the victors), which is an explanation of His saying: وَنَجْعَلُ لَكُما سُلْطاناً (and We will give you both an authority) etc., clarifying that this authority encompasses them and whoever follows them among the people.

It has thus become clear that "authority" (السلطان) is in the sense of dominance and victory. It is also said: it is in the sense of proof (الحجة), in which case it is better for His saying: بِآياتِنا (With Our signs) to be connected to His saying: الْغالِبُونَ (the victors), not to His saying: فَلا يَصِلُونَ إِلَيْكُما (so they will not reach you). Other views have been mentioned regarding the verse, but there is no benefit in addressing them.

His saying, the Exalted: فَلَمَّا جاءَهُمْ مُوسى بِآياتِنا بَيِّناتٍ قالُوا ما هذا إِلَّا سِحْرٌ مُفْتَرىً — "When Moses came to them with Our manifest signs, they said, ‘This is nothing but a fabricated magic" (28:36) etc., meaning magic described as being fabricated. "Fabricated" (المفترى) is a passive participle meaning "invented," or a mim-infinitive (مصدر ميمي) used to describe the magic for emphasis.

The reference in His saying: ما هذا إِلَّا سِحْرٌ مُفْتَرىً (This is nothing but a fabricated magic) is to the signs he brought; meaning: "These miracles he has brought are nothing but an invented magic that he has forged and falsely attributed to Allah."

The reference in His saying: وَما سَمِعْنا بِهذا فِي آبائِنَا الْأَوَّلِينَ — "and we never heard of such a thing among our forefathers" (28:36) is to the call he brought and for which he established the proof of the signs. As for the possibility that it refers to the signs, it does not harmonize with the repetition of the demonstrative pronoun, especially since they were claiming they would bring the like of them, as Allah narrated from Pharaoh in his saying: فَلَنَأْتِيَنَّكَ بِسِحْرٍ مِثْلِهِ — "we will surely bring you a magic like it" (20:58). Furthermore, the lack of familiarity with magic or the lack of a precedent for it would not benefit them at all in their claim.

So the meaning is: "What Musa has brought is an innovated religion that has not been transmitted from our early forefathers as having been adopted by them at any time." This is consistent with what is narrated in the following verse of Musa's saying: رَبِّي أَعْلَمُ بِمَنْ جاءَ بِالْهُدى (My Lord knows best him who brings guidance) etc.

His saying, the Exalted: وَقالَ مُوسى رَبِّي أَعْلَمُ بِمَنْ جاءَ بِالْهُدى مِنْ عِنْدِهِ وَمَنْ تَكُونُ لَهُ عاقِبَةُ الدَّارِ — "Moses said, ‘My Lord knows best him who brings guidance from Him, and him for whom shall be the ultimate abode" (28:37) etc. The requirement of the context is that this is a response from Musa to their saying: وَما سَمِعْنا بِهذا فِي آبائِنَا الْأَوَّلِينَ (and we never heard of such a thing among our forefathers) in their rejection of Musa's claim. It is a response based on challenge (التحدي), as if he is saying: "Indeed my Lord—and He is the Lord of all the worlds, to Whom belongs the creation and the command—is more knowledgeable than you as to who has brought guidance and for whom shall be the ultimate abode. He is the One who sent me as a messenger bringing guidance—which is the religion of monotheism—and He promised me that whoever adopts my religion shall have the ultimate abode. The proof for this is the manifest signs He has given me from Himself."

By His saying: رَبِّي أَعْلَمُ بِمَنْ جاءَ بِالْهُدى مِنْ عِنْدِهِ (My Lord knows best him who brings guidance from Him), he intends himself, and by "guidance" (الهدى) he means the religious call he brought.

By His saying: وَمَنْ تَكُونُ لَهُ عاقِبَةُ الدَّارِ (and him for whom shall be the ultimate abode), what is meant by "the ultimate abode" (عاقبة الدار) is either Paradise, which is the Abode of the Hereafter inhabited by the felicitous, as the Exalted said narrating from them: وَأَوْرَثَنَا الْأَرْضَ نَتَبَوَّأُ مِنَ الْجَنَّةِ حَيْثُ نَشاءُ — "and given us the land to inherit, so that we may settle in paradise wherever we wish" (39:74); or the outcome of the abode of this world, as in His saying: قالَ مُوسى لِقَوْمِهِ اسْتَعِينُوا بِاللهِ وَاصْبِرُوا إِنَّ الْأَرْضَ لِلَّهِ يُورِثُها مَنْ يَشاءُ مِنْ عِبادِهِ وَالْعاقِبَةُ لِلْمُتَّقِينَ — "Moses said to his people, ‘Plead with Allah for help and be patient. Indeed the earth belongs to Allah: He gives its inheritance to whomever He wishes of His servants, and the outcome will be in favour of the God-conscious" (7:128); or it is more general, encompassing both this world and the Hereafter. The third is the best of the views, followed by the second, as supported by His justification: إِنَّهُ لا يُفْلِحُ الظَّالِمُونَ — "Indeed the wrongdoers will not be felicitous" (28:37).

In His saying: إِنَّهُ لا يُفْلِحُ الظَّالِمُونَ (Indeed the wrongdoers will not be felicitous), there is an allusion to Pharaoh and his people, and in it is a negation that they shall have the ultimate abode, for they built their way of life upon injustice (الظلم). In it is also a deviation from social justice (العدالة الاجتماعية) to which the human primordial nature (الفطرة) — the innate disposition toward truth that Allah placed in every human being — guides, in accordance with the cosmic order.

Some commentators said: The reason for conjoining His saying: وَقالَ مُوسى رَبِّي أَعْلَمُ (Moses said, ‘My Lord knows best) etc., to their saying: ما هذا إِلَّا سِحْرٌ مُفْتَرىً (This is nothing but a fabricated magic) etc., is to narrate both statements so that the listener may weigh them to distinguish the sound from the corrupt. What we have presented—that Musa's (peace be upon him) statement was intended to refute their statement—is more in accord with the context.

His saying, the Exalted: وَقالَ فِرْعَوْنُ يا أَيُّهَا الْمَلَأُ ما عَلِمْتُ لَكُمْ مِنْ إِلهٍ غَيْرِي — "Pharaoh said, ‘O elite! I do not know of any god for you other than myself" (28:38) until the end of the verse. In it is an allusion to Musa regarding the true call he brought, supported by miraculous signs. He intends that the truth of what Musa calls to has not become clear to him, nor has it been established that the miracles he brought are signs from Allah, and that he knows of no god for them other than himself.

His saying: ما عَلِمْتُ لَكُمْ مِنْ إِلهٍ غَيْرِي (I do not know of any god for you other than myself) presents the speech in the guise of fairness so that it might find acceptance in the hearts of the elite, as is apparent in his saying narrated elsewhere: ما أُرِيكُمْ إِلَّا ما أَرى وَما أَهْدِيكُمْ إِلَّا سَبِيلَ الرَّشادِ — "I only show you what I see, and I only guide you to the way of rectitude" (40:29).

The summary of the meaning is: it appeared to the elite that it was not clear to him from Musa's call and signs that there is a god who is the Lord of all the worlds, nor did he obtain knowledge that there is a god other than himself. Then he commanded Haman to build him a tower so that he might perhaps look upon the god of Musa.

Through this, it becomes clear that his saying: ما عَلِمْتُ لَكُمْ مِنْ إِلهٍ غَيْرِي (I do not know of any god for you other than myself) is of the nature of "reversal of restriction" (قصر القلب). For Musa (peace be upon him) was affirming divinity for Allah, the Glorified, and negating it from others, while Pharaoh was negating it from Him, the Exalted, and affirming it for himself. As for the other deities that he and his people worshipped, they were not addressed here.

His saying: فَأَوْقِدْ لِي يا هامانُ عَلَى الطِّينِ فَاجْعَلْ لِي صَرْحاً — "so kindle for me, O Haman, [a fire] for [baking] bricks, and build me a tower" (28:38). What is meant by "kindling upon the clay" is stoking the fire upon it for the manufacture of bricks used in buildings. A tower (الصرح) is a high, exposed building, from saraha (صرح) when something becomes manifest. Thus, the sentence contains a command to make bricks and build a high palace from them.

His saying: لَعَلِّي أَطَّلِعُ إِلى إِلهِ مُوسى — "that I may perhaps have a look at the god of Moses" (28:38). He attributed the god to Musa with the consideration that he is the one who calls to Him. The speech is of the nature of placing the result in the place of the premise. The estimation is: "Make me a tower that I may ascend to its highest levels and look toward the sky, that I may perhaps look upon the god of Musa." It is as if he thought that He, the Exalted, is a body (جسم) residing in some layers of the atmosphere or the celestial spheres, so he hoped that if he looked from the top of the tower, he might look upon Him. Or perhaps this statement was of the nature of mystification (التعمية) of the people and misleading them.

It is possible that the meaning was for him to build an observatory (رصدا) to observe the stars to see if there was anything in them indicating the sending of a messenger or the truth of what Musa (peace be upon him) described. This is supported by his saying as narrated elsewhere: يا هامانُ ابْنِ لِي صَرْحاً لَعَلِّي أَبْلُغُ الْأَسْبابَ أَسْبابَ السَّماواتِ فَأَطَّلِعَ إِلى إِلهِ مُوسى وَإِنِّي لَأَظُنُّهُ كاذِباً — "O Haman! Build me a tower so that I may reach the routes, the routes of the heavens, and look at the God of Moses, though I indeed consider him a liar" (40:36-37).

His saying: وَإِنِّي لَأَظُنُّهُ مِنَ الْكاذِبِينَ — "though I indeed consider him a liar" (28:38). This is a progression (ترق) from the ignorance indicated by his saying: "I do not know of any god for you other than myself" to the suspicion of non-existence. He was indeed a liar in this statement of his, and he only said it to deceive and mystify the people. Musa had addressed him saying: لَقَدْ عَلِمْتَ ما أَنْزَلَ هؤُلاءِ إِلَّا رَبُّ السَّماواتِ وَالْأَرْضِ — "You certainly know that no one has sent these down except the Lord of the heavens and the earth" (17:102).

Some have mentioned that his saying: ما عَلِمْتُ لَكُمْ مِنْ إِلهٍ غَيْرِي (I do not know of any god for you other than myself) is of the nature of negating the known by negating the knowledge—meaning if it existed, it would have appeared. Thus, it would be similar to His saying: قُلْ أَتُنَبِّئُونَ اللهَ بِما لا يَعْلَمُ فِي السَّماواتِ وَلا فِي الْأَرْضِ — "Say, ‘Will you inform Allah of something He does not know in the heavens or on the earth?’" (10:18). You are well aware that this does not harmonize with the end of the verse.

His saying, the Exalted: وَاسْتَكْبَرَ هُوَ وَجُنُودُهُ فِي الْأَرْضِ بِغَيْرِ الْحَقِّ وَظَنُّوا أَنَّهُمْ إِلَيْنا لا يُرْجَعُونَ — "He and his hosts acted arrogantly in the land unduly, and they thought they would not be brought back to Us" (28:39). That is, their state was that of one for whom the lack of return is more probable. This is because they were certain in themselves, as the Exalted said: وَجَحَدُوا بِها وَاسْتَيْقَنَتْها أَنْفُسُهُمْ ظُلْماً وَعُلُوًّا — "They impugned them—though they were convinced of them in their hearts—out of wrongdoing and pride" (27:14).

His saying, the Exalted: فَأَخَذْناهُ وَجُنُودَهُ — "So We seized him and his hosts" (28:40) etc. Casting (النبذ) is throwing, and the sea (اليم) is the ocean; the rest is clear.

In the verse, there is a disdain for their affair and a terrifying description of the punishment that befell them, which is not hidden.

His saying, the Exalted: وَجَعَلْناهُمْ أَئِمَّةً يَدْعُونَ إِلَى النَّارِ وَيَوْمَ الْقِيامَةِ لا يُنْصَرُونَ — "We made them leaders who invite to the Fire, and on the Day of Resurrection they will not be helped" (28:41). Inviting to the Fire is inviting to that which necessitates the Fire, such as disbelief and sins, because these are what will be manifested for them on the Day of Resurrection as a fire in which they are punished. Or what is meant by "the Fire" is that which necessitates it, metaphorically, by mentioning the effect and intending the cause.

The meaning of "making them leaders who invite to the Fire" is making them predecessors in misguidance (الضلال) whom the later ones follow. There is no harm in this, as it is by way of recompense (المجازاة) for their precedence in disbelief and denial, and it is not of the nature of initial misleading (الإضلال الابتدائي) at all.

It is said: what is meant by making them leaders who invite to the Fire is naming them as such, similar to His saying: وَجَعَلُوا الْمَلائِكَةَ الَّذِينَ هُمْ عِبادُ الرَّحْمنِ إِناثاً — "They have made the angels, who are servants of the All-beneficent, females" (43:19).

However, the following verse—according to what will come of its meaning—does not harmonize with this. Furthermore, that "making" (الجعل) in the cited verse means "naming" is not established.

His saying: وَيَوْمَ الْقِيامَةِ لا يُنْصَرُونَ (and on the Day of Resurrection they will not be helped) means they will not receive the intercession (الشفاعة) — the act of mediating on behalf of others before Allah — of any helper.

His saying, the Exalted: وَأَتْبَعْناهُمْ فِي هذِهِ الدُّنْيا لَعْنَةً وَيَوْمَ الْقِيامَةِ هُمْ مِنَ الْمَقْبُوحِينَ — "We made a curse to follow them in this world, and on the Day of Resurrection they will be among the loathed" (28:42). This is an explanation of the consequence of what He described them with in the previous verse. Since they are leaders followed by those after them in disbelief and sins, the misguidance of disbelief and sins from their followers and adherents continues to follow them, and they bear burdens like those of the followers. Thus, a curse follows them, continuing as long as disbelief and sins continue after them.

So the verse is in the sense of His saying: وَلَيَحْمِلُنَّ أَثْقالَهُمْ وَأَثْقالاً مَعَ أَثْقالِهِمْ — "They will surely carry their own burdens, and [other] burdens along with their own burdens" (29:13) and His saying: وَنَكْتُبُ ما قَدَّمُوا وَآثارَهُمْ — "and We write what they have sent ahead and their effects" (36:12). The indefiniteness (تنكير) of "curse" (لعنة) is to indicate its magnitude and continuity.

Similarly, since no help from a helper reaches them on the Day of Resurrection, they are such that souls are repelled and disgusted by them, people flee from them, and no one draws near to them—which is the meaning of "loathsomeness" (القبح). Allah the Exalted has described much of their loathsome appearance in His speech.

(Narrative Discussion)

In al-Majma‘, al-Wahidi narrated with a chain of transmission from Ibn ‘Abbas, who said: The Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) was asked: "Which of the two terms did Musa complete?" He said: "The more complete and the longer of them."

I say: Something of similar meaning was narrated with a chain of transmission from Abu Dharr from him (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم).

In al-Durr al-Manthur, Ibn Mardawayh extracted from Miqsam, who said: I met al-Hasan ibn ‘Ali ibn Abi Talib (رضي الله عنه) and said to him: "Which of the two terms did Musa complete? The first or the last?" He said: "The last."

In al-Majma‘, Abu Basir narrated from Abu Ja‘far (عليه السلام), who said: "When Musa completed the term and set out with his family toward the House, he lost the way and saw a fire. قالَ لِأَهْلِهِ امْكُثُوا إِنِّي آنَسْتُ ناراً (He said to his family, ‘Wait! Indeed I descry a fire)."

From the book Tibb al-A’immah, with its chain of transmission from Jabir al-Ju‘fi from al-Baqir (عليه السلام) in a tradition, he said: "And Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, said in the story of Musa (عليه السلام): وَأَدْخِلْ يَدَكَ فِي جَيْبِكَ تَخْرُجْ بَيْضاءَ مِنْ غَيْرِ سُوءٍ (And insert your hand into your opening. It will emerge white, without any ill)—meaning without leprosy."

In Tafsir al-Qummi, regarding His saying, the Exalted: وَأَخِي هارُونُ هُوَ أَفْصَحُ مِنِّي لِساناً فَأَرْسِلْهُ مَعِي رِدْءاً يُصَدِّقُنِي (And my brother Aaron is more eloquent than me in speech. So send him with me as a helper to confirm me). The narrator said: I said to Abu Ja‘far (عليه السلام): "How long did Musa (عليه السلام) remain absent from his mother until Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, returned him to her?" He said: "Three days."

He said: I said: "Was Harun the brother of Musa (عليه السلام) from his father and mother?" He said: "Yes. Do you not hear Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, saying: يَا بْنَ أُمَّ لا تَأْخُذْ بِلِحْيَتِي وَلا بِرَأْسِي (O son of my mother! Do not seize my beard, nor my head)?" I said: "Which of them was older in age?" He said: "Harun." I said: "Did the revelation descend upon both of them together?" He said: "The revelation would descend upon Musa, and Musa would reveal it to Harun."

I said to him: "Inform me about the laws, the judgment, the command, and the prohibition—was that for both of them?" He said: "It was Musa who would commune with his Lord, write the knowledge, and judge among the Children of Israel, and Harun would succeed him when he was absent from his people for the communion." I said: "Which of them died before the other?" He said: "Harun died before Musa, and they both died in the Wilderness (التيه)." I said: "Did Musa have children?" He said: "No, the children were for Harun and the progeny was his."

I say: The end of the narration does not agree with other narrations indicating that he had children, and in the current Torah, there is also evidence for that.

In Jawami‘ al-Jami‘, regarding His saying, the Exalted: وَاسْتَكْبَرَ هُوَ وَجُنُودُهُ (He and his hosts acted arrogantly). He (عليه السلام) said, narrating from his Lord, the Mighty and Majestic: "Pride (الكبرياء) is My cloak and greatness (العظمة) is My lower garment; whoever disputes with Me regarding either of them, I shall cast him into the Fire."

In al-Kafi, with its chain of transmission from Talhah ibn Zayd from Abu ‘Abdillah (عليه السلام), he said: "The leaders (الأئمة) in the Book of Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, are two types of leaders. Allah, the Blessed and Exalted, said: وَجَعَلْناهُمْ أَئِمَّةً يَهْدُونَ بِأَمْرِنا (We made them leaders who guide by Our command)—not by the command of the people; they put the command of Allah before their own command and the judgment of Allah before their own judgment. And He said: وَجَعَلْناهُمْ أَئِمَّةً يَدْعُونَ إِلَى النَّارِ (We made them leaders who invite to the Fire); they put their own command before the command of Allah and their own judgment before the judgment of Allah, and they follow their own desires in opposition to what is in the Book of Allah, the Mighty and Majestic."

(A Discussion Regarding the Stories of Musa and Harun, peace be upon them)

In several sections:

1 - The Station of Musa before Allah and his state of servitude: He (peace be upon him) was one of the five "Possessors of Resolve" (أولو العزم) who are the masters of the prophets and possess a Book and a Law (شريعة), as Allah the Exalted specifically mentioned them in His saying: وَإِذْ أَخَذْنا مِنَ النَّبِيِّينَ مِيثاقَهُمْ وَمِنْكَ وَمِنْ نُوحٍ وَإِبْراهِيمَ وَمُوسى وَعِيسَى ابْنِ مَرْيَمَ وَأَخَذْنا مِنْهُمْ مِيثاقاً غَلِيظاً — "When We took a pledge from the prophets, and from you and from Noah, Abraham, Moses and Jesus son of Mary, We took from them a solemn pledge" (33:7). And He said: شَرَعَ لَكُمْ مِنَ الدِّينِ ما وَصَّى بِهِ نُوحاً وَالَّذِي أَوْحَيْنا إِلَيْكَ وَما وَصَّيْنا بِهِ إِبْراهِيمَ وَمُوسى وَعِيسى — "He has prescribed for you the religion which He had enjoined upon Noah and which We have revealed to you, and which We had enjoined upon Abraham, Moses and Jesus" (42:13).

Allah, the Glorified, bestowed His favor upon him and his brother in His saying: وَلَقَدْ مَنَنَّا عَلى مُوسى وَهارُونَ — "Certainly We certainly favoured Moses and Aaron" (37:114), and He sent peace upon them in His saying: سَلامٌ عَلى مُوسى وَهارُونَ — "Peace be to Moses and Aaron!" (37:120).

He praised Musa (peace be upon him) with the most beautiful praise in His saying: وَاذْكُرْ فِي الْكِتابِ مُوسى إِنَّهُ كانَ مُخْلَصاً وَكانَ رَسُولاً نَبِيًّا وَنادَيْناهُ مِنْ جانِبِ الطُّورِ الْأَيْمَنِ وَقَرَّبْناهُ نَجِيًّا — "And mention in the Book Moses. Indeed he was dedicated, and he was an apostle and a prophet. We called him from the right side of the Mount and We brought him near for a confidential discourse" (19:51-52). And He said: وَكانَ عِنْدَ اللهِ وَجِيهاً — "and he was distinguished in the sight of Allah" (33:69). And He said: وَكَلَّمَ اللهُ مُوسى تَكْلِيماً — "and Allah spoke to Moses directly" (4:164).

He mentioned him among those prophets He mentioned in Surah al-An‘am (verses 84-88), informing that they were virtuous and righteous, that He preferred them over all the worlds, chose them, and guided them to a straight path (الصراط المستقيم) — the direct and unerring way to Allah. He mentioned him among the prophets in Surah Maryam, then mentioned in verse 58 thereof that they are those whom Allah has blessed.

Thus, there gathered for him (peace be upon him) the meanings of sincerity (الإخلاص), proximity (التقريب), distinction (الوجاهة), virtue (الإحسان), righteousness (الصلاح), preference (التفضيل), choice (الاجتباء), guidance (الهداية), and blessing (الإنعام). The discussion regarding the meanings of these attributes has passed in appropriate places in this book, as has the discussion regarding the meaning of prophethood (النبوة), messengership (الرسالة), and speaking (التكليم).

He mentioned the Book revealed to him, which is the Torah (التوراة), describing it as a guide and a mercy (46:12), as a criterion (الفرقان) — the decisive standard that distinguishes truth from falsehood — a light, and a reminder (21:48), and that in it is guidance and light (5:44). He said: وَكَتَبْنا لَهُ فِي الْأَلْواحِ مِنْ كُلِّ شَيْءٍ مَوْعِظَةً وَتَفْصِيلاً لِكُلِّ شَيْءٍ — "We wrote for him in the Tablets advice concerning all things and an elaboration of all things" (7:145).

However, He, the Exalted, mentioned in several places of His speech that they distorted it and differed concerning it. The story of Nebuchadnezzar and his second conquest of Palestine, his destruction of the Temple, his burning of the Torah, and his gathering of the Jews to Babylon in the year 588 BC, then the conquest of Babylon by King Cyrus in the year 538 BC and his permission for the Jews to return to Palestine again, and the writing of the Torah for them by Ezra the Priest, is well-known in histories. Reference to this has preceded in the third volume of this book in the stories of the Messiah (peace be upon him).

2 - The stories of Musa (peace be upon him) in the Quran: He (peace be upon him) is the prophet most frequently mentioned in the Noble Quran. His name has been mentioned—according to what has been counted—in 136 places of His speech, and his story is referred to summarily or in detail in 34 surahs of the Quran. He was distinguished among the prophets by the abundance of miracles. Many of his dazzling miracles are mentioned in the Quran, such as his staff becoming a serpent, the white hand, the flood, the locusts, the lice, the frogs, the blood, the parting of the sea, the sending down of manna and quails, the gushing of springs from the rock by the strike of the staff, the reviving of the dead, the raising of the Mount over the people, and others.

A portion of his stories (peace be upon him) has appeared in His speech without exhausting every minor and major detail, but rather by restricting to chapters whose mention is important for the purpose of guidance and instruction, as is the habit of the Quran in referring to the stories of the prophets and their nations.

These chapters, which contain the generalities of his stories, are: that he was born in Egypt in an Israeli house when they were slaughtering the male newborns of the Children of Israel by order of Pharaoh; his mother placed him in a chest and cast him into the sea; Pharaoh took him, then returned him to his mother for nursing and upbringing; and he grew up in the house of Pharaoh.

Then he reached his maturity, killed the Copt, and fled from Egypt to Madyan out of fear that Pharaoh and his elite would kill him in retaliation.

Then he remained in Madyan with the prophet Shu‘ayb (peace be upon him) and married one of his two daughters.

Then, when Musa completed the term and set out with his family, he descried a fire on the side of the Mount. They had lost their way on a wintry night, so he made them stop at their place and went to the fire to bring them a brand or find guidance at the fire. When he came to it, Allah called him from the right bank of the valley in the blessed site, from the tree, spoke to him, chose him, and gave him the miracle of the staff and the white hand among nine signs. He chose him for the message to Pharaoh and his elite and for the delivery of the Children of Israel, and commanded him to go to him.

So he came to Pharaoh and invited him to the word of truth and to send the Children of Israel with him and not to punish them. He showed him the sign of the staff and the white hand, but he refused and countered him with the magic of the sorcerers. They brought a great magic of pythons and serpents, so he threw his staff and behold, it swallowed up what they had faked. The sorcerers were cast down prostrate; they said, "We believe in the Lord of all the worlds, the Lord of Moses and Aaron." Pharaoh persisted in his denial, threatened the sorcerers, and did not believe.

Musa (peace be upon him) continued to invite him and his elite and show them sign after sign, such as the flood, the locusts, the lice, the frogs, and the blood—distinct signs—while they persisted in their arrogance. Whenever the plague befell them, they said: "O Moses! Pray to your Lord for us by the covenant He has made with you. If you remove the plague from us, we will surely believe in you and we will surely send the Children of Israel with you." But when Allah removed the plague from them until a term they were to reach, behold, they would break their word.

Then Allah commanded him to take the Children of Israel by night, so they set out until they reached the shore of the sea. Pharaoh followed them with his hosts. When the two groups saw each other, the companions of Musa said, "We are indeed overtaken." He said, "No! Indeed my Lord is with me; He will guide me." He was commanded to strike the sea with his staff, so the water parted and they crossed the sea. Pharaoh and his hosts followed them until, when they were all caught within it, Allah closed the water upon them and drowned them to the last man.

When Allah delivered them from Pharaoh and his hosts and brought them out to the wilderness where there was neither water nor pasture, Allah honored them and sent down upon them manna and quails. He commanded Musa, who struck the rock with his staff, and twelve springs gushed from it; every people knew their drinking place. They drank from them and ate from those two, and the clouds shaded them.

Then Allah made a covenant with Musa for forty nights for the revelation of the Torah at Mount Sinai. He chose seventy men from his people to hear His speaking to him. They heard, then said: "We will not believe you until we see Allah openly." So the thunderbolt took them while they were looking. Then Allah revived them by the prayer of Musa. When the appointed time was completed, Allah revealed the Torah to him and informed him that the Samiri had misguided his people after him, and they had worshipped the calf.

Musa returned to his people, angry and sorrowful. He burnt the calf and scattered it into the sea, and expelled the Samiri, saying to him: "Go! It shall be your [lot] in this life to say, ‘Touch me not!’" As for the people, they were commanded to repent and kill themselves, and they were forgiven after that. Then they were too arrogant to accept the law of the Torah until Allah raised the Mount over them.

Then they grew weary of the manna and quails and said, "We will not endure one [kind of] food," and they asked him to pray to his Lord to bring forth for them of what the earth grows—its herbs, its cucumbers, its garlic, its lentils, and its onions. They were commanded to enter the Holy Land which Allah had ordained for them, but they refused. So Allah forbade it to them and afflicted them with the Wilderness (التيه), wandering in the land for forty years.

Among the stories of Musa (peace be upon him) is what Allah mentioned in Surah al-Kahf regarding his journey with his young man to the junction of the two seas to meet the righteous servant and his companionship with him until he parted from him.

3 - The station of Harun (peace be upon him) before Allah and his state of servitude: Allah the Exalted associated him with Musa (peace be upon him) in Surah al-Saffat regarding the favor, the bestowing of the Book, the guidance to the straight path, and the salutation, and that he is among the virtuous and among His faithful servants (37:114-122). He counted him as a messenger (20:47) and a prophet (19:53), and that he is among those whom He has blessed (19:58). He associated him with those prophets He enumerated in Surah al-An‘am in their beautiful attributes of virtue, righteousness, preference, choice, and guidance (6:84-88).

In the prayer of Musa on the night of the Mount: وَاجْعَلْ لِي وَزِيراً مِنْ أَهْلِي هارُونَ أَخِي اشْدُدْ بِهِ أَزْرِي وَأَشْرِكْهُ فِي أَمْرِي كَيْ نُسَبِّحَكَ كَثِيراً وَنَذْكُرَكَ كَثِيراً إِنَّكَ كُنْتَ بِنا بَصِيراً — "Appoint for me a minister from my family, Aaron, my brother. Strengthen my back by him, and make him a partner in my affair, so that we may glorify You much, and remember You much. Indeed You are watchful of us" (20:29-35).

He (peace be upon him) was a constant companion to his brother in all his stations, sharing in his general affairs and assisting him in all his objectives.

Nothing has appeared in the Noble Quran specifically regarding his stories except for his succession to his brother when he was absent from the people for the appointed time, and he said to his brother Harun: "Succeed me among my people, and set things right, and do not follow the way of the agents of corruption." When Musa returned to his people, angry and sorrowful, and they had worshipped the calf, he threw down the Tablets and seized his brother by the head, pulling him toward himself. He said: "O son of my mother! Indeed the people thought me weak and they almost killed me. So do not let the enemies gloat over me, and do not count me among the wrongdoing people." He said: "My Lord, forgive me and my brother, and admit us into Your mercy, for You are the most merciful of the merciful."

4 - The story of Musa (peace be upon him) in the current Torah: His stories (peace be upon him) are placed in the four books of the Torah following the first book, namely: Exodus, Leviticus, Numbers, and Deuteronomy. In them are mentioned the details of his stories (peace be upon him) from the time of his birth until the time of his death, and the laws and judgments revealed to him.

However, there are discrepancies in the narration of the story compared to the Quran in significant matters.

Among the most important is that it mentions that the calling of Musa and his being spoken to from the tree was in the land of Madyan before he set out with his family. This was while he was tending the flock of Jethro, his father-in-law, the priest of Midian. He led the flock to the back of the wilderness and came to Horeb, the mountain of God. The angel of the Lord appeared to him in a flame of fire from the midst of a bush, and God called him and spoke to him what He spoke, and sent him to Pharaoh to deliver the Children of Israel.

Among them is what it mentioned: that the Pharaoh to whom Musa was sent was other than the Pharaoh who took Musa and raised him, then Musa fled from him when he killed the Copt out of fear of retaliation. Among them is that it did not mention the faith of the sorcerers when they threw their staffs and they became serpents and Musa's staff swallowed them. Rather, it mentions that they were with Pharaoh and opposed Musa regarding the signs of blood and frogs, bringing by their magic the like of what Musa (peace be upon him) brought as a miracle.

Among them is that it mentions that the one who made the calf for them, which they then worshipped, was the prophet Harun, the brother of Musa (peace be upon him). This was because when the people saw that Musa delayed in descending from the mountain, the people gathered around Harun and said to him: "Arise, make us gods who shall go before us; for as for this Moses, the man who brought us up out of the land of Egypt, we do not know what has become of him." So Harun said to them: "Break off the golden earrings which are in the ears of your wives, your sons, and your daughters, and bring them to me."

So all the people broke off the golden earrings which were in their ears and brought them to Harun. He took them from their hands and fashioned it with a tool, and made it a molded calf. Then they said: "These are your gods, O Israel, who brought you up out of the land of Egypt."

In the Quranic verses, there are allusions to the Torah in these places of his stories (peace be upon him) that are not hidden from the one who reflects upon them.

There are also many minor discrepancies, such as what occurred in the Torah in the story of the killing of the Copt: that the two who were fighting the second time were both Israelites.

Also, it occurred therein that the one who threw the staff which then swallowed the serpents of the sorcerers was Harun; he threw it by the command of Musa.

Also, the story of the selection of the seventy men for the appointed time, the descent of the thunderbolt upon them, and their revival after it is not mentioned therein.

Also, it contains that the Tablets that were with Musa when he descended from the mountain and threw them were two tablets of stone, and they were the Tablets of the Testimony. This is in addition to other discrepancies.

Ayahs

وَلَقَدْ ءَاتَيْنَا مُوسَى ٱلْكِتَـٰبَ مِنۢ بَعْدِ مَآ أَهْلَكْنَا ٱلْقُرُونَ ٱلْأُولَىٰ بَصَآئِرَ لِلنَّاسِ وَهُدًى وَرَحْمَةً لَّعَلَّهُمْ يَتَذَكَّرُونَ (٤٣)
Certainly We gave Moses the Book, after We had destroyed the former generations, as [a set of] eye-openers, guidance and mercy for mankind, so that they may take admonition. (43)
وَمَا كُنتَ بِجَانِبِ ٱلْغَرْبِىِّ إِذْ قَضَيْنَآ إِلَىٰ مُوسَى ٱلْأَمْرَ وَمَا كُنتَ مِنَ ٱلشَّـٰهِدِينَ (٤٤)
You were not on the western side when We revealed the commandments to Moses, nor were you among the witnesses. (44)
وَلَـٰكِنَّآ أَنشَأْنَا قُرُونًا فَتَطَاوَلَ عَلَيْهِمُ ٱلْعُمُرُ ۚ وَمَا كُنتَ ثَاوِيًا فِىٓ أَهْلِ مَدْيَنَ تَتْلُوا۟ عَلَيْهِمْ ءَايَـٰتِنَا وَلَـٰكِنَّا كُنَّا مُرْسِلِينَ (٤٥)
But We brought forth other generations and time took its toll on them. You did not dwell among the people of Midian reciting to them Our signs, but it is We who are the senders [of the apostles]. (45)
وَمَا كُنتَ بِجَانِبِ ٱلطُّورِ إِذْ نَادَيْنَا وَلَـٰكِن رَّحْمَةً مِّن رَّبِّكَ لِتُنذِرَ قَوْمًا مَّآ أَتَىٰهُم مِّن نَّذِيرٍ مِّن قَبْلِكَ لَعَلَّهُمْ يَتَذَكَّرُونَ (٤٦)
And you were not on the side of the Mount when We called out [to Moses], but [We have sent you as] a mercy from your Lord that you may warn a people to whom there did not come any warner before you, so that they may take admonition. (46)
وَلَوْلَآ أَن تُصِيبَهُم مُّصِيبَةٌۢ بِمَا قَدَّمَتْ أَيْدِيهِمْ فَيَقُولُوا۟ رَبَّنَا لَوْلَآ أَرْسَلْتَ إِلَيْنَا رَسُولًا فَنَتَّبِعَ ءَايَـٰتِكَ وَنَكُونَ مِنَ ٱلْمُؤْمِنِينَ (٤٧)
And lest—if an affliction were to befall them because of what their hands have sent ahead —they should say, ‘Our Lord! Why did You not send us an apostle so that we might have followed Your signs and been among the faithful?’ (47)
فَلَمَّا جَآءَهُمُ ٱلْحَقُّ مِنْ عِندِنَا قَالُوا۟ لَوْلَآ أُوتِىَ مِثْلَ مَآ أُوتِىَ مُوسَىٰٓ ۚ أَوَلَمْ يَكْفُرُوا۟ بِمَآ أُوتِىَ مُوسَىٰ مِن قَبْلُ ۖ قَالُوا۟ سِحْرَانِ تَظَـٰهَرَا وَقَالُوٓا۟ إِنَّا بِكُلٍّ كَـٰفِرُونَ (٤٨)
But when there came to them the truth from Us, they said, ‘Why has he not been given the like of what Moses was given?’ Did they not disbelieve what Moses was given before, and said, ‘Two magicians abetting each other,’ and said, ‘Indeed we disbelieve both of them’? (48)
قُلْ فَأْتُوا۟ بِكِتَـٰبٍ مِّنْ عِندِ ٱللَّهِ هُوَ أَهْدَىٰ مِنْهُمَآ أَتَّبِعْهُ إِن كُنتُمْ صَـٰدِقِينَ (٤٩)
Say, ‘If you are truthful, bring some Book from Allah better in guidance than the two on them so that I may follow it.’ (49)
فَإِن لَّمْ يَسْتَجِيبُوا۟ لَكَ فَٱعْلَمْ أَنَّمَا يَتَّبِعُونَ أَهْوَآءَهُمْ ۚ وَمَنْ أَضَلُّ مِمَّنِ ٱتَّبَعَ هَوَىٰهُ بِغَيْرِ هُدًى مِّنَ ٱللَّهِ ۚ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ لَا يَهْدِى ٱلْقَوْمَ ٱلظَّـٰلِمِينَ (٥٠)
Then if they do not respond to you[r] [summons], know that they only follow their desires, and who is more astray than him who follows his desires without any guidance from Allah? Indeed Allah does not guide the wrongdoing lot. (50)
۞ وَلَقَدْ وَصَّلْنَا لَهُمُ ٱلْقَوْلَ لَعَلَّهُمْ يَتَذَكَّرُونَ (٥١)
Certainly We have carried on this discourse for them so that they may take admonition. (51)
ٱلَّذِينَ ءَاتَيْنَـٰهُمُ ٱلْكِتَـٰبَ مِن قَبْلِهِۦ هُم بِهِۦ يُؤْمِنُونَ (٥٢)
Those to whom We gave the Book before it are the ones who believe in it, (52)
وَإِذَا يُتْلَىٰ عَلَيْهِمْ قَالُوٓا۟ ءَامَنَّا بِهِۦٓ إِنَّهُ ٱلْحَقُّ مِن رَّبِّنَآ إِنَّا كُنَّا مِن قَبْلِهِۦ مُسْلِمِينَ (٥٣)
and when it is recited to them, they say, ‘We believe in it. It is indeed the truth from our Lord. Indeed we were Muslims [even] before it [came].’ (53)
أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ يُؤْتَوْنَ أَجْرَهُم مَّرَّتَيْنِ بِمَا صَبَرُوا۟ وَيَدْرَءُونَ بِٱلْحَسَنَةِ ٱلسَّيِّئَةَ وَمِمَّا رَزَقْنَـٰهُمْ يُنفِقُونَ (٥٤)
Those will be given their reward two times for their patience. They repel evil [conduct] with good, and spend out of what We have provided them, (54)
وَإِذَا سَمِعُوا۟ ٱللَّغْوَ أَعْرَضُوا۟ عَنْهُ وَقَالُوا۟ لَنَآ أَعْمَـٰلُنَا وَلَكُمْ أَعْمَـٰلُكُمْ سَلَـٰمٌ عَلَيْكُمْ لَا نَبْتَغِى ٱلْجَـٰهِلِينَ (٥٥)
and when they hear vain talk, they avoid it and say, ‘Our deeds belong to us, and your deeds belong to you. Peace be to you. We do not court the ignorant.’ (55)
إِنَّكَ لَا تَهْدِى مَنْ أَحْبَبْتَ وَلَـٰكِنَّ ٱللَّهَ يَهْدِى مَن يَشَآءُ ۚ وَهُوَ أَعْلَمُ بِٱلْمُهْتَدِينَ (٥٦)
You cannot guide whomever you wish, but [it is] Allah [who] guides whomever He wishes, and He knows best those who are guided. (56)

(Commentary)

The context of the verses testifies that the polytheists (المشركون) among the people of the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) consulted some of the People of the Book (أهل الكتاب) and sought their formal legal opinion (استفتوهم) regarding his affair (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله). They presented to them some of the Quran revealed to him, which confirms the Torah (التوراة). The People of the Book responded by confirming its truth and believing in the true knowledge (المعارف الحقة) contained within the Quran, stating that they had known him by his descriptions before he was commissioned as a prophet, as the Exalted says: وَإِذا يُتْلى عَلَيْهِمْ قالُوا آمَنَّا بِهِ إِنَّهُ الْحَقُّ مِنْ رَبِّنا إِنَّا كُنَّا مِنْ قَبْلِهِ مُسْلِمِينَ — "When it is recited to them, they say, ‘We believe in it. It is indeed the truth from our Lord. Indeed we were Muslims even before it.’" (28:53).

This displeased the polytheists, who then quarreled with them and spoke to them harshly, saying that the Quran is sorcery and the Torah is sorcery like it: سِحْرانِ تَظاهَرا — "two sorceries backing each other" (28:48) and إِنَّا بِكُلٍّ كافِرُونَ — "Indeed we disbelieve in both" (28:48). Consequently, the People of the Book turned away from them and said: سَلامٌ عَلَيْكُمْ لا نَبْتَغِي الْجاهِلِينَ — "Peace be to you! We do not seek the ignorant" (28:55).

This is what the noble verses suggest through their context. When He, the Glorified, recounted the story of Moses (عليه‌السلام) and informed how He made a people who were oppressed (مستضعفين), enslaved, and tormented—whose sons were slaughtered and whose women were spared—prevail over a people who were high-handed, arrogant (مستكبرين), tyrannical, and corrupt. This was achieved through a newborn among them whom He raised in the lap of his enemy, who by his command slaughtered thousands of their sons. Then He brought him out from among them when he grew up, then commissioned him and returned him to them, granting him victory over them until He drowned them all and saved the tribe of Israel, making them the inheritors.

He then turned the discourse toward the Heavenly Book (الكتاب السماوي), which contains the call (الدعوة), through which the proof (الحجة) is completed, and which carries the reminder (التذكرة). He mentioned that He revealed the Torah to Moses (عليه‌السلام) containing insights (بصائر) for the people, guidance (الهدى) — the divine leading toward truth and the straight path — and mercy (الرحمة), so that they might take admonition and desist from disobeying Allah after He had destroyed the earlier generations (القرون الأولى) due to their sins.

Similarly, He revealed the Quran to the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) and recounted to him the stories of Moses (عليه‌السلام), though he was not a witness to the revelation of the Torah to him, nor was he present at the Mount (الطور) when He called him and spoke to him. He also recounted what transpired between Moses and Shu‘ayb (عليه‌السلام), though he was not a dweller (ثاوياً) in Midian (مدين) reciting His signs (آياته) to them. Rather, He revealed it and recounted what He recounted as a mercy (رحمة) from Him, so that he might warn a people to whom no warner (نذير) had come before him, because they—due to their disbelief (كفر) and transgression (فسوق)—were liable to the descent of punishment and the strike of calamity. Had He not revealed the Book and conveyed the call, they would have said: "Our Lord, why did You not send us a messenger so that we might follow Your signs?" and thus they would have had a proof (الحجة) against Allah, the Glorified.

When the Truth (الحق) came to them from Him with the commissioning of the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) and the revelation of the Quran, they said: لَوْ لا أُوتِيَ مِثْلَ ما أُوتِيَ مُوسى أَوَلَمْ يَكْفُرُوا بِما أُوتِيَ مُوسى مِنْ قَبْلُ — "‘Why has he not been given the like of what Moses was given?’ Did they not disbelieve in what Moses was given before?" (28:48), referring to when they consulted the People of the Book regarding his affair and they confirmed him. Then the polytheists said: سِحْرانِ تَظاهَرا — "two sorceries backing each other," meaning the Torah and the Quran, and they said: إِنَّا بِكُلٍّ كافِرُونَ — "Indeed we disbelieve in both."

Then He, the Glorified, prompted His Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) with the proof against them by His saying: قُلْ فَأْتُوا بِكِتابٍ مِنْ عِنْدِ اللهِ هُوَ أَهْدى مِنْهُما أَتَّبِعْهُ إِنْ كُنْتُمْ صادِقِينَ — "Say, ‘Then bring a book from Allah that is better in guidance than these two, so that I may follow it, if you are truthful.’" (28:49). This means that it is necessary according to the wisdom (حكمة) of Allah that there be a revealed Book from Allah that guides to the truth and completes the proof over the people, and they recognize it. If the Torah and the Quran were not books of guidance and sufficient for guiding the people, then there would be a book better in guidance than them—but that is not the case, as the true knowledge (المعارف الحقة) in the two books is supported by miracles (الإعجاز) and the indication of rational proofs (البراهين العقلية). Furthermore, there is no Heavenly Book better in guidance than them. Thus, the two books are books of guidance, and the people, in turning away from them, are followers of whim (الهوى), straying from the straight path (الصراط المستقيم) — the direct and unerring way to Allah. This is His saying: فإِنْ لَمْ يَسْتَجِيبُوا لَكَ فَاعْلَمْ أَنَّما يَتَّبِعُونَ أَهْواءَهُمْ — "Then if they do not respond to you, know that they only follow their desires..." (28:50).

Then He, the Glorified, praised a group from the People of the Book whom the polytheists had consulted regarding the affair of the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) and the Quran; they manifested faith (الإيمان) and confirmation (التصديق) to them and turned away from the vain talk (لغو القول) with which they were confronted.

His saying, the Exalted: وَلَقَدْ آتَيْنا مُوسَى الْكِتابَ مِنْ بَعْدِ ما أَهْلَكْنَا الْقُرُونَ الْأُولى بَصائِرَ لِلنَّاسِ — "Certainly We gave Moses the Book, after We had destroyed the former generations, as insights for mankind..." (28:43). The lam is for an oath, meaning: I swear, We gave Moses the Book, which is the Torah, through His revelation to him.

His saying: مِنْ بَعْدِ ما أَهْلَكْنَا الْقُرُونَ الْأُولى — "after We had destroyed the former generations," refers to the generations prior to the revelation of the Torah, such as the people of Noah and the destroyed nations after them, and perhaps among them were the people of Pharaoh. In this qualification is an indication of the dire need at that time for the revelation of the Book, due to the effacement of the landmarks of the divine religion (الدين الإلهي) with the passing of those who went before, and so that their stories and the descent of divine punishment upon them due to their denial of Allah's signs might be mentioned in the Divine Book, so that those who take heed might be cautioned and those who remember might be reminded.

His saying: بَصائِرَ لِلنَّاسِ — "as insights for mankind." Basa'ir (بصائر) is the plural of basirah (بصيرة), meaning that by which one sees. What is meant by it here are the clear proofs (الحجج البينة) by which the truth is seen and by which it is distinguished from falsehood. It is a circumstantial qualifier (حال) for "the Book," though it has been said it is an object of purpose (مفعول له).

His saying: وَهُدىً — "and guidance," meaning the guide or that by which one is guided. Likewise, His saying: وَرَحْمَةً — "and mercy," meaning that by which mercy is shown. Both are circumstantial qualifiers for "the Book" like "insights," though it has been said each is an object of purpose.

The meaning is: I swear, We gave Moses the Book, which is the Torah, after We had destroyed the first generations, for wisdom necessitated the renewal of the call and the warning, while the Book consists of clear proofs by which people see the true knowledge, a guidance by which they are guided to it, and a mercy by which they are shown mercy due to acting upon its laws (شرائعه) and rulings (أحكامه), so that they might take admonition and thus understand what is required of them in terms of belief (الاعتقاد) and action (العمل).

His saying, the Exalted: وَما كُنْتَ بِجانِبِ الْغَرْبِيِّ إِذْ قَضَيْنا إِلى مُوسَى الْأَمْرَ وَما كُنْتَ مِنَ الشَّاهِدِينَ — "You were not on the western side when We concluded the matter with Moses, and you were not among the witnesses." (28:44). The address is to the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله). "The western" (الغربي) is an adjective whose noun is omitted; what is meant is the western side of the valley or the western side of the mountain.

His saying: إِذْ قَضَيْنا إِلى مُوسَى الْأَمْرَ — "when We concluded the matter with Moses." The "concluding" (القضاء) here implies the meaning of a covenant (العهد). What is meant by the covenant of the matter to him—according to what has been said—is the firm establishment of the matter of his prophethood (نبوته) by revealing the Torah to him. As for the covenant to him regarding the essence of the message (الرسالة), it is indicated by His later saying: وَما كُنْتَ بِجانِبِ الطُّورِ إِذْ نادَيْنا — "And you were not on the side of the Mount when We called out" (28:46). His saying: وَما كُنْتَ مِنَ الشَّاهِدِينَ — "and you were not among the witnesses" is an emphasis of the preceding statement.

The meaning is: You were not present and witnessing when We revealed the Torah to Moses on the western side of the valley or the mountain.

His saying, the Exalted: وَلكِنَّا أَنْشَأْنا قُرُوناً فَتَطاوَلَ عَلَيْهِمُ الْعُمُرُ — "But We brought forth [other] generations and their lives were prolonged." (28:45). The "prolonging of life" (تطاول العمر) is the extension of the span of time. The sentence is an emendation (استدراك) of the negation in His saying: وَما كُنْتَ بِجانِبِ الْغَرْبِيِ. The meaning is: You were not present there witnessing what transpired, but We brought into existence generations after him and the span of time was extended for them, then We revealed to you his story and the news of the revelation of the Book to him. Thus, there is a brevity by omission (إيجاز بالحذف) in the speech, as the context indicates it.

His saying, the Exalted: وَما كُنْتَ ثاوِياً فِي أَهْلِ مَدْيَنَ تَتْلُوا عَلَيْهِمْ آياتِنا وَلكِنَّا كُنَّا مُرْسِلِينَ — "And you were not a dweller among the people of Midian, reciting to them Our signs, but We have been sending [messengers]." (28:45). The "dweller" (الثاوي) is the resident; it is said: "he dwelt (ثوى) in the place" when he resided in it. The pronoun in "to them" (عَلَيْهِمُ) refers to the polytheists of Mecca to whom the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) was reciting the signs of Allah that recount what happened to Moses (عليه‌السلام) in Midian during the time he was there.

His saying: وَلكِنَّا كُنَّا مُرْسِلِينَ — "but We have been sending [messengers]" is an emendation of the negation at the beginning of the verse.

The meaning is: You were not residing among the people of Midian—who were Shu‘ayb and his people—witnessing what happened to Moses there, such that you would recite to the polytheists Our signs recounting his news there; rather, We were sending you to your people, revealing these signs to you so that you might recite them to them.

His saying, the Exalted: وَما كُنْتَ بِجانِبِ الطُّورِ إِذْ نادَيْنا وَلكِنْ رَحْمَةً مِنْ رَبِّكَ — "And you were not on the side of the Mount when We called out, but [it is] a mercy from your Lord..." (28:46) to the end of the verse. It appears from the juxtaposition of this verse with His previous saying: وَما كُنْتَ بِجانِبِ الْغَرْبِيِّ إِذْ قَضَيْنا etc., that what is meant by this "calling" (النداء) is what occurred from the tree on the night he perceived a fire from the side of the Mount.

His saying: وَلكِنْ رَحْمَةً مِنْ رَبِّكَ — "but [it is] a mercy from your Lord" is an emendation of the previous negation. It appears that "mercy" (رَحْمَةً) is an object of purpose. The shift (الالتفات) from the first-person plural to the third person in His saying: مِنْ رَبِّكَ — "from your Lord" is to indicate the perfection of His care, the Exalted, for him (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله).

His saying: لِتُنْذِرَ قَوْماً ما أَتاهُمْ مِنْ نَذِيرٍ مِنْ قَبْلِكَ — "so that you may warn a people to whom no warner had come before you" (28:46). It appears that what is meant by this "people" (قَوْماً) are the people of the era of the prophetic call, or they and their immediate ancestors, for the Arabs had messengers among them in the past, such as Hud, Salih, Shu‘ayb, and Ishmael (عليه‌السلام).

The meaning is: You were not present on the side of the Mount when We called Moses, spoke to him, and chose him for the message, such that you would report this story as a present witness; rather, as a mercy from Us, We informed you of it so that you might warn a people to whom no warner had come before you, that they might take admonition.

His saying, the Exalted: وَلَوْ لا أَنْ تُصِيبَهُمْ مُصِيبَةٌ بِما قَدَّمَتْ أَيْدِيهِمْ فَيَقُولُوا رَبَّنا — "Lest—were a calamity to strike them for what their hands have sent ahead, and they should say, ‘Our Lord...’" (28:47). What is meant by "what their hands have sent ahead" (بما قدمت أيديهم) are the evil deeds they earned through belief and action, as evidenced by the end of the verse. What is meant by the "calamity" (المصيبة) that strikes them is more general than the calamities of this world and the Hereafter, for turning away from the truth through disbelief and transgression entails divine reckoning in this world just as it entails it in the Hereafter. Some discussion on this has preceded under His saying: وَلَوْ أَنَّ أَهْلَ الْقُرى آمَنُوا وَاتَّقَوْا لَفَتَحْنا عَلَيْهِمْ بَرَكاتٍ مِنَ السَّماءِ وَالْأَرْضِ — "If the people of the towns had believed and been God-conscious, We would have opened to them blessings from the heaven and the earth" (7:96) and elsewhere.

His saying: فَيَقُولُوا رَبَّنا لَوْ لا أَرْسَلْتَ — "and they should say, ‘Our Lord, why did You not send...’" is branched from what preceded it on the assumption of not sending a messenger. The apodosis (جواب) of lawla (لو لا) is omitted because it is obvious; the estimation is: "We would not have sent a messenger."

The sum of the meaning is: Were it not that they would have a proof against Us on the assumption of not sending a messenger and their being seized by punishment for what their hands sent ahead of disbelief and transgression, We would not have sent a messenger to them. But they would say: "Our Lord, why did You not send a messenger to us so that we might follow Your signs which he recites to us and be among the believers?"

His saying, the Exalted: فَلَمَّا جاءَهُمُ الْحَقُّ مِنْ عِنْدِنا قالُوا لَوْ لا أُوتِيَ مِثْلَ ما أُوتِيَ مُوسى — "But when the truth came to them from Us, they said, ‘Why has he not been given the like of what Moses was given?’" (28:48). That is, We sent the Messenger to them with the truth and revealed the Book. When the truth came to them from Us—and it appears that it is the Book revealed to the Messenger, which is the Quran revealed to the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله).

What is meant by their saying: لَوْ لا أُوتِيَ مِثْلَ ما أُوتِيَ مُوسى is: "Why was the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) not given the like of the Torah that was given to Moses (عليه‌السلام)?" It is as if they wanted the Quran to be revealed all at once, as Allah the Exalted recounted from them in His saying: وَقالَ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا لَوْ لا نُزِّلَ عَلَيْهِ الْقُرْآنُ جُمْلَةً واحِدَةً — "The faithless say, ‘Why has the Quran not been sent down to him all at once?’" (25:32).

Allah answered their statement with His saying: أَوَلَمْ يَكْفُرُوا بِما أُوتِيَ مُوسى مِنْ قَبْلُ قالُوا سِحْرانِ تَظاهَرا — "Did they not disbelieve in what Moses was given before? They said, ‘Two sorceries backing each other,’" meaning the Quran and the Torah, وَقالُوا إِنَّا بِكُلٍّ كافِرُونَ — "and they said, ‘Indeed we disbelieve in both.’" The difference between the two statements is that the first is disbelief in the two books, while the second is disbelief in the essence of prophethood; perhaps this is the reason for the repetition of "they said" (قالُوا) in the speech.

His saying, the Exalted: قُلْ فَأْتُوا بِكِتابٍ مِنْ عِنْدِ اللهِ هُوَ أَهْدى مِنْهُما أَتَّبِعْهُ إِنْ كُنْتُمْ صادِقِينَ — "Say, ‘Then bring a book from Allah that is better in guidance than these two, so that I may follow it, if you are truthful.’" (28:49). This is branched from the fact that the Quran and the Torah were "two sorceries backing each other." This branching is only valid if it is necessary that there be a Book from Allah, the Glorified, among the people to guide them and which they must follow. If the two were false sorceries, then the truth would be something else. This is indeed the case, as clarified by His saying: وَلَوْ لا أَنْ تُصِيبَهُمْ مُصِيبَةٌ etc., that the people have a right upon Allah that He reveal the Book to them and send the Messenger to them. Therefore, Allah commanded His Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) to demand from them a book other than these two that is better in guidance than them, so that he might follow it.

Furthermore, if the two books were "two sorceries backing each other," they would be false and misleading, containing no guidance such that another book they might bring could be "better in guidance" than them—since the superlative form (أهدى) necessitates that both the preferred and the one it is preferred over share the base attribute. However, since the context is one of argumentation (المحاجة), he claimed that the two books are guides and there is nothing superior to them in guidance. If the opponent does not accept that, then let him bring a book that exceeds them in the meaning of the exposition of reality they contain, such that it would be "better in guidance" than them.

Although the noble Quran explicitly states the infiltration of alteration (التحريف) and defect into the present Torah, which does not suit its being counted as a book of guidance in an absolute sense, the discussion here concerns the actual Torah revealed to Moses (عليه‌السلام), which is what the Quran confirms.

Moreover, the subject of the discourse is both of them together, and the Quran rectifies the present Torah by clarifying the defects within it; thus, together they are guidance, and there is no book better in guidance than them.

His saying: إِنْ كُنْتُمْ صادِقِينَ — "if you are truthful," meaning in the claim that they are "two sorceries backing each other."

His saying, the Exalted: فَإِنْ لَمْ يَسْتَجِيبُوا لَكَ فَاعْلَمْ أَنَّما يَتَّبِعُونَ أَهْواءَهُمْ — "Then if they do not respond to you, know that they only follow their desires..." (28:50). Istijabah (الاستجابة) and ijabah (الإجابة) have the same meaning. It is said in al-Kashshaf: This verb is transitive to the "call" (الدعاء) directly, and to the "caller" (الداعي) with the lam. The "call" is usually omitted when it is made transitive to the "caller"; thus it is said: "Allah responded to his call" (استجاب الله دعاءه) or "He responded to him" (استجاب له), and it is rarely said: "He responded to him his call" (استجاب له دعاءه). [End quote].

So His saying: فَإِنْ لَمْ يَسْتَجِيبُوا لَكَ is branched from His saying: قُلْ فَأْتُوا بِكِتابٍ مِنْ عِنْدِ اللهِ هُوَ أَهْدى مِنْهُما أَتَّبِعْهُ. That is: if you say this to them and charge them with that, and they do not bring a book that is better in guidance than the Quran and the Torah, and it becomes certain that there is no guidance more complete and perfect than their guidance, yet they still label them as sorcery and turn away from them, then know that they are not seeking the truth nor are they in the process of following what is a clear proof of reason (حجة العقل). Rather, they only follow their whims (أهواءهم) and defend the desires of their natures with such falsehoods as: "two sorceries backing each other" and "Indeed we disbelieve in both."

It is possible that what is meant by His saying: أَنَّما يَتَّبِعُونَ أَهْواءَهُمْ is that if they do not bring a book better in guidance than them while they are non-believers in them, then know that they only build the way of life (سنة الحياة) upon following whims. They do not believe in the essence of prophethood (أصل النبوة) nor that Allah has a Heavenly religion revealed to them through revelation (الوحي) which they must follow and by which they must traverse the path of life through the guidance of their Lord. This meaning is perhaps supported by His later saying: وَمَنْ أَضَلُّ مِمَّنِ اتَّبَعَ هَواهُ بِغَيْرِ هُدىً مِنَ اللهِ — "And who is more astray than him who follows his desires without any guidance from Allah?" (28:50).

His saying: وَمَنْ أَضَلُّ مِمَّنِ اتَّبَعَ هَواهُ بِغَيْرِ هُدىً مِنَ اللهِ is a rhetorical question (استفهام إنكاري), and what is meant by it is the conclusion that they are astray (ضالون). His saying: إِنَّ اللهَ لا يَهْدِي الْقَوْمَ الظَّالِمِينَ — "Indeed Allah does not guide the wrongdoing people" (28:50) is the justification for their being astray by following whim, for following whim is a turning away from the truth and a deviation from the path of rectitude (صراط الرشد), and that is wrongdoing (ظلم). Allah does not guide the wrongdoing people (القوم الظالمين), and the one who is not guided is astray.

The summary of the proof is that if they do not bring a book better in guidance than them and they are not believers in them, then they are followers of whim; the follower of whim is a wrongdoer, the wrongdoer is not guided, and the one not guided is astray; therefore, they are astray.

His saying, the Exalted: وَلَقَدْ وَصَّلْنا لَهُمُ الْقَوْلَ لَعَلَّهُمْ يَتَّذَكَّرُونَ — "Certainly We have plyingly delivered the Word to them, so that they may take admonition." (28:51). Tawsil (التوصيل) is the taf‘il form of wasl (الوصل), which denotes frequency or intensity (التكثير), like qat‘ and taqti‘, or qatl and taqtil. The pronoun refers to the polytheists of Mecca. The meaning is: We revealed the Quran to them connectedly, one part after another: verse after verse, and surah after surah, consisting of promise and threat, knowledge and rulings, stories and lessons, wisdom and exhortations, so that they might take admonition.

His saying, the Exalted: الَّذِينَ آتَيْناهُمُ الْكِتابَ مِنْ قَبْلِهِ هُمْ بِهِ يُؤْمِنُونَ — "Those to whom We gave the Book before it, they believe in it." (28:52). Both pronouns refer to the Quran, though it has been said they refer to the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله). The first is more in accord with the context. In this verse and those following it is a praise for a group of the believers from the People of the Book, following the previous verses' censure of the polytheists of Mecca.

The context of the end of the verses testifies that these praised ones are a specific group from the People of the Book who believed in it; thus, no weight is given to the opinion that what is meant by them is the believers among them in general.

His saying, the Exalted: وَإِذا يُتْلى عَلَيْهِمْ قالُوا آمَنَّا بِهِ إِنَّهُ الْحَقُّ مِنْ رَبِّنا — "When it is recited to them, they say, ‘We believe in it. It is indeed the truth from our Lord...’" (28:53). The singular pronouns refer to the Quran. The lam in "the Truth" (الْحقُ) is for familiarity (العهد). The meaning is: when the Quran is read to them, they say: "We believe in it; it is indeed the Truth that we recognize from our Lord, for we knew it from before."
