Just as human beings of influence and power, when they wish to punish those they dominate, ask them about their sins to convict them of the crime and then the punishment—and sometimes the criminal diverts their punishment from himself with the excuses he fabricates—Allah, the Glorified, due to His knowledge of the reality of the situation, does not ask the guilty about their sins. Rather, He passes judgment upon them, and a punishment comes to them that cannot be repelled.

Based on this, it appears that the sentence is a completion of the previous rebuke and a response to his attributing his wealth to his knowledge. The gist is that the divine reckoning is not like the reckoning of people, such that if they blame him or advise him, he can divert that from himself with the response he fabricates so as to benefit from his knowledge in that regard. Rather, He, the Glorified, is All-Knowing (عليم) and a Witness (شهيد); He does not ask the criminal about his sin, but rather He takes him to task for his sin; furthermore, He takes him to task suddenly while he is unaware.

This is what the context provides regarding the meaning of the verse. Others have different opinions:

It was said: The meaning of "knowledge" in his saying "I have indeed been given this because of the knowledge I have" is the knowledge of the Torah, for he was the most knowledgeable of the Children of Israel regarding it.

It was said: The meaning is the science of alchemy (علم الكيمياء), which he had learned from Moses, Joshua son of Nun, and Caleb son of Jephunneh. The meaning of the knowledge being "with him" is his exclusivity to it over other people, and he had produced a great amount of gold with it.

It was said: The meaning of "knowledge" is the knowledge of extracting treasures and buried hoards, and he had extracted many treasures and hoards with it.

It was said: The meaning of "knowledge" is the knowledge of Allah, the Exalted. The meaning would be: I was given it based on knowledge from Allah and a specific selection by which He intended me. The meaning of his saying "with me" (عِنْدِي) is: so it is in my thought and opinion.

It was said: The knowledge is the knowledge of Allah, but in the sense of the "known" (المعلوم). The meaning is: I was given it based on a good that Allah knew was with me. In all these opinions, the preposition "upon" (عَلى) denotes superiority (الاستعلاء), though it was also permitted to be for causation (التعليل).

It was said: The meaning of the "questioning" in His saying "The guilty will not be questioned about their sins" is the questioning on the Day of Resurrection. What is negated is the questioning for information (سؤال الاستعلام), because Allah knows their sins better and has no need to ask, and the angels know them from the records of their deeds and recognize them by their marks. As for His saying, the Exalted: "Stop them! For they must be questioned" (37:24), it is a questioning of upbraiding and rebuke (سؤال تقريع وتوبيخ), not a questioning for information. It is possible that the questioning in both verses has the same meaning, and the negation and affirmation are due to the different stations (المواقف) on the Day of Resurrection; they are questioned in one station and not in another, so there is no contradiction between the two verses.

It was said: The pronoun in His saying "about their sins" refers to those who were "mightier," and the "guilty" refers to others. The meaning would be: the other guilty ones will not be asked about the sins of those whom Allah destroyed from the previous generations.

All of these are interpretive views that do not suit the context.

His saying, the Exalted: "So he went out before his people in his finery. Those who desired the life of the world said, ‘Would that we had the like of what Qarun has been given! Indeed he is very fortunate’" (28:79). Fortune (الحظ) is the portion of happiness and luck.

His saying: "Those who desired the life of the world" (28:79). That is: they make it the sought-after goal in their efforts, having no goal beyond it. Thus, they are in ignorance of the Hereafter and the reward Allah has prepared for His servants therein. The Exalted said: "So avoid those who turn away from Our remembrance and desire nothing but the life of the world. That is the ultimate reach of their knowledge" (53:29-30). For this reason, they considered what Qarun was given of wealth to be a great happiness for him without any qualification or condition.

His saying, the Exalted: "But those who were given knowledge said, ‘Woe to you! Allah’s reward is better for him who has faith and acts righteously’" (28:80) etc. Woe (الويل) is destruction; it is used to pray for destruction and as a deterrent against what is not approved. In this context, it is a deterrent against wishing [for the like of Qarun].

Those who said this were the believers, the people of knowledge (أهل العلم) of Allah, addressing those ignorant ones who wished to be given the like of what Qarun was given and considered it an absolute great happiness. Their meaning was that Allah's reward is better for him who has faith and performs righteous deeds (عملاً صالحاً) than what Qarun was given; so if they were righteous believers, let them wish for that.

His saying: "and none shall be granted it except the patient" (28:80). Granting (التلقية) is making one understand, and receiving (التلقي) is understanding and taking. The pronoun—according to what they said—refers to the "word" (الكلمة) understood from the context. The meaning is: none understands this word—which is their saying: "Allah’s reward is better for him who has faith and acts righteously"—except the patient (الصابرون).

It was said: The pronoun refers to the "conduct" (السيرة) or "path" (الطريقة), and the meaning of being granted it is understanding it or being granted the success (التوفيق) to act upon it.

The patient (الصابرون) are those who clothe themselves with patience (الصبر) during hardships, in performing acts of obedience, and in refraining from acts of disobedience. The reason they are the ones who receive this word, conduct, or path is that the affirmation that the reward of the Hereafter is better than worldly fortune—which is inseparable from faith and righteous deeds that necessitate abandoning many desires and being deprived of many pleasures—cannot be realized except by one who possesses the quality of patience over the bitterness of opposing one's nature and disobeying the soul that commands to evil (النفس الأمارة).

His saying, the Exalted: "So We caused the earth to swallow him and his dwelling" (28:81) to the end of the verse. The two pronouns refer to Qarun, and the sentence is branched from his tyranny.

His saying: "and he had no host to help him against Allah, nor was he of those who could save themselves" (28:81). A host (الفئة) is a group that leans toward one another. In "help" (النصر) and "saving oneself" (الانتصار) there is the meaning of prevention and self-prevention. The gist of the meaning is: he had no group to prevent the punishment from him, nor was he of those who could prevent it themselves—contrary to what he used to imagine, that what brings him good and repels evil from him was his power and accumulation which he acquired through his knowledge. His accumulation did not protect him, nor did his power avail him against Allah, and it became clear that Allah, the Glorified, was the one who gave him what He gave him.

The "so" (الفاء) in His saying "and he had no" (فَما كانَ) is to branch the sentence from His saying "So We caused the earth to swallow him" etc. That is: it became manifest through Our causing the earth to swallow him and his dwelling that his claims of merit and independence from Allah, the Glorified, were false, and that his claim that what brings him good and repels evil is his power and accumulation acquired through his scientific brilliance was also false.

His saying, the Exalted: "By morning, those who had sighed for his place the day before were saying, ‘Ah! It is indeed Allah who expands the provision for whomever He wishes of His servants, and tightens it’" (28:82) etc. They mentioned that way (وي) is a word of regret, and it is sometimes used for wonder; both meanings are applicable to the context, though regret comes more readily to mind.

Their saying: "Ah! It is indeed Allah who expands the provision for whomever He wishes of His servants, and tightens it" is an admission from them of the falsehood of what Qarun used to claim—and they used to believe him—that power and accumulation in the world are due to a person's brilliance in his knowledge and the excellence of his management, not by grace from Allah, the Glorified; rather, the expansion and tightening of provision are by the will of Allah.

The context is one of realization (التحقيق) rather than comparison (التشبيه) which is suitable for doubt and hesitation. However, they only used "Ah, it is as if" (وَيْكَأَنَ) in their speech to indicate the beginning of their hesitation regarding Qarun's saying, which they had previously accepted and believed; this is a common rhetorical device in usage.

The proof for this is their saying afterward: "Had Allah not been gracious to us, He would have made it swallow us [too]" (28:82) in a manner of certainty and realization.

Their saying: "Ah! It is as if the faithless do not prosper" (28:82) is a second regret from them and a withdrawal from what was the necessary consequence of their wishing for Qarun's place.

His saying, the Exalted: "This is the abode of the Hereafter, which We shall grant to those who do not desire to be highhanded in the earth or to cause corruption, and the outcome will be in favor of the God-conscious" (28:83). This verse and the one following it are like a conclusion extracted from the story.

His saying: "This is the abode of the Hereafter" (تِلْكَ الدَّارُ الْآخِرَةُ). The reference to it with the word for the distant (تِلْكَ) is to indicate its nobility, splendor, and the loftiness of its status. This is the evidence that what is meant by it is the happy abode of the Hereafter; thus, they interpreted it as Paradise.

His saying: "which We shall grant to those who do not desire to be highhanded in the earth or to cause corruption" (28:83). That is: We reserve it for them. The desire for highhandedness (العلو) is seeking superiority and arrogance over the servants of Allah, and the desire for corruption (الفساد) therein is seeking acts of disobedience to Allah, the Exalted. For Allah built His laws—which are obligations for man—upon the requirements of his primordial nature (الفطرة) and creation. His primordial nature only requires what accords with the best system governing human life on earth. Thus, every act of disobedience leads to corruption in the earth, either directly or indirectly. The Exalted said: "Corruption has appeared on land and sea because of what the hands of the people have earned" (30:41).

From here it becomes clear that the desire for highhandedness is one of the instances of the desire for corruption; it was singled out and specifically mentioned out of concern for its gravity. The gist of the meaning is: that happy abode of the Hereafter is reserved for those who do not desire corruption in the earth through highhandedness over the servants of Allah, nor through any other act of disobedience.

The verse is general, specified by the saying of the Exalted: "If you avoid the major sins that you are forbidden, We will absolve you of your misdeeds and admit you to a noble entrance" (4:31).

His saying: "and the outcome will be in favor of the God-conscious" (28:83). That is: the praised and beautiful outcome, which is the happy abode of the Hereafter, or the happy outcome in this world and the Hereafter; however, the context of the two verses supports the former.

His saying, the Exalted: "Whoever brings a good deed shall receive [a reward] better than it" (28:84). That is: because it is multiplied for him by grace from Allah. The Exalted said: "Whoever brings a virtue shall receive ten times its like" (6:160).

His saying, the Exalted: "and whoever brings an evil deed—those who commit evil deeds shall not be requited except for what they used to do" (28:84). That is: they will not be given more than what they did, and in this is perfect justice, just as in the requital of a good deed with something better than it is perfect grace.

The apparent requirement in His saying "those who commit" (الَّذِينَ عَمِلُوا) etc., would have been a pronoun. Perhaps placing the relative clause in place of the pronoun is an indication that this requital is only for one who frequently commits acts of disobedience and whose sins have encompassed him, as suggested by the plural "evil deeds" (السَّيِّئاتِ) and the phrase "they used to do" (كانُوا يَعْمَلُونَ) which indicates persistence and continuity. As for one who brings both an evil deed and a good deed, it is hoped that Allah will forgive him, as He said: "And [there are] others who have confessed to their sins: they have mixed a righteous deed with another that was evil. Maybe Allah will accept their repentance. Indeed Allah is all-forgiving, all-merciful" (9:102).

Let it be known that the criterion for a good deed (الحسنة) and an evil deed (السيئة) is based on the effect produced by them in the person; by this, actions are called good or evil, and upon this—not upon the external substance of the action which is a type of movement—is a person rewarded or punished. The Exalted said: "Whether you disclose what is in your souls or hide it, Allah will bring you to account for it" (2:284).

By this, the answer becomes clear to the objection raised against the generality of the verse—that monotheism (التوحيد) is a good deed and it is inconceivable for anything to be better or more excellent than it; thus, the verse is either specific to things other than true beliefs or is specified by monotheism.

The answer is that the effect produced by monotheism can be assumed to have something better than it, even if monotheism does not accept that by way of conceptualization.

Furthermore, monotheism, however it is assumed, accepts intensity and weakness, increase and decrease. If it is multiplied at the time of requital as mentioned before, its multiplied form is better than its non-multiplied form.

(Narrative Discussion)

In al-Durr al-Manthur: Ibn Abi Shaybah in al-Musannaf, Ibn al-Mundhir, Ibn Abi Hatim, al-Hakim (who authenticated it), and Ibn Mardawayh narrated from Ibn Abbas: Qarun was from the people of Moses; he said: He was his cousin and he used to seek knowledge until he gathered knowledge—and he continued in that state until he tyrannized Moses and envied him.

Moses (peace be upon him) said to him: "Indeed Allah has commanded me to take the zakat (الزكاة)," but he refused. He said: "Moses wants to consume your wealth; he brought you the prayer (الصلاة) and brought you things which you bore, so will you bear that you give him your wealth?" They said: "We will not bear it, so what do you see?" He said to them: "I see that I should send for a prostitute from the prostitutes of the Children of Israel, and we will send her to him so she may accuse him of having desired her." So they sent for her and said to her: "We will give you whatever you judge, on condition that you testify against Moses that he committed immorality with you." She said: "Yes."

Qarun came to Moses (peace be upon him) and said: "Gather the Children of Israel and inform them of what your Lord has commanded you." He said: "Yes." So he gathered them and they said to him: "What has your Lord commanded you?" He said: "He commanded me that you worship Allah and not associate anything with Him, and that you maintain ties of kinship, and such and such—and He has commanded me regarding the adulterer, if he commits adultery and is married, that he be stoned." They said: "Even if it were you?" He said: "Yes." They said: "Then you have committed adultery." He said: "I?"

So they sent for the woman and she came. They said: "What do you testify against Moses?" Moses (peace be upon him) said to her: "I adjure you by Allah that you speak the truth." She said: "As for your adjuring me, they called me and set for me a reward on condition that I accuse you of myself—and I testify that you are innocent and that you are the Messenger of Allah."

Moses (peace be upon him) fell down prostrating and weeping. Then Allah revealed to him: "What makes you weep? We have given you authority over the earth, so command it and it will obey you." He raised his head and said: "Take them!" So it took them to their heels. They began saying: "O Moses! O Moses!" He said: "Take them!" So it took them to their necks. They began saying: "O Moses! O Moses!" He said: "Take them!" So it hid them. Then Allah revealed: "O Moses, My servants asked you and implored you, but you did not answer them. By My Might, had they called upon Me, I would have answered them."

Ibn Abbas said: That is His saying, the Exalted: "So We caused the earth to swallow him and his dwelling"—he was swallowed down to the lowest earth.

I say: It is also narrated therein from Abd al-Razzaq and Ibn Abi Hatim from Ibn Nawfal al-Hashimi the same story, but in it, the woman was brought to the assembly of Qarun to testify before the elite of the Children of Israel against Moses (peace be upon him) regarding immorality and to complain of him to Qarun. She came to him and confessed the truth before the elite. News of that reached Moses (peace be upon him), so he complained of him to his Lord, and Allah gave him authority over him.

Al-Qummi narrated in his Tafsir regarding the story that Moses (peace be upon him) came to Qarun and informed him of the ruling of zakat, but he mocked him and expelled him from his house. So he complained of him to his Lord, and Allah gave him authority over him, so He caused the earth to swallow him and his dwelling. The narration is mawquf (attributed to a companion/successor) and contains objectionable matters; for that reason, we have omitted its full citation, just as the two narrations of Ibn Abbas and Ibn Nawfal are also mawquf.

Furthermore, the narration of Ibn Abbas recounts his tyranny against Moses (peace be upon him), whereas what the verses recount is his tyranny against the Children of Israel. It also indicates that the knowledge he possessed was what he acquired through learning, while the apparent meaning of the verse, as mentioned, is that it was knowledge of the ways of acquiring wealth and the like.

The story is presented in the current Torah in another way. In the sixteenth chapter of the Book of Numbers: "Now Korah the son of Izhar, the son of Kohath, the son of Levi, with Dathan and Abiram the sons of Eliab, and On the son of Peleth, sons of Reuben, took men; and they rose up before Moses with some of the children of Israel, two hundred and fifty leaders of the congregation, representatives of the congregation, men of renown. They gathered together against Moses and Aaron, and said to them, ‘You take too much upon yourselves, for all the congregation is holy, every one of them, and the Lord is among them. Why then do you exalt yourselves above the assembly of the Lord?’

So when Moses heard it, he fell on his face; and he spoke to Korah and all his company, saying, ‘Tomorrow morning the Lord will show who is His and who is holy, and will cause him to come near to Him. That one whom He chooses He will cause to come near to Him. Do this: Take censers, Korah and all your company; put fire in them and put incense in them before the Lord tomorrow, and it shall be that the man whom the Lord chooses is the holy one. You take too much upon yourselves, you sons of Levi!’"

Then the story continues, mentioning their attendance the next day, their bringing of the censers with fire and incense, and their gathering at the door of the Tabernacle of Meeting. Then it is said: "the ground split apart under them, and the earth opened its mouth and swallowed them up, with their households and all the men with Korah, with all their goods. So they and all those with them went down alive into the pit; the earth closed over them, and they perished from from among the assembly. Then all Israel who were around them fled at their cry, for they said, ‘Lest the earth swallow us up also!’ And a fire came out from the Lord and consumed the two hundred and fifty men who were offering incense." [End of the relevant portion].

In al-Majma‘, regarding His saying, the Exalted: "Indeed Qarun was from the people of Moses"—and he was his maternal aunt's son: from ‘Ata’ from Ibn Abbas, and it is what is narrated from Abu ‘Abdillah (peace be upon him).

In the Tafsir of al-Qummi, regarding His saying, the Exalted: "that their keys would weigh down" (28:76) the verse, he said: The company possessed of strength used to carry the keys to his storehouses.

In al-Ma‘ani, with its chain of narration from Musa ibn Isma‘il ibn Musa ibn Ja‘far (peace be upon him), from his father, from his grandfather, from his forefathers, from Ali (peace be upon him): regarding the saying of Allah, the Mighty and Majestic: "and do not forget your share of this world" (28:77), he said: "Do not forget your health, your strength, your free time, your youth, and your vigor—that you seek the Hereafter through them."

In the Tafsir of al-Qummi, regarding His saying, the Exalted: "So he went out before his people in his finery" (28:79), he said: "In dyed garments that he dragged along the ground."

In al-Majma‘: Zadhan narrated from the Commander of the Faithful (peace be upon him) that he used to walk in the markets while he was the ruler, guiding the lost and helping the weak. He would pass by the seller and the grocer, opening the Quran for them and reciting: "This is the abode of the Hereafter, which We shall grant to those who do not desire to be highhanded in the earth or to cause corruption" (28:83), and he would say: "This verse was revealed concerning the people of justice and humility among the rulers and the people of power among the rest of the people."

Therein also: Sallam al-A‘raj narrated from the Commander of the Faithful (peace be upon him) who said: "A man may be pleased with the strap of his sandal, and thus enter into this verse: ‘This is the abode of the Hereafter...’"

I say: It is narrated from Sayyid Ibn Tawus in Sa‘d al-Su‘ud that he narrated it from al-Tabarsi thus: "Indeed, a man may be pleased that the strap of his sandal be better than the strap of his companion's sandal, and thus fall under it."

In al-Durr al-Manthur: al-Mahamili and al-Daylami narrated from Abu Hurayrah from the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) regarding the verse, he said: "It is tyranny in the earth and taking without right."

Ayahs

إِنَّ ٱلَّذِى فَرَضَ عَلَيْكَ ٱلْقُرْءَانَ لَرَآدُّكَ إِلَىٰ مَعَادٍ ۚ قُل رَّبِّىٓ أَعْلَمُ مَن جَآءَ بِٱلْهُدَىٰ وَمَنْ هُوَ فِى ضَلَـٰلٍ مُّبِينٍ (٨٥)
Indeed He who has revealed to you the Quran will surely restore you to the place of return. Say, ‘My Lord knows best him who brings guidance and him who is in manifest error.’ (85)
وَمَا كُنتَ تَرْجُوٓا۟ أَن يُلْقَىٰٓ إِلَيْكَ ٱلْكِتَـٰبُ إِلَّا رَحْمَةً مِّن رَّبِّكَ ۖ فَلَا تَكُونَنَّ ظَهِيرًا لِّلْكَـٰفِرِينَ (٨٦)
You did not expect that the Book would be delivered to you; but it was a mercy from your Lord. So do not be ever an advocate of the faithless. (86)
وَلَا يَصُدُّنَّكَ عَنْ ءَايَـٰتِ ٱللَّهِ بَعْدَ إِذْ أُنزِلَتْ إِلَيْكَ ۖ وَٱدْعُ إِلَىٰ رَبِّكَ ۖ وَلَا تَكُونَنَّ مِنَ ٱلْمُشْرِكِينَ (٨٧)
Do not ever let them bar you from Allah’s signs after they have been sent down to you. Invite to your Lord, and never be one of the polytheists. (87)
وَلَا تَدْعُ مَعَ ٱللَّهِ إِلَـٰهًا ءَاخَرَ ۘ لَآ إِلَـٰهَ إِلَّا هُوَ ۚ كُلُّ شَىْءٍ هَالِكٌ إِلَّا وَجْهَهُۥ ۚ لَهُ ٱلْحُكْمُ وَإِلَيْهِ تُرْجَعُونَ (٨٨)
And do not invoke another god besides Allah; there is no god except Him. Everything is to perish except His Face. All judgement belongs to Him, and to Him you will be brought back. (88)

(Commentary)

These verses conclude the Surah and contain a beautiful promise to the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) that Allah, the Glorified, will bestow His favor upon him by raising his status, making his word prevail, advancing his religion, and spreading security and peace upon him and the believers, just as He did for Moses and the Children of Israel. Indeed, the story of Moses and the Children of Israel was recounted in this Surah to illustrate this very point.

His saying, the Exalted: إِنَّ الَّذِي فَرَضَ عَلَيْكَ الْقُرْآنَ لَرادُّكَ إِلى مَعادٍ — "Indeed He who has revealed to you the Quran will surely restore you to the destination" (28:85), to the end of the verse. Obligation (الفرض), according to what has been mentioned, carries the meaning of making something mandatory (الإيجاب). Thus, the meaning of "He who has revealed (فرض) to you the Quran" is: He who has made it mandatory for you to act upon it—that is, upon the rulings contained within it. In this sense, there is a metaphorical attribution (مجاز في النسبة).

Better than this is the view of some who say the meaning is: He has made its recitation, its propagation, and acting upon it mandatory for you. This is more consistent with His saying, "will surely restore you to the destination (لَرادُّكَ إِلى مَعادٍ)," based on the meaning that will follow.

Regarding His saying: لَرادُّكَ إِلى مَعادٍ, the destination (المعاد) is a noun of place or time derived from "returning" (العود). Opinions have differed regarding the interpretation of this "destination." It has been said it refers to Mecca; thus, the verse is a promise to him that Allah will return him to Mecca a second time after his migration. It has also been said it refers to death, or the Resurrection (القيامة), or the Place of Gathering (المحشر), or the Praiseworthy Station (المقام المحمود)—which is the station of the Great Intercession (الشفاعة الكبرى). Others have said it refers to Paradise, or Jerusalem (بيت المقدس)—which would effectively be a promise of a second Ascension (معراج) in which he returns to Jerusalem after having entered it during the first Ascension. It has also been said it refers to "that which is beloved," which would then be applicable to most or all of the previous opinions.

What contemplation (التدبر) upon the context of the verses of the Surah yields is that the verse is an explicit statement of what the story narrated at the beginning of the Surah alluded to, and the subsequent verses support this.

For the Exalted recounted the story of the Children of Israel and Moses (عليه السلام) at the beginning of the Surah, detailing how He favored them with security, peace, honor, and empowerment after they had been humiliated and oppressed at the hands of the House of Pharaoh—who slaughtered their sons and spared their women. The story indicated by implication—and the opening of the Surah supports this—a beautiful promise to the believers that Allah, the Glorified, would save them from the trials, hardship, and adversity they were facing, make their religion prevail over all religions, and empower them in the land after they had neither a sky to shade them nor a land to carry them.

Then, after concluding the story, He mentioned that it is a requirement of wisdom (الحكمة) that He send down a Book to guide people to the truth as a reminder and a completion of the proof (إتمام للحجة), so that they might guard themselves against the punishment of Allah—just as He sent it down to Moses after destroying the former generations, and just as He sent it down to the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم), even if they denied it out of stubbornness toward the truth and preference for the world over the Hereafter.

This context gives the listener hope that the Exalted will explicitly address what He alluded to in the narration of the story. Thus, when one hears His saying: "Indeed He who has revealed to you the Quran will surely restore you to the destination," one immediately understands that this is the beautiful promise that was being anticipated, especially since it begins with: "Indeed He who has revealed to you the Quran," having already presented the Torah as a parallel to the Quran. What was narrated regarding the salvation of the Children of Israel was a prelude to the revelation of the Torah, so that by holding fast to it and acting upon it, they might become leaders and be the inheritors.

Thus, the meaning of the verse is: Indeed, He who made the Quran mandatory upon you—that you recite it to the people, propagate it, and that you all act upon it—will surely return you and bring you to a place such that your arrival there will be a return (عود), and it will be a destination (معاد) for you, just as He made the Torah mandatory upon Moses and thereby raised his status and the status of his people. It is well known that he (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) was in Mecca amidst its hardship and trials, then he migrated from it, then returned to it as a victorious conqueror. The foundations of his religion were established, the pillars of his creed (ملته) were strengthened, the idols were broken, and the structure of polytheism (الشرك) was demolished, while the believers became the inheritors of the land after they had been humiliated and tormented.

The use of the indefinite form in His saying "a destination (مَعادٍ)" alludes to the greatness of the status of this return, and that it cannot be compared to his previous residence there; history confirms this.

His saying: قُلْ رَبِّي أَعْلَمُ مَنْ جاءَ بِالْهُدى وَمَنْ هُوَ فِي ضَلالٍ مُبِينٍ — "Say, ‘My Lord knows best him who brings guidance and him who is in manifest error’" (28:85), supports the meaning we have presented. It parallels the saying of Moses (عليه السلام)—when they denied him and accused his clear signs of being fabricated magic: "My Lord knows best him who brings guidance from Him and him who shall have the ultimate abode." Thus, the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) was commanded to say to the "Pharaohs" among the polytheists of his people—when they denied him and accused him of magic—exactly what Moses said to the House of Pharaoh when they denied him and accused him of magic, due to the complete similarity between their missions and the courses of their calls. This becomes fully apparent by reflecting on His saying, the Exalted: إِنَّا أَرْسَلْنا إِلَيْكُمْ رَسُولاً شاهِداً عَلَيْكُمْ كَما أَرْسَلْنا إِلى فِرْعَوْنَ رَسُولاً — "Indeed We have sent to you an apostle to be a witness to you, just as We sent an apostle to Pharaoh" (73:15).

Perhaps the restriction to the first part of Moses' (عليه السلام) statement and the silence regarding the second part—namely his saying: "and him who shall have the ultimate abode"—is because the discourse, according to its context, is built upon not exceeding the limit of allusion and hint, as can also be sensed from the context of His saying: "will surely restore you to the destination," where the address was specific to the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) and "destination" was left indefinite.

In any case, what is meant by His saying: "him who brings guidance (مَنْ جاءَ بِالْهُدى)" is the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) himself, and by His saying: "him who is in manifest error (وَمَنْ هُوَ فِي ضَلالٍ مُبِينٍ)" the polytheists of his people. The difference in the phrasing of the two clauses—where it is said regarding him (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم): "him who brings guidance," and regarding them: "him who is in manifest error," contrasting their error with his bringing of guidance rather than his being guided—is because their denial was naturally directed toward what he brought, not toward his person.

Regarding His saying: أَعْلَمُ مَنْ جاءَ بِالْهُدى, they have mentioned that "who (مَنْ)" is in the accusative case (منصوب) due to a hidden verb indicated by "knows best (أَعْلَمُ)," the estimation being: "He knows who brought it," based on the famous rule that the superlative (أفعل التفضيل) does not take a direct object. Some mentioned it is in the accusative due to "knows best" acting with the meaning of "knowing" (عالم), but there is no evidence for this. I do not recall anyone saying it is in the accusative due to the removal of the preposition (نزع الخافض)—even if the accusative mark does not appear due to its indeclinable nature—the estimation being: "My Lord is most knowing of him who brings guidance," and there is no evidence to prevent this.

His saying, the Exalted: وَما كُنْتَ تَرْجُوا أَنْ يُلْقى إِلَيْكَ الْكِتابُ إِلَّا رَحْمَةً مِنْ رَبِّكَ فَلا تَكُونَنَّ ظَهِيراً لِلْكافِرِينَ — "You did not expect that the Book would be delivered to you; but it was a mercy from your Lord. So do not ever be a supporter of the faithless" (28:86). The beginning of the verse is a confirmation of the promise in His saying: "Indeed He who has revealed to you the Quran will surely restore you to the destination"—meaning: He will surely restore you to a destination—which you did not expect—just as the Book was delivered to you—which you did not expect.

It has also been said: it is a new introductory reminder of the favor of the Exalted upon him (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم). This is a plausible view, the explanation being: when the Exalted promised him the return to a destination—which involves the elevation of his mention, the advancement of his call, and the spread of his religion—He outlined for him the path he must follow with effort and vigilance. He explained to him that the delivery of the Book to him was not according to the course of ordinary events that are usually expected or anticipated; rather, it was a special mercy (رحمة) from his Lord. Since He promised him what He promised in revealing it to him, it is mandatory upon him, in exchange for this favor and for the advancement of his call to the goal He promised, that he does not support the disbelievers, nor obey them, but rather calls to his Lord, and is not among the polytheists, nor calls upon another god along with Him.

His saying: إِلَّا رَحْمَةً مِنْ رَبِّكَ is a disconnected exception (استثناء منقطع)—meaning: "but He delivered it to you as a mercy from your Lord," and it was not an ordinary delivery that one would expect the like of.

His saying: فَلا تَكُونَنَّ ظَهِيراً لِلْكافِرِينَ is a derivation from His saying: "but it was a mercy from your Lord"—meaning: since its delivery to you was a mercy from your Lord with which He favored you, and it was beyond your expectation, then disassociate yourself from the disbelievers and do not be a helper or supporter to them.

It is quite possible that there is in this sentence a type of parallel to the saying of Moses (عليه السلام)—when he killed the Copt: "My Lord! As You have blessed me, I will never be a supporter of the guilty." Accordingly, the prohibition against helping them contains an allusion that the delivery of the Book to him (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) is a favor Allah bestowed upon him, by which He guides to the truth and calls to monotheism (التوحيد); thus, he must not help the disbelievers in their disbelief, nor incline toward their turning him away from the signs of Allah after they have been sent down to him, just as Moses (عليه السلام) covenanted with his Lord—by the judgment and knowledge He bestowed upon him—that he would never be a supporter of the guilty. It will follow that His saying: "And do not let them bar you..." etc., is like an explanation for this sentence.

His saying, the Exalted: وَلا يَصُدُّنَّكَ عَنْ آياتِ اللهِ بَعْدَ إِذْ أُنْزِلَتْ إِلَيْكَ — "And do not let them bar you from the signs of Allah after they have been sent down to you" (28:87), to the end of the verse. This is a prohibition to him (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) against turning away from the signs of Allah, expressed by prohibiting the disbelievers from barring and diverting him. The reason for this is that his turning away would be caused by their barring him. It is like His saying to Adam and his wife: "so let him not expel you from paradise"—meaning: do not leave it by his expelling you through whispering.

It is apparent that this verse and what follows it are in the position of explaining His saying: "So do not ever be a supporter of the faithless." Its benefit is to emphasize the prohibition across its various instances one by one. He first prohibited him from turning away from the Quran revealed to him due to their labeling the Book of Allah as magic, poetry, soothsaying, or fables of the ancients which he had written down. Secondly, He commanded him to call to his Lord. Thirdly, He prohibited him from being among the polytheists (المشركين), and explained this as calling upon another god along with Allah.

The attribute of "Lord" (الرب) has been repeated while being attributed to him (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) to indicate his exclusivity in receiving mercy and favor, and that he (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) is unique in his worship, with the polytheists having no share in it.

His saying, the Exalted: وَلا تَدْعُ مَعَ اللهِ إِلهاً آخَرَ — "And do not invoke another god besides Allah" (28:88). It has already been mentioned that this is like an explanation for His saying: "And do not be among the polytheists."

His saying, the Exalted: لا إِلهَ إِلَّا هُوَ كُلُّ شَيْءٍ هالِكٌ إِلَّا وَجْهَهُ لَهُ الْحُكْمُ وَإِلَيْهِ تُرْجَعُونَ — "There is no god but He. Everything is to perish except His Face. All sovereignty belongs to Him, and to Him you will be brought back" (28:88). The word of sincerity (كلمة الإخلاص) — the declaration of "There is no god but He" — is in the position of providing the reason (التعليل) for His preceding saying: "And do not invoke another god besides Allah"—meaning: because there is no god other than Him. What follows it is in the position of providing the reason for the declaration itself, as will become clear.

Regarding His saying: كُلُّ شَيْءٍ هالِكٌ إِلَّا وَجْهَهُ, "thing" (الشيء) is equivalent to "existent" (الموجود) and is applied to every existing matter, even to the Exalted Himself, as indicated by His saying: "Say, ‘What thing is greatest as a witness?’ Say, ‘Allah’" (6:19). Perishing (الهلاك) is nullity (البطلان) and non-existence (الانعدام).

"Face" (الوجه) and "direction" (الجهة) are the same, like "promise" (الوعد) and "appointment" (العدة). The "face" of a thing in general usage is that by which it meets others and by which others relate to it, just as the face of a body is its outer surface, and the face of a human is the front half of his head. His "Face," the Exalted, is that by which He meets His creation and by which His creation turns toward Him; this refers to His noble attributes (صفاته الكريمة) such as Life, Knowledge, Power, Hearing, and Sight, and what stems from them of the attributes of action (صفات الفعل) such as creation, providing sustenance, giving life, causing death, forgiveness, and mercy. Likewise, it refers to His signs (آياته) that point to Him insofar as they are His signs.

Thus, everything is perishing in itself, null in its essence, having no reality except what it possesses from Him of what Allah has poured upon it. As for that which is not attributed to the Exalted, it is nothing but what the imagination of the imaginer has fabricated, or a mirage depicted by fancy. This is like the idols: they have no reality except that they are stones, wood, or some metal; as for their being lords, gods, beneficial, or harmful, these are nothing but names their worshippers have named. It is also like a human: he has no reality except the spirit and body that creation has deposited in him and the perfections he has acquired—all of which are attributed to Allah, the Glorified. As for what the social intellect attributes to him of power, authority, leadership, prestige, wealth, honor, children, and supporters, it is nothing but a perishing mirage and a false wish. All other existents follow this path.

Therefore, they possess no reality except what Allah has poured upon them by His grace, and they are His signs pointing to His noble attributes of mercy, sustenance, grace, bounty, and so on.

Thus, the fixed reality in actuality, which is not perishing or null among things, is His noble attributes and His signs pointing to them, all of which are fixed by the fixity of the Sacred Essence (الذات المقدسة).

This is on the assumption that "perishing" (الهالك) in the verse means perishing in actuality (بالفعل). Accordingly, the summary of the reasoning for the word of sincerity by His saying: "Everything is to perish except His Face" is that the God (الإله)—who is the one worshipped in truth—can only be a worshipped God if He is a matter possessing an actual reality, neither perishing nor null, having governance (تدبير) in the world with this description. Everything other than the Exalted is perishing and null in itself except what is a "Face" for Him, attributed to Him; thus, there is no god in existence other than Him, the Glorified.

The pagans, although they see the existence of their gods as attributed to the Exalted and from His side, they make them independent in governance, severed in that regard from Him, without their command being His command. For this reason, they worship them instead of Allah. Yet, nothing has independence in anything apart from Allah, the Glorified; therefore, none deserves worship but He.

There is another view here, more subtle than this, based on the idea that "Face" means the essence (ذات) of the thing. Some have mentioned this as one of the meanings of "Face," as one says "the face of the day" or "the face of the road" for the things themselves, though this can be contested. Others mentioned that what is meant by it is the Noble Essence (الذات الشريفة), as one says "the faces of the people," meaning their nobles; this would be a trope (مجاز مرسل) or a metaphor (استعارة). On both assumptions, the meaning is that existents other than the Exalted are contingent (ممكنة), and the contingent—even if it exists by the Exalted's bringing it into existence—is non-existent when considering the limit of its own essence, perishing in itself. That which has no path to nullity or perishing is His Essence, which is Necessary by Itself (الواجبة بذاتها).

The summary of the reasoning according to this meaning is: the God worshipped in truth must be an Essence in whose hand is something of the governance of the world. Cosmic governance (التدبير الكوني) is inseparable from creation and bringing into existence (الإيجاد); thus, it makes no sense for one thing to bring events into existence while another thing governs their affairs—as we have clarified repeatedly in this book. The Creator and Bringer-into-existence can only be the Necessary Existent (واجب الوجود), and there is no Necessary Existent but He, the Exalted; thus, there is no god but He.

As for their saying: "He, the Exalted, is too sublime for any intellect or imagination to encompass Him, so it is impossible to direct worship toward Him; therefore, one must direct worship toward some of those close to His presence, such as the noble angels and others, so they may be intercessors with Him"—this is refuted by denying that directing worship depends on encompassing knowledge (العلم الإحاطي); rather, knowledge of a "Face" (المعرفة بوجه) is sufficient, and that is attained by necessity.

As for the assumption that "perishing" (الهالك) means that which will face perishing and annihilation (الفناء) in the future—based on what has been said that the active participle (اسم الفاعل) is apparent in the future tense—the outward meaning of the verse is that everything will face perishing after its existence except His Face. Yes, the facing of perishing differs according to the difference in things: its facing in temporal things (الزمانيات) is the end of the term of their existence and their nullity thereafter, while in others, it is the fact that their existence is surrounded by non-existence from every side.

The perishing of things according to this is the nullity of their initial existence and the first realm (النشأة الأولى) becoming empty of them by their transition to the other realm, their return to Allah, and their settling with Him. As for absolute nullity after existence, the explicit text of the Book of Allah denies it; the verses are consecutive in stating that everything returns to Allah, that He is the ultimate end, to Him is the return, and He is the one who originates creation then repeats it.

Thus, the summary of the verse's meaning—if "Face" is intended to mean His noble attributes—is that everything will vacate its place and return to Him except His noble attributes, which are the principles of His effusion (فيضه); they effuse and then effuse without end. The God must be such that there is no nullity for His Essence nor any cessation for His effusive attributes, and nothing other than the Exalted possesses this description; thus, there is no god but He.

If by His "Face" the Sacred Essence is intended, the summary is that everything will face perishing and annihilation by returning to Allah, the Glorified, except His True, Fixed Essence, to which there is no path for nullity—and the attributes, on this view, are considered part of the realm of the Essence (صقع الذات). The God must be such that annihilation cannot reach Him, and nothing other than Him possesses this description; thus, there is no god but He.

By the preceding explanation, the objection against the generality of the verse—using examples like Paradise, Hell, and the Throne (العرش)—is refuted. For Paradise and Hell do not cease to exist after coming into being and remain without end; the Throne is likewise, based on what has been narrated in some reports that the ceiling of Paradise is the Throne.

The way this is refuted is that what is meant by "perishing" is the changing of the realm of existence and the return to Allah, expressed as the transition from the world to the Hereafter and being clothed with the "Return" (العود) after the "Beginning" (البدء). This only occurs in that which exists with an initial, worldly existence. As for the Abode of the Hereafter and what exists with an after-worldly existence, such as Paradise and Hell, nothing of this sort is described as "perishing" in this sense.

The Exalted said: ما عِنْدَكُمْ يَنْفَدُ وَما عِنْدَ اللهِ باقٍ — "That which is with you will be exhausted but what is with Allah is lasting" (16:96). He also said: وَما عِنْدَ اللهِ خَيْرٌ لِلْأَبْرارِ — "and what is with Allah is better for the pious" (3:198). And He said: سَيُصِيبُ الَّذِينَ أَجْرَمُوا صَغارٌ عِنْدَ اللهِ وَعَذابٌ شَدِيدٌ — "Soon the guilty will be overtaken by debasement from Allah and a severe punishment" (6:124). Similar to these are the treasuries of mercy, as He said: وَإِنْ مِنْ شَيْءٍ إِلَّا عِنْدَنا خَزائِنُهُ — "There is not a thing but its treasuries are with Us" (15:21), and likewise the Preserved Tablet (اللوح المحفوظ), as He said: وَعِنْدَنا كِتابٌ حَفِيظٌ — "and with Us is a preserving Book" (50:4).

As for what they mentioned regarding the Throne, the discussion concerning it has already passed in the commentary on His saying, the Exalted: "Indeed your Lord is Allah..." (7:54).

It is possible that "Face" refers to His "side" (جهته) which is attributed to Him, meaning the direction from which He is intended and toward which one turns. This is supported by the frequent use of "Face" in His speech, the Exalted, with this meaning, such as His saying: يُرِيدُونَ وَجْهَهُ — "desiring His Face" (6:52), and His saying: إِلَّا ابْتِغاءَ وَجْهِ رَبِّهِ الْأَعْلى — "only seeking the Face of his Lord, the Most Exalted" (92:20), among many other verses.

Accordingly, it would be an expression for everything that is attributed to Him alone. If the speech is taken on its apparent generality, it applies to the first meaning we presented, and its instances would include His names, His attributes, His prophets, His vicegerents (خلفاؤه), and His religion through which He is approached.

If the "Face" is restricted to the religion alone—as has occurred in some narrations, if it is not by way of application (تطبيق)—then "perishing" would mean corruption and lack of effect. The sentence would then be a reasoning for His saying: "And do not invoke another god besides Allah," and what precedes it would be a context indicating that by "thing" (الشيء), the religion and the deeds related to it are intended. The summary of the meaning would be: Do not follow any religion other than the religion of monotheism, because every religion is null and has no effect except His religion.

It is most appropriate on this basis that "sovereignty" (الحكم) at the end of the verse be understood as legislative sovereignty (الحكم التشريعي) or something broader than it and ontological sovereignty (التكويني). The meaning would be: Every religion is perishing except His religion, because the legislation of religion belongs to Him, and to Him you will return, not to the legislators of other religions.

This is what contemplation upon the noble verse yields; the commentators have other differing opinions regarding it.

It has been said: "Face" refers to His Sacred Essence, and "perishing" to non-existence. The meaning is: Everything in itself is subject to non-existence because its existence is from another, except His Essence, which is the Necessary Existent. The speech on this basis is built upon a comparison—that is, everything other than Him is as if perishing because its existence is dependent on another.

It has been said: "Face" means essence, and what is meant is the essence of the thing itself, with the pronoun referring to Allah in the sense that the face of the thing is owned by Him. The meaning is: Everything is perishing except the Face of Allah, which is the essence and existence of that thing.

It has been said: "Face" means the intended direction, with the pronoun referring to Allah. The meaning is: Everything is perishing in all that relates to it except the direction attributed to Him, the Exalted, which is the existence that Allah, the Exalted, poured upon it.

It has been said: "Face" is the intended direction, meaning Allah, the Glorified, toward whom everything turns, with the pronoun referring to the "thing." The meaning is: Everything is perishing except Allah, who is the direction sought by it.

It has been said: "perishing" refers to the perishing of death, and the generality is specific to those possessing life. The meaning is: Everything possessing life shall die except His Face.

It has been said: "Face" refers to righteous deeds. The meaning is that the deed was in the realm of non-existence, but when the servant performed it in obedience to His command, Allah kept it without nullifying it (إحباط) so that He may reward him, or that by acceptance it became incapable of perishing because the reward stands in its place and it is lasting.

It has been said: "Face" refers to His prestige (جاهه) which He established among the people.

It has been said: "perishing" is general to all besides Him, the Exalted, perpetually, because the existence poured upon them is renewed at every moment; thus, they are changing and perishing perpetually in this world and the Hereafter. The meaning is: Everything is of a perpetually changing essence except His Face.

These views range between those that do not fit the context of the verse, those whose argument does not succeed, and those that are far-fetched. By reflecting on what we have presented, the flaws in each of them become apparent, so we shall not prolong the discussion.

His saying: لَهُ الْحُكْمُ وَإِلَيْهِ تُرْجَعُونَ, "sovereignty" (الحكم) is His decree (قضاؤه) that is effective in all things, and upon it the governance in the system of the universe revolves. As for it being in the sense of the final judgment on the Day of Resurrection, this is made unlikely by the mention of "sovereignty" before "returning to Him," which is the Day of Resurrection, for the final judgment is derived from it.

Both sentences are intended for reasoning, and each one alone is a complete proof of His uniqueness in divinity, suitable for providing the reason for the word of sincerity. It has already been mentioned that "sovereignty" can be taken, according to some views, in the sense of legislative sovereignty.

(Narrative Discussion)

In al-Durr al-Manthur, it is narrated by Ibn Abi Shaybah, Abd ibn Humayd, al-Bukhari, al-Nasa'i, Ibn Jarir, Ibn al-Mundhir, Ibn Abi Hatim, Ibn Mardawayh, and al-Bayhaqi in al-Dala'il through various chains from Ibn Abbas regarding His saying, the Exalted: لَرادُّكَ إِلى مَعادٍ — he said: "To Mecca." Ibn Mardawayh added: "Just as He expelled you from it."

I say: It has also been narrated from him and from Abu Sa'id al-Khudri that what is meant by it is death. Also from Ali, from the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم): that what is meant by it is Paradise. The applicability of these two to the verse is not without obscurity.

Al-Qummi narrated in his Tafsir from Hariz, from Abu Ja'far (عليه السلام), and from Abu Khalid al-Kabuli, from Ali ibn al-Husayn (عليه السلام): that what is meant by it is the Return (الرجعة) — the restoration of certain people to life before the Day of Resurrection. Perhaps this is from the inner meaning (البطن) rather than the literal interpretation (التفسير).

In al-Ihtijaj, from the Commander of the Faithful (عليه السلام) in a long hadith: "As for His saying: كُلُّ شَيْءٍ هالِكٌ إِلَّا وَجْهَهُ, what is meant is: everything is perishing except His religion. For it is impossible that everything of Him should perish and the face remain. He is more sublime and greater than that. Rather, that which is not from Him perishes—do you not see that He said: كُلُّ مَنْ عَلَيْها فانٍ وَيَبْقى وَجْهُ رَبِّكَ (Everyone on it is ephemeral, yet there lasts the Face of your Lord)? Thus, He distinguished between His creation and His Face."

In al-Kafi, with its chain to Sayf, from one who mentioned it, from al-Harith ibn al-Mughirah al-Nasri, who said: "Abu Abdillah (عليه السلام) was asked about the saying of Allah, Blessed and Exalted: كُلُّ شَيْءٍ هالِكٌ إِلَّا وَجْهَهُ. He said: 'What do they say about it?' I said: 'They say: everything perishes except the Face of Allah.' He said: 'Glory be to Allah! They have said something momentous. He only meant by it the Face of Allah through which He is approached (يؤتى منه).'"

I say: A similar report was narrated in al-Tawhid with its chain to al-Harith ibn al-Mughirah al-Nasri from him (عليه السلام), and its wording is: "I asked Abu Abdillah (عليه السلام) about the saying of Allah, Mighty and Majestic: كُلُّ شَيْءٍ هالِكٌ إِلَّا وَجْهَهُ. He said: 'Everything is perishing except he who takes the path of truth.'"

In Mahasin al-Barqi, there is a similar report, except its end is: "he who takes the path that you all are upon."

The confusion that appears in the narrations has entered them by way of transmission of meaning (النقل بالمعنى). If what is meant by the "Face through which He is approached" is everything attributed to Him and from His realm and side, it would be consistent with the first meaning we presented for the verse.

If "Face" is in the sense of the religion toward which one turns by intending Him, the Exalted, then "perishing" would mean nullity and lack of effect. The meaning would be: There is no god but He; every religion is null except His true religion through which He is approached, for it will benefit and be rewarded. Both views were alluded to in the commentary on the verse.

In the Tafsir of al-Qummi, regarding His saying, the Exalted: فَلا تَكُونَنَّ ظَهِيراً لِلْكافِرِينَ, he said: "The address is to the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) but the meaning is for the people." And regarding His saying: وَلا تَدْعُ مَعَ اللهِ إِلهاً آخَرَ, he said: "The address is to the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) but the meaning is for the people." This is the saying of al-Sadiq (عليه السلام): "Indeed, Allah sent His Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) with the principle: 'I mean you, but listen, O neighbor!'" — an Arabic idiom meaning the address is directed to one person but intended for others.
